These tenets of the Litany have theoretically remained the same since their first revelation, but some believe that this codification is merely oral tradition and that the Litany’s tenets have evolved as the Garou Nation matured. The tenets themselves are considered inviolate, even though the interpretations regarding how and when to enforce them have changed throughout history. Many werewolves perceive a hypocritical gap between what Garou Elders preach and what werewolves actually do. Masters of Garou law can cite dozens of examples of precedent, but as fewer Cubs learn to chant the details, more argue ways to bend the rules in their favor. 

## Tenets of the Litany 

## Garou Shall Not Mate With Garou 

This precept is likely one of the most controversial tenets of the Litany in the modern age. The conflict centers on the definition of the word “mate,” and the interpretation of this tenet has caused fierce arguments. In past Ages, this tenet has been enforced to varying degrees of severity, depending on both the culprits as well as their accuser. Some tribes merely punished the parents, using methods as various as punishment rites, exile, or even execution, while raising the resultant child as well as possible, despite its Metis nature. Others have used this precept of the Litany to slaughter all involved, using this punishment as a warning and reminder of the law to any others who may even think about transgressing it. Such severity is not the 

norm, as Metis still appear to this day, and some are able to keep their secret transgression hidden from those who would condemn them for it. 

In modern days, the Council of the Tribes has determined that the tenet’s true purpose is to caution against making Metis Garou, not merely the act of intercourse. This precept is also no longer considered violated when someone is the victim of rape; only those who inflict such a crime bear the burden of punishment for violating this tenant of the Litany. 

Given their infertile nature, as well as the fact that bearing such a child likely results in the death of its mother, Garou consider Metis births a detriment to the continuation of the Garou race—the very thing this tenet intends to avoid. While its title remains the same, the tenet’s common interpretation is now to take no action that will lead to the birth of a Metis child. 

Adding controversy to the interpretation of this tenet is how the newly discovered Bitten Garou are to be classified. Bitten inherit the tribe of the Garou who created them, resulting in some werewolves arguing that Bitten are considered “children.” These proponents consider the Bite merely another way of breeding, and argue that since a Bitten werewolf is sterile and possibly suffering from mental trauma, she is similar to a Metis—thus to be avoided. 

Love and lust are both passionate emotions, and werewolves are nothing if not passionate. Some are able to overcome this impulse though, transitioning their love for another Garou into a strong and deep friendship—one forging a bond between them that never violates this tenet of the Litany. For others, love will not be denied, and they bear the risk that being together may cause a pregnancy and birth of a Metis child. Those who find themselves in such straits often seek out the help or assistance of the Glass Walkers or Children of Gaia, both tribes well known for their willingness to assist such parents, keeping their indiscretions secret. 

## Combat the Wyrm Wherever It Dwells and Whenever It Breeds 

This tenet guides Garou society as a whole, more than any other precept of the Litany. The continuous war against the Wyrm is a way of life for all werewolves. Historically this tenet was thought to encourage Garou to not only fight minions of the Wyrm that attacked them, but also to aggressively seek out and eradicate any who would serve the Wyrm, as well as to destroy or cleanse any corrupted place that may encourage others to walk the spiral path of the Wyrm. 

The World of darkness 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

To a lesser extent, some werewolves consider those who have “heard the One Song” and welcome it, thus gaining an affinity to the Weaver, another threat to be destroyed. During the Age of Apocalypse, war has made for strange bedfellows, and the staggering amount of caerns destroyed has led Garou to seek alliances once thought reprehensible. Now some Garou consider it a priority to seek allies wherever they can, and even creatures like vampires, which were once killed on sight, are tolerated when bigger threats loom. 

The Council of Tribes has ruled, through the narrowest of voting margins, that only those who have taken the Wyrm into their hearts and gained an affinity to its ways are to be required targets, according to this tenet of the Litany. Dealing with other creatures tainted by the Wyrm can wait until the war and time allows, they determined. They also decreed, at the forceful urging of the Concordat of Stars, that those with affinity to the Weaver are not threats, and should not be considered targets of this particular precept. While many members of the Sanctum of Gaia pushed back against this idea, ultimately the leaders of the Garou Nation determined that the Weaver could be dealt with at a later time. The tenet remains the same, but those allies that might be turned to the good of Gaia are now exempt from its influence. 

One example of this tenet’s new interpretation is based on the events that sparked its change in the first place. A great caern in Russia was under siege by a massive horde, and an alliance with a powerful vampire was the only thing that kept that caern from violation and destruction. Those present determined that the vampires present, while undead and a blight on Gaia, could not truly be active servants of the Wyrm while working to preserve a caern. Although the werewolves did not welcome their putrescent nature, the Garou tolerated it long enough to save the caern, pitting those vampires against true minions of the Wyrm in battle. 

Those who have studied the ways of vampires have shared that not all undead are the same—one subset is tainted beyond the rest. This ravenous horde of vampires, known for forsaking their humanity altogether and giving into the bestial nature of their kind, have embraced the teachings of the Wyrm, giving up what made them even partially human. The Elders have determined that these vampires deserve nothing but the searing pain of tooth and claw to rend the flesh from their undead bones. From dust were they born, and to dust they must return. Enemies such as these, who share the affinity of the Wyrm, are the true threat that must be hunted down and eradiated. 

## Respect the Territory of Another 

Territorial claims are inherently important to werewolves. Territory is a reflection of not only ownership, but also the 

Garou’s holy purpose of defending Gaia. The importance of that mission is underscored by the very fact that this precept exists within the Litany. This tenet also showcases the importance the Garou place in form and ritual. The elaborate traditions they use for greeting, hospitality, and departure are almost ritualistic in nature, helping contain the everpresent rage within and keeping any meeting of Garou from descending into a maelstrom of violence. Historically this precept of the Litany helped invent behaviors that many human cultures later adopted as guest rites, such as the sharing of food, crying out in greeting before approaching a stranger’s fire, and bringing no violence upon the host. 

This tenet has seen little to no new interpretation in recent times, at least by the Council of Tribes. While different areas, septs, and packs have different ideas about what “respect” means and how to show that respect, most werewolves can continue to agree that the burden lies on the guest to ensure she understands the laws of any territory she enters. In fact, the act of seeking out the owners of an entered territory is usually a sign of respect for that territory in and of itself. 

Truly respecting the territory of another when travelling relies on understanding. For example, when travelling into lands claimed by Red Talons, if a guest approached in Homid form, ignoring the scent markings and warning signs displayed grossly violating this tenet, it is her own fault when consequences descend on her without regard to mercy or excuses. On the other hand, standing at a border and howling in Lupus form, alerting residents to the presence of a traveler and requesting permission to enter ensures that the response received involves far less bloodshed—usually. While it is not always possible to be aware of every territorial claim, it is good to know the common traditions, so one can invoke them as necessary. 

## Accept an Honorable Surrender 

This is another tenet of the Litany that finds its base in the ritual behavior required to leash the rage within every Garou. In order to give outlet to these urges, many issues are solved through personal combat. When each and every warrior is sorely needed, especially after the death of so many, it is important to ensure that lives are not lost in conflicts taken too far. By codifying this precept as sacred within the Litany, it reminds Garou, even those caught in the depths of rage, that showing throat to one who has defeated you is an honorable action itself. 

This tenet requires Garou to accept an honorable surrender. No mercy is shown to those who seek to use this precept to trick or trap the unwary. Whether such a surrender takes the form of a bearing of throat, laying down weapons, or 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

even a verbal call for surrender, when such an action is done, any failure to acknowledge it is a breach of this tenet of the Litany. 

Garou are rage-filled creatures, and among them there is no tenet of the Litany that prohibits death or murder of another Garou. This precept helps prevent conflict and combat between Garou from ending the life of another and depriving Gaia of another warrior and protector. When disagreements can only be settled through strength of arms, an honorable werewolf accepts her due when an opponent gives her surrender, acknowledging her as the victor in their contest and ceasing the battle then and there. Failing to do so and continuing the contest is a violation of this tenet, and consequences can range anywhere from punishment to death. However, some seek dishonor, using this tenet of the Litany to cause another Garou to drop her guard and then attacking from surprise. Such cowards are shown no mercy from their opponents or those who watch such a dishonorable action occur. 

## Submission to Those of Higher Station 

Order and structure are important in Garou society. By establishing a hierarchy, leadership can more easily contain their lessers’ violent impulses. Those who stand higher within the social structure often make requests or give orders to those below them, expecting them to be obeyed. While those who have truly earned the respect of their station find their commands more swiftly acted upon, all who possess greater Rank and Renown can reasonably expect that their orders will be followed. 

Realistically, those who earn the respect of those they lead are far more likely to have little trouble with this tenet of the Litany. Those who abuse its power and make frivolous or self-serving demands, are likely to find themselves with little to no station to make such demands in the future. 

This tenet of the Litany has seen recent controversy among the Council of Tribes. Some werewolves argue that while respect is due, it should be earned and that having a law that requires obedience is comparable to slavery, instead of conveying respect and upholding hierarchy. Others fervently disagree, stating that such distinctions keep the rage within all Garou in check, giving their society an understandable hierarchy that suits both human and wolf natures. For now, no change has been made to the interpretations of this tenet of the Litany, though the voices of the young clamor for change. 

For example, when a Master of the Rite demands that rituals be handled at a sept in a certain way, those members of the sept must obey. The Master of the Rite has gained 

the position to oversee such activities in order to best respect the spirits and the traditions. When acting within her sphere of influence, a Master of the Rite’s position is considered highest in station. Failure to adhere to this standard is a violation of this tenet of the Litany. However, if an Elder demands that every Cliath and Fostern of the sept show up on Saturday to wash her car, those who fail to do so would still find themselves violating this tenet, but many other sept members, if not the Cliath and Fostern themselves, would likely stand in line to challenge such a foolish Elder for making frivolous demands and demeaning the traditions of the Litany. 

## Respect Those of Lower Station, for All Are of Gaia 

Gaia created all creatures that walk, crawl, swim, and fly. They deserve respect for the roles they play within the natural order. As Gaia’s protectors and champions, the Garou are expected to understand this fact intrinsically and uphold those values with every creature they encounter. Remembering this core precept, that all are of Gaia, reminds the werewolves that understanding and compassion are just as vital to their war as the strength of tooth and claw. 

This tenet of the Litany is seen as the foil to Submission to Those of Higher Station. It ensures that orders given are respectful to those who receive them, and it reminds the Garou and Fera alike that although they are blessed by Gaia to serve as her champions, all creatures are her creations and deserve respect. The Council of Tribes has not made changes to this tenet or its interpretation, relying on this precept to balance out the previous. 

Garou higher in station observe this tenet by ensuring the orders they give are necessary and respectful to the ones entrusted to carry them out. For example, an Athro ordering a Fostern to clean and prepare the challenge mound in the event it is required is far different from ordering the same Fostern to clean his house because he doesn’t want to. This tenet can and is abused. Some Garou of lower rank attempt to use this precept offensively to avoid punishment or orders by claiming the order is disrespectful. Those who attempt to do so are often forcefully reminded of other tenets of the Litany, such as submission to those higher in station. This does not mean that those of higher rank always avoid the temptation to abuse their privilege; those of lesser station can and do raise legitimate grievances. In such cases, punishments are usually harsh, as those of such lofty station are expected to know better, and, most importantly, know the difference between authority and tyranny. 

The World of darkness 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## The First Share of the Kill for Greatest in Station 

Originally this tenet of the Litany reinforced the natural order, where the leader eats first of a kill, out of respect for her role in helping the group attain it. Werewolves expanded this concept, allowing leaders to claim trophies or spoils after battle. This tenet was added to the Litany to show respect for those who put the worth of the community before their own wants and needs. Greedy Garou who seek to abuse this right, claiming more than their fair share through the power of this tenet, find themselves often challenged by packmates and septmates, reminding them that strength comes in the bonds formed by pack and sept, not in the abuse of power. 

The Council of Tribes has not issued new interpretation of this tenet. For most Garou, the definition of a “share” is left for the group as a whole to determine. While it is customary to offer some sign of respect to a Sept Leader, the core of this tenet allow those who do the seizing to apportion their plunder amongst themselves. A tithe to a leader outside the group reaffirms respect for the hierarchy and traditions that guide the lives of Garou. 

When a pack defeats minions of the Wyrm, when a sept survives an attack, or even when werewolves hunt for meat to feed each other, it is a sign of respect to offer the first and choicest parts to the highest in station. This offering can range from the most select cuts of meat to a powerful fetish. This simple tradition reaffirms the natural order of everyday life among the Garou. Ignoring or disrespecting this tenet and acting on selfish impulses is seen as a first step down a deceptively swift road to corruption. 

## Ye Shall Not Eat the Flesh of Humans 

During the Impergium, werewolves often overlooked this precept. Although werewolves understand that in the heat of battle, things happen, during the excess of those times far too many Garou opened themselves to the Wyrm’s corruption by embracing cannibalistic urges. It became a fundamental failure to uphold the protection of humanity as one of Gaia’s creatures. Thus this tenet came back into prominence and has been heavily enforced for generations. 

Packs police their own when it comes to this tenet of the Litany. They watch each other to ensure that none fall victim to the desire to consume human flesh, even if only in the heat of battle. Further, septs watch their packs, hoping to ensure no one falls into this trap. The Council of Tribes has reaffirmed that each Garou must watch her 

brothers and sisters, ensuring that none succumb to the temptation, even in the deepest throes of frenzy. 

Garou in battle are the epitome of savagery. They rend their enemies with tooth and claw, slashing and biting with a fury unmatched by any of Gaia’s other creatures. In the depth of such fury, it is not uncommon for a Garou to bite and rip the flesh of her enemies. Doing so is not a violation of this tenet; she must make a choice, willing or not, to consume the flesh of humans and to eat a human as if she was a prey animal. That is when a Garou has violated the Litany. 

## The Veil Shall Not Be Lifted 

Few responsibilities are more dear to Garou than the protection of the Veil. There are too many enemies waiting for a chance to expose and eradicate the werewolves. Humanity itself, one of the creatures Gaia tasked the Garou to protect, would be the first to seek the utter destruction of the Garou if the humans learned of their existence. As such, this precept is one of the most often-repeated tenets, as Cubs and Cliath are taught the Litany, reminding them of the danger inherent in exposure. 

The Council of Tribes has frequently discussed this tenet in recent days, especially with destruction of so many Garou and caerns. Now more than ever, the Garou cannot afford attention to be drawn to their few remaining safe places and people. Elders have placed strong emphasis on being far more aware of each individual’s actions that may risk to the Veil. For this reason, those who damage the Veil without ensuring that their actions are concealed are severely punished. No more attention can be called to the Garou if they are to succeed at their tasks. 

Garou who count on the Delirium to protect the Veil walk a dangerous line. While rare, there are some humans who are naturally immune to its effects, allowing them to remember in whole any supernatural activities they may witness. Especially in this day and age of cameras and smart phones, recording devices are everywhere. Combatting the Wyrm where it dwells and breeds may encourage a brave Cliath to leap a counter of a convenience store to slaughter a fomor clerk, but doing so without thinking may lead the Garou to murder any possible witnesses or commit other attention-gaining actions to cover up her activities. These two tenets of the Litany are often at odds with each other, requiring wisdom and experience discern exactly how to handle such incidents. Instead of leaping the counter, one might be better served by marking the target and staking out the store until the clerk leaves for the night alone, allowing the Cliath to accomplish her mission to combat the Wyrm, while still defending the Veil. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## Do Not Suffer Thy People to Tend Thy Sickness 

Rare though it is, sometimes a Garou becomes too old, or too infirm through battle scars and injury, to carry on in the fight against the Wyrm. When this situation occurs, two choices remain for such a werewolf. Most commonly, she performs the Rite of Winter Wolf and, after saying goodbye to friends and loved ones, throws herself into battle against a mighty enemy in the hope of slaying it as she dies. The other option, heavily advocated by the Children of Gaia, is to go among humans or wolves to live the remainder of her life out quietly, outside of Garou society. Few werewolves take this option, considering it cowardly. They simply are unable to suddenly cease fighting a war they have dedicated their lives to prosecuting. 

Few dispute the necessity of this tenet of the Litany. While some argue that every willing body in the war is worth keeping as another warrior, others maintain that the resources needed to tend to those who cannot contribute as front-line warriors are better put to use helping those who can. No matter the reality, the truth of the matter is that none have pushed for a change in interpreting this tenet of the Litany with any success. 

Garou bear the scars of many fights. Each one a tells a tale of battle, renown, and one more escape from an enemy that could have claimed their lives. The power of regeneration often allows werewolves to continue the fight, healing their bodies after serious wounds. Sometimes though, these wounds leave battle scars so deep and so damaging that it is impossible to return to the front lines in the war against the Wyrm. Garou who realize this do not burden their packs or septmates with their injury and infirmity. They fight to the last, thrusting themselves into one more battle—one final strike at the Wyrm, if they can. This course is the honorable way, and it is considered worthy of respect. However, there are some Garou who hold too tightly to life, refusing to admit they can no longer fight. They remain far too long, forcing others to care for them, hunt for them, and support them. These Garou force sept and pack to tend their sickness, until something is done about it. Such werewolves are often killed by their own kind, as the Litany demands. 

## The Leader May Be Challenged at Any Time During Peace 

In a structure built upon hierarchy and rigidly defined governance roles, the system of challenging for leadership serves to ensure that the strongest leaders are the ones making the decisions for the whole. This tenet serves as a reminder that, when not under direct threat, the leader 

may be challenged, either proving herself through victory allowing another to rise to power through her defeat. Unscrupulous leaders often find excuses to avoid challenges, declaring the constant threat of the Wyrm too much to allow for a challenge to occur or pushing for challenges that heavily favor the inertia of existing leadership. Rarely does such a situation stand for long, when a new leader takes power, her reprisals are often both swift and severe. 

Historically this measure serves as permission as well as a warning. It reminds Garou that in times of peace, rest, and healing, they should reflect on the wisdom and judgement of those who lead. If another exists who wishes to step forward and put herself into such a position, she must challenge and prove her worth. On the other hand, peacetime provides an opportunity for the current leader to prove to those who beneath her that she remains strong enough, wise enough, and honorable enough to lead. 

Up-and-coming leaders, be they found among one’s pack, sept, or the Garou Nation as a whole, prove themselves in times of peace. While arguments can and are made that the Garou are constantly at war, even the most diehard traditionalist realizes that in longstanding war there are many battles, and the lulls between them are as necessary to winning as each victory is in and of itself. Challenges do not solely exist for the purpose of changing the leadership, but also provide a chance for the populace to see a leader prove herself to one and all. This process helps to instill respect and faith in those would guide the Garou going forward. 

## The Leader May Not Be Challenged During Wartime 

The last thing needed when in the middle of a battle is jockeying for position; it weakens a pack or sept with infighting, instead of focusing on the fight at hand. This tenet ensures that during times of direct threat, challenges for leadership cannot occur, allowing even the most stationhungry Garou to focus on the immediate task, instead of attaining power. This precept is often abused or twisted to suit the purposes of those who benefit most. Citing the constant wartime of the Age of Apocalypse, dishonorable Garou claim that threats are too prevalent to allow for squabbling over leadership, using this tenet of the Litany as a shield for their positions. Since the revival of the Council of Tribes, along with the rise of strong national leadership, such claims have declined, as the focus on reseeding caerns has taken the highest of priorities. 

The Tribal Councilors, when speaking about the Litany and the favored interpretations during this tumultuous time, reiterated that this tenet provides a warning, advising against voluntarily changing leadership while in the midst 

The World of darkness 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

of crisis. It is not a shield; it is not a blanket defense against any challengers arising to seek positions of leadership. It is, however, a caution and a firm reminder that such challenges are to be left for times of at least tenuous peace. Even the opposing factions among council agreed that this precept needed some additional interpretation in the modern era. 

During a siege, with enemies at the gates, is the absolute worst time to seek a change in leadership for any reason other than the death or disability of the previous leader. Even the most dangerous situations become far worse when no one knows where to look for direction. However, Garou are passionate creatures, and the desire to immediately correct a perceived failure is strong in them. This tenet ensures that even when rage and frustration course through their bodies, the law requires them to wait until the proper moment to vent that anger into a formal challenge. So ingrained are the laws of the Litany, that they serve to leash the rage and emotion that would otherwise get the better of even the calmest Garou. 

## Ye Shall Take No Action That Causes a Caern to Be Violated 

Equivalent in seriousness to the protection of the Veil, allowing a caern to be destroyed is a dire crime worthy of the most extreme punishment. When such horrible travesties to come to pass, it is not uncommon for the responsible Garou to be sentenced to death for their laxity. The advent of the Age of Apocalypse has further driven home the importance of this tenet; it is now of paramount importance to guard, nurture, and protect the fragile hope that lies within shard seeds as the werewolves attempt to restore their network of lost caerns. 

The Council of Tribes has decreed that this tenet strictly applies to any who have failed to do everything in their power to protect the caerns they swore to safeguard. With the Wyrm’s myriad attacks on bawns, reaching deep into caern hearts, the definition of “violate” has become a hotly contested issue. Traditionally minded werewolves believe that defenders who allowed their caerns to fall without first sacrificing their own lives were derelict in their duties and should be punished as such, while less strict interpreters of this tenet believe that each warrior is vital, and that allowing them to die now, executed as punishment, when they are needed to ensure the protection of shard seeds, is a great waste. 

Entering the bawn of a caern, bearing Wyrm Taint from battle, in an effort to return to safety and be cleansed is not considered violating a caern. Caerns are intended to provide safe haven and serve as places of security. However, the same Garou, entering a caern heart bearing taint of 

Wyrm corruption, no matter how slight, may be judged differently. The latter instance directly threatens corrupting the most sacred site within the caern itself. How a judge prosecutes this tenet of the Litany against those who may push its boundaries truly differs, depending on the customs of the sept where the offense occurs. Knowledge of how and where to step can prevent tragedy or death when it comes to the safety of a caern. 

## A Brief History of the World of Darkness 

_The wolf has a simple perception of time: what was, what is, and what might be. Humans confuse things by trying to ascribe meaning and purpose to the things we’ve endured. — Verity Argyris, Black Fury, Historian_ 

_The sky was a blanket of naked stars, unobscured by the pollution and lights of the city. Temperatures dropped quickly at night in the desert. Verity felt the bracing sting of the wind even through her black fur, but she caught the faint scent of smoked wood and oiled leather._ 

_Not too far, she thought, maybe a mile or so farther into the badlands. She relished the hunt. It had been far too long since she had run as a wolf under the full moon. Clearly, making this meeting place easy to find hadn’t been a priority, but then she supposed that was the way of oracles. You had to work for your knowledge, and that’s why most of the Elders resented her and the book._ 

_She discovered the camp by accident, blindly following the trail until she almost ran past it. A spark from the campfire, a glowing ember in the darkness, caught her attention and led her down the dune hill to a small crook in the landscape. There she discovered a hidden camp little more than a couple of worn sleeping bags and a small fire._ 

_Verity released a low howl of introduction, neither wanting to go where she was not welcome, nor be throated by an angry Elder. A smoky voice called out to her from the shadows. “I expected you two hours ago, little Fostern.”_ 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

_She shifted back to Homid, adjusted her glasses, and scanned the campsite, searching for the source of the voice. “I can’t see you. I’ve come to meet Amy Hundred-Voices.”_ 

_“That’s because I didn’t want to be seen.” An old woman wearing traveling leathers and a wide-brimmed hat stepped out from the shadows. There was wisdom in the lines of her face. Clearly, she was a wise crone of her tribe. The sigils and patches on her jacket, all revering the horned snake, suggested Uktena. “But you didn’t come here to see me. You want to hear the old stories.”_ 

_Verity shook her head defiantly. “I want to know facts. We deserve to know our history and to learn about the mistakes of the past.”_ 

_“So that you can fix them?” Amy Hundred-Voices set aside her hat and turned towards the fire, placing an old metal coffee pot on a flat stone near the flame. “The tribes have clung to the oral tradition for thousands of years, maybe more. You think you are the one to change all of that.”_ 

_“Not me. I’m just a historian,” Verity admitted, almost defeated. “Maybe my work can inspire someone else—give them a tool to see things from a different perspective. Not a very traditional way to look at it, I know. My moms are furious. I’m supposed to be collecting tales of glory and honor.”_ 

_Amy Hundred-Voices laughed softly. “I hold three degrees and a Master’s in Occidental Culture from the University of New Mexico, and I never thought about writing a textbook for Cubs.”_ 

_“You’ll help me, then?” Verity could hardly believe it would be that easy. “Put me in touch with the Ananasi?”_ 

_The old Uktena shook her head. “Not yet, I think.”_ 

_Verity stepped closer than she intended, passionate about her cause. “I might be a Black Fury, but I haven’t joined the Sanctum of Gaia. Not yet. I’m still trying to decide who is right. Maybe this book will help me with that, if nothing else.”_ 

_“Secrets given out freely lose their value.” The Elder Uktena flashed a wry smile. “You want to find the Ananasi, you’ll have to earn it.”_ 

_This, Verity expected. Elders always had things that needed to be done. “You need me to run an errand? Clear out a periphery zone? My pack’s near. I can call them.”_ 

_“You want my knowledge, my secrets?” Amy Hundred-Voices asked, with a touch of skepticism. “I need to see if you are worthy of them—and I know just how to test you.”_ 

_“I’m ready for anything.”_ 

_“Read to me from your book.”_ 

## _“What?”_ 

_“You’re a historian, aren’t you? I want to know how you will teach others, and if you impress me, I might just help you.”_ 

_Verity swallowed. This was just like defending her thesis—except Professor Amy Hundred-Voices has claws. She dug her binder out from her pack and opened it to the beginning. “Our history begins at the start of the Age of Myth, before the creation of the universe, and the dawn of time….”_ 

## _Historian’s Note_ 

_The book you hold in your hands is the result of years of painstaking research. I’ve traveled the world, recording oral histories from lore masters of all the tribes and even a few rare Fera willing to share knowledge._ 

_I’ve catalogued events and stories using the classical historian’s tool of periodization to categorize the past into quantified, named blocks of time. The definitions are arbitrary, but they facilitate the study and analysis of history._ 

_In the end, I can’t help but feel like I have failed in my ambitions. Turns out that it’s difficult to locate primary sources verifying events that happened before the very creation of the universe._ 

_The truth is, it doesn’t matter if every one of these myths actually happened as we know them. These are the stories that define us as Garou._ 

_Verity Argyris Black Fury, Historian_ 

The World of darkness 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## Age of Myth: Stories of the Creation of the Cosmos 

called Pangaea. She created the shapeshifters, the Garou and the Fera, assigning each of them a role to keep her creation safe and in balance with the Triat. 

The Garou were designated to protect the Material Realm. They culled overgrown populations that threatened to unbalance the ecosystem, hunted powerful predators that would otherwise rampage if unchecked, and fended off the otherworldly spirits and beasts from alien realms that would despoil the purity of the land. 

_The common mistake made when dealing with spirits is presuming that they see time and events from the same perspective as those of us limited by flesh. To them, allegory is reality. — Verity Argyris, Black Fury, Historian_ 

The cosmos can be divided into three primal forces that form all of creation: the Wyld, the Weaver, and the Wyrm, known collectively as the _Triat_ . In the beginning, each member of the Triat operated on pure instinct and acted according to its fundamental nature. The Wyld gave birth to the whole of creation without form. The Weaver shaped reality and gave it form and substance. The Wyrm decayed and destroyed the aspects of the cosmos that had served their purposes, making room for new creations. Together, they forged the stars, built infinite worlds and realms of spirit and flesh, and created all life and gave it meaning. The Wyrm maintained balance, selectively recycling the creations of Wyld and Weaver, forcing harmony from the opposing forces of chaos and order. 

What happened next is open to rampant speculation, strenuous debates, and the cause of more than one challenge. Garou and Fera alike agree that at the beginning, the Triat was in balance. 

Some believe that the Weaver gained consciousness and went mad after gazing upon the whole of the cosmos. Her obsession drove her to attempt to spin the entirety of the Wyld into a patterned existence, and the Wyrm, overwhelmed attempting its job to bring the Weaver into balance, was caught in the Weaver’s web by accident. Others argue that the Weaver grew bitter at the Wyrm’s interference with her creations and sought to seize control. And then there are those who believe that the Wyrm sought to destroy the Wyld altogether, in order to end existence and reshape the next iteration of the cosmos. 

None know the truth for certain. We only agree that the Triat became unbalanced and the cosmos suffered. 

The Celestine spirit Gaia, mother of the Triat, birthed the Material Realm and shaped it according to her designs. The world she created was whole and pure, united in one land 

As the Triat warred against itself, the shockwave of their struggle echoed throughout the cosmos. The Material Realm shattered into the continents. Once a single people, the Garou scattered to the corners of the world, where they founded civilizations and encouraged the growth of kin. The world was still strongly aligned with the Wyld, and little of the Weaver or the Wyrm corrupted it. 

Inspired by the Weaver, early humans created tools to shape and tame the Wyld. They built shelters and forged weapons to protect themselves from the elements and predators. They gathered together, first in villages, and later they began to build cities. The humans weaved order from the chaos of the Wyld, and the Weaver loved them for it. 

Fear of mankind’s potential led to the start of the _Impergium_ , the wholesale slaughter of humanity intended to decimate their population to less-threatening numbers. The spirits say that during this time, the Garou, and most of the Fera, destroyed larger human settlements and purposely retarded the technological and scientific progress of the human race. 

For thousands of years, the Garou and the Fera strictly imposed population caps on early humanity, killing and sometimes eating them when their herds grew too large. Memories from these dark nights still survives in humans, taking shape as a primal fear of shapeshifters called the _Delirium_ . 

Furious with Gaia’s children, the Weaver began calcifying the Umbra, leading to the creation of the _Gauntlet_ . This act separated the Material Realm from the infinite spirit worlds of the Umbra, and made it more difficult for the Garou and the Fera to hunt humanity. 

## Coyote Tells his Tale 

In the beginning, Coyote created all things. Bored with his place in the stars, he sang the Earth into existence. First, he created the water and then the ducks, for Coyote did not want to be lonely on his new planet. As the creatures among the stars saw the beautiful world Coyote had created, they came to visit, and many of them chose to stay. Coyote was very proud of his creation and all of his new friends, and he boasted of his exploits to Luna. Luna was enraged with Coyote for luring all 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

of the creatures to Earth, and she turned her face away from him. This is the tale of why Coyote sings to Luna every night, in hopes of gaining her blessing once again. 

**
## _Historian’s Note_ 

_Although Garou have their tales of how the world began, each breed of Fera maintains their own legends. While there are some similarities found between these different tales, each breed has their own unique view on how the world came to be._ 

_Although relations with the Fera have improved in recent years, many are still hesitant to divulge too much about the history of their people. Those who were willing to speak have given me glorious tales, some of which are catalogued here for posterity._ 

_Verity Argyris Black Fury, Historian_ 

After creating the ducks and singing down the other creatures, Coyote created man, a brand-new creature that he hoped would bring him a great deal of joy and amusement. It was not long after that the other creatures begged Coyote to undo what he had done. The men were too violent and destructive, and they could not peacefully live alongside the other creatures. To placate his friends, Coyote allowed each animal to take some of the humans away with them, in order to teach them how to live properly. This is how Wolf, Cat, Bear, Shark, and the others came to have changing families of their own. When all of the creatures had taken their share of humans, Spider gathered all of the remaining to her breast, ensnaring them in her expansive web. Now when Worm, being too slow by far, came to claim his share of the humans, there were none left. He became upset and begged Coyote for help, for he coveted humans of his own. Coyote sent Worm to see Spider to ask for some of her children; after all, that would only be fair. Coyote knew that this request would be refused, for Spider was greedy and wanted to keep all of the humans to herself. Spider told Worm that she would give him all of the humans that he could free from her web. As Worm climbed higher, he was able to shake loose some of the humans, but he found that he himself had been caught up in her silk, thwarted by his driving need to steal as many of Spider’s humans as he could. As Worm thrashed within her web, Coyote appeared to offer his help—but Worm was as proud as he was greedy, and he refused all assistance. This is the story of how the Wyrm became trapped in the Weaver’s web, and how he will remain in her clutches until he learns his lesson of humility. 

The humans who fell from Spider’s web began to roam the land, and they were touched by a darkness of insanity. They began to build and explore, and destruction followed in their wake. Although he was still ensnared, Worm was able to influence his new followers. The changers of the world tried to hold his people back, but there were too many lands and too many divides between changer families. Coyote finally felt that it was time for him to do something, for of course, he had caused all of this in the first place. He would not rob the humans of the freedom he had given them, but nor could he ignore the suffering of his children. Coyote gathered up the cleverest and wisest beings he could find, and he showered them with his gifts and blessings. He taught his children to teach lessons and pull pranks, and he gave them the keys to the stars. He taught them to integrate with the humans, so none would ever suspect that they existed. Coyote taught his children to act as the teachers of humans and shifters alike, to show others the errors of their ways, in hopes of teaching them to resist the temptations of Worm. Above all, he told his children to have fun and enjoy the life that he had given them, for their time on Earth would be short. This is the story of how Coyote created the Nuwisha. 

## Age of Legends: Founding the Garou Nation 

_A legend frames the values of the society that created it and reveals the character of the society that retells it. — Verity Argyris, Black Fury, Historian_ 

The Impergium lasted for thousands of years. Humanity came to fear the woods at night, as the Garou hunted them for sport and feasted on their flesh. Hunter and hunted were forever marked. The primal dread of the beast—known as Delirium—was burned into humanity’s consciousness, scarring them with a racial memory that is now triggered by a mere glimpse of a werewolf in Crinos form. Their overzealousness to perform their sacred duties to protect humanity and Gaia left the Garou vulnerable to the influence of the Wyrm. 

The World of darkness 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

The Children of Gaia rejected this senseless slaughter, holding close the Garou’s ancient pledge to protect humanity and their sacred duty to Gaia. They were cast aside as the tribes went to war for dominance, territory, and control over the humans as breeding stock and slaves. Once the Garou began to fight among each other, they were challenged for dominance by the humans they claimed to protect. 

Terrified, humans organized and learned the weaknesses of the monsters that hunted them by moonlight. Ordinary wolves were hunted to near extinction in many lands. They fought the creatures from their nightmares with fire and silver, and on occasion, with newfound faith and otherworldly powers connected to the Triat. 

The Garou had always known other monsters hid in the night, but they came to learn that these others also had an interest in humanity as feeding stock. These creatures were willing to defend what they considered theirs. Immortal vampires protected the cities where they slept and organized humans to defend them. Some human mystics made their own pacts with the spirits, gaining access to vast powers over reality itself. 

Rests-the-Moon, a mighty Silver Fang king, understood the situation was no longer tenable and decided to undergo a quest to seek wisdom from Gaia and Luna. He returned alone, bloody and bruised, but determined to bring the world back from the precipice of disaster. First, he passed along sacred knowledge from Gaia to the Silver Fang Theurges, and they raised the first caern, the Sept of the Crescent Moon. 

The wisest Elders of each tribe called a Concolation. Reststhe-Moon freely shared the lore and knowledge about how to raise and cultivate a caern. Then, when the Elders were impressed with his generosity, he asked each of them for their advice about the Impergium. The Red Talons argued to continue the hunt and ensure that humanity would never threaten the Wyld or their wolf kinfolk again. Many among the tribes agreed with this wisdom. Why allow the humans time to rise up against them? 

The Black Furies protested that the humans had not yet learned their lesson and continued to enslave women and children as their property. The Glass Walkers argued that they were pushing the humans away from the places of the Wyld. They already clustered into cities where the power of the Weaver reigned supreme. How long would it be before the clever hairless apes turned their industry towards the destruction of the Wyld? Why drive them towards their enemies? 

This argument split the Concolation. Some tribes argued that the Garou should discipline the rebellious villages, keeping their activities in check. Others argued that the humans would better serve as partners, as Gaia had planned. 

It was then that Rests-the-Moon revealed Gaia’s wisdom to the tribes. The Garou agreed to maintain their own society, separate from humanity. They forged the Western Concordat and set into law the Litany that governs it, creating what is commonly known as the Garou Nation. 

Rests-the-Moon ruled over this new civilization as High King, and every tribe selected their best, in their own fashion, to send as advisors to this new king. These Garou became the first Council of Tribes. The Impergium came to an end, and werewolves faded into the shadows of history, becoming legends. 

## Age of Tragedy: The War of Rage 

_Rage is a gift from Luna to inspire us towards courage and provide us the strength to protect this world. The tragedy is that we’ve abused this gift, more often than not, and suffered the consequences. — Verity Argyris, Black Fury, Historian_ 

Word of the new Western Concordat quickly spread across the Material Realm. The Litany became the supreme law among the tribes, and one by one septs confirmed their membership in the Garou Nation. 

The period of isolation ended, and the roles of the auspices within Garou society calcified. Philodox assumed the role of Truthcatcher and became judges over the Litany. The Ragabash questioned the Litany and tested boundaries as sept laws became uniform across tribal lines. Theurges mastered the secrets of the spirits, and a Fianna named Wyrmslayer forged the first klaive. The Ahroun patrolled bawns and killed the enemies of the nation, and the Galliards sang tales of heroic deeds and terrible downfalls. Yet still, the specter of rage hung over the new nation of Garou. 

The Silver Fangs and the Shadow Lords warred against the tribes that would one day be known as the Uktena, the Croatan, and the Wendigo. Receiving divine inspiration from Gaia herself, these tribes crossed the Bering Strait into what they would name the Pure Lands, where they 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

**
discovered a brave new world tainted by banes and monsters. Together, they cleansed the continent of the Wyrm; trapping the banes they could not destroy, discovering new kin, and helping found human civilizations. 

Arrogant from their victories and showing dominance, the Silver Fangs plunged the Garou Nation into a vicious conflict with the Fera known as the _War of Rage_ . Animosity between the Garou and the Fera first surfaced during the Impergium. Fera breeds complained that the Garou targeted their kinfolk during raids against humanity to purposely limit their populations. The Garou countered that their leadership of Gaia’s children was challenged by the Fera’s continual disrespect of the werewolves’ role as protectors of Gaia, and that it was necessary to show dominance to ensure the security of the Material Realm. 

Tensions mounted to untenable levels, war seemed inevitable, and the flashpoint that triggered the bloodshed occurred at a diplomatic meeting between the Silver Fangs and a number of the Fera. Historical accounts of the events of the meeting differ wildly, but the single constant is the death of the heroic Silver Fang, Petros the Unyielding. 

King Konstantos the Savage accused a Nagah named Vinta of murdering the Silver Fang hero. According to the Fera, Petros the Unyielding had been a messenger of peace, returning to the tribes with a proposal for a treaty and a warning that there would be war should aggressions continue. The Nagah claimed Konstantos himself killed Petros in a fit of uncontrollable rage, and blamed the murder on the Fera. 

The Silver Fangs demanded the other tribes support their war against the Nagah. The idea that one of the Changing Breeds, the lowly Nagah, would dare to strike down a Garou, enraged the werewolves, but the Children of Gaia pleaded with the Garou Nation to make one last desperate attempt at unity. They convinced the Gurahl to meet with the Garou Nation and try to resolve the underlying issues before unending war killed everyone and left Gaia unprotected. 

Again, accounts of this failed diplomatic venture differ between the Fera and the Garou. The Gurahl claim the haughty Silver Fangs demanded the secret of defeating death that Gaia had taught them. The Garou claim they asked the Gurahl to resurrect Petros as penance from the Fera, but the werebears refused, as the dead warrior’s soul was now with Gaia, and they claimed it would be foolish to disrupt the cosmic dance to return him to life when his purpose had been fulfilled. 

The Garou threatened war again, saying the Fera should return what they took from the nation or pay the price in blood. Defiant, the Gurahl refused, and the two sides 

came to blows, starting the War of Rage. Those Gurahl who survived the encounter sought allies among the other Fera. The vicious Grondr, the wereboars, and the brutal Apis, the wereaurochs, came to their aid, and together they retaliated. 

The conflict quickly extended throughout the known world. The Mokolé, the werelizards, suffered terrible losses in the initial stages of the War of Rage. The Garou defiled their wallows, smashed their eggs, and slaughtered their kin. They survived only by retreating to the deepest sections of the Wyld. There, the early humans turned against them, fearing their Drachid forms as monsters. 

The Silent Striders battled the Bubasti of the Bastet in Egypt, and the other tribes of the Garou Nation decimated the werecats where they found them. The Bastet were overwhelmed, and a number of their lesser breeds were devastated until they retreated to tropical climates without native wolf populations or to remote territories, such as mountain ranges or rainforests. The Simba and Khan fortified Africa, driving the Silent Striders from their lands. 

The Garou Nation hunted the Nagah to near-extinction. The Fianna wiped out the Nagah in Ireland and killed every single snake there. Yet strangely, the Fenrir and the Fianna turned their gaze from the Corax, allowing them to survive. The wereravens attempted to mediate a truce to end the war, but neither side was in the mood for peace. However, the Corax did manage to secure safe routes for Fera refugees and aided the Ratkin in escaping complete annihilation. 

The Ratkin were on the verge of extinction before the last of the Ratkin Bards gathered at the Field of Nettles in an attempt to sue for peace. The Bards delayed the final attacks long enough for the remainder of the Ratkin to escape into the Deep Umbra. 

The War of Rage did not extend to the Pure Lands of the Uktena, Croatan, and Wendigo. They made peace and found kin among those who migrated there. The other werewolves of the Garou Nation did not pursue those Fera who fled to those lands. 

The Apis and Grondr refused to hide, and in turn they were slaughtered by the superior numbers and forces of the Garou. The war had turned, and even the stubborn Gurahl realized that unless they fled, all would be lost. 

Eventually the Garou lost their taste for war, but their pride kept them from accepting surrender. The Children of Gaia and Stargazers argued that the Fera were needed for Gaia to thrive, and that surely the Garou would not place their egos above their mother’s needs. The Silent Striders agreed, and simply made peace with the few Bastet they could find, 

The World of darkness 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

ignoring further requests for troops and aid. The Bone Gnawers and Glass Walkers followed suit, but they had mostly stayed out of the conflict, their interests leaning more towards humans and the new advancements in technology that had long been stymied by the Garou. 

Some felt that Luna was angered at how the Garou misused her blessing and cursed the Silver Fangs for their arrogance. The Shadow Lords subtly blamed the Silver Fang’s lack of leadership for the horrors of the War of Rage, sensing a chance to claim primacy of the tribes. The Fenrir, Black Furies, and Fianna privately lamented their deeds during the war, but could not bring themselves to question the decisions that led to them. Only the White Howlers wanted to continue the fight, having come to hunger for the flesh of the Fera. 

The Ananasi remained hidden, subtly stoking the fires in hopes that both sides would weaken the other. Even through their seething need for vengeance, they could see that the war had gone too far, and the whole of creation could be destroyed by this conflict. Through the connection between the Corax and Fianna, the Ananasi leaked information suggesting that the Heart of the Wyrm had been located deep inside of Malfeas, and if it could be captured and destroyed, the Wyrm’s influence would subside. In truth, this was a clever ruse. The location really led to the opal-prison of Queen Ananasa, the mother of all Ananasi. The fact that those few who realized this truth died in the mission is likely the only reason the Garou as a whole did not turn on the werespiders and wipe them from existence. 

The Garou Nation, yearning for glory, temporarily abandoned the War of Rage to prepare for an assault upon Malfeas. Their scouts blazed a trail to the hellish realm, and the entire nation rallied behind the idea of ending evil forever. Many warriors died horrifically before the Theurges determined that they had been deceived. When the werewolves returned to their efforts destroying the Fera, the Garou discovered that nearly all of the other shapeshifters had vanished. Some privately believed the war and the actions that followed were one of the plagues foretold to descend on the world by the Prophecy of the Phoenix. The deception and madness that seemed to linger in the minds of the greatest leaders brought the death toll as high as any naturally occurring pestilence. 

The Council of Tribes gathered together at another Concolation to discuss the matter. Some claimed that the dominance of the Garou was now clear, and the point of the war had already been made. Others argued that the real war should be with the Wyrm, and that the Fera were needed to maintain Gaia. In the end, the Garou Nation simply declared its victory and privately licked its wounds. 

## Age of Man: Rise of Human Civilization 

_Humans lack claws and fangs to defend themselves in a world teeming with monsters and beasts. Is it any wonder that they turned to the Weaver for protection? — Verity Argyris, Black Fury, Historian_ 

## **1000 BCE to 500 CE** 

While the Garou Nation recovered from the War of Rage, and the Fera hid in the remote and desolate places of the world, humans came to forget both of them and flexed their own muscles. They mastered the arts of the written word, architecture, and science. Humanity leveraged the Weaver’s tools to create great cities of brick and stone, and founded vast empires—including Babylon, Tikal, Alexandria, Beijing, and Rome. 

The Silver Fangs involved themselves with the noble families of Russia, founding settlements in Novgorod and Kiev Rus. As the centuries passed, they and their kinfolk migrated south and married into many of Europe’s royal and noble human tribes and petty kingdoms, until they reached Rome. 

The Glass Walkers and Bone Gnawers had already found a niche in the Eternal City, keeping their presence subtle, so as to not arouse the potent vampires and Wyrm-affiliated creatures that hid beneath the earth. The Glass Walkers had come to Rome from the Middle East, relishing in the Greek philosophies and the opportunities in trade. The Bone Gnawers settled in the Aventine Hill, living amongst the outcasts and poor of society. The power and prestige of influence proved to be too tempting for these tribes, and soon they fought on behalf of Rome. 

The Stargazers retreated from the world, joining and founding great monasteries, pondering where the Garou Nation had gone wrong. Likewise, the Shadow Lords elected to have little involvement with human civilization, choosing instead to dwell among the barbarians who would one day sack Rome. 

In the Pure Lands, around 400 Before the Common Era (BCE), the Guatemalan Mayan city-states rose and came 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

to power over Mesoamerica. The Uktena advised caution as they discovered these humans worshipped blood gods that demanded sacrifices from their people. The Pure Ones and the hidden vampires waged a secret war for the soul of the land in shadows and the hidden places where none could see. 

In Egypt, during that same time, the Silent Striders apologized to the Mokolé and made peace. Together, they allied themselves with the vampiric Osirian League to stop the ancient vampire Sutekh. At the final battle, as the tribe bound the godlike vampire into unconsciousness and imprisonment far beneath the earth, the creature struck one final blow—a curse that would reverberate throughout the tribe for two millennia. 

The Fenrir and their ancient enemy Wotan, known to the humans as Odin, founded new settlements that quickly spread into human myth. The Fenrir of Heimhalla became locked into a war with the Jotunn kingdom of Utgard and the Aesir kingdom of Valhalla. This bloody conflict decimated all sides, until the surviving Fenrir, led by Great Fenris himself, travelled westward, settling in the lands of modern Scandinavia. 

By the time Augustus seized control of the Roman Empire, its reach extended across Europe and swaths of Africa. A coalition of warriors from the Black Furies, Fianna, White Howlers, and the Get of Fenris aided their kinfolk in resisting this brutal human empire’s effort at conquering their homelands. 

who survived joined their ranks. The Roman Centurion Titus Germanicus, stationed at Hadrian’s Wall, attempted to forge an alliance with the Pict king Brennus, the last of the true White Howlers, against the onslaught, but their new alliance was crushed by the savagery of the Black Spiral Dancers. 

The Fenrir and the Fianna turned against each other, furious at the loss of the White Howlers and the advance of the Roman Empire. New enemies, called fomori, were discovered and battled. These so-called Hollow Men were thought to have come from Rome itself. 

In the Pure Lands, the three brother tribes explored and expanded their influence by mingling with the native people they discovered. The Uktena shared their wisdom, teaching their new kin about Gaia and the spirits. Some of these new kinfolk turned that knowledge against their benefactors, attempting to rule over humans. The Wendigo and the Croatan destroyed the Mound Builders in retaliation for falling to the Wyrm’s deceptions. 

The Uktena took it upon themselves to patrol the Pure Lands and seek out the source of the growing Wyrm Taint, only calling upon their brothers when a particular danger had been identified and quantified. Great civilizations arose, and the brother tribes were there to guide and protect them from Wyrm-spawned beasts and monsters. 

## Age of Ruin: The Long Night 

A young Fianna kinfolk, Queen Boudicca of the Iceni, was publically flogged by Roman legionnaires and forced to watch as her children were brutalized. The trauma of the incident forced her First Change late in life. Queen Boudicca raised an army against the Roman legions, which were backed by the Wyrm. 

By 80 Common Era (CE), the Caledonian human tribes of Britannia had fought so fiercely that the Romans built Hadrian’s Wall to mark the furthest border of Rome and to protect themselves from the wild people of the north. Forces of the Wyrm opened a Wyrm-Tunnel in Caledonia, which would come to be known as Scotland. A pack of White Howlers attempted to raze this breach into their territory and were captured, dragged away to Malfeas, and tortured. Strangely, their torturers sensed a commonality with the White Howlers, based on their penchant for cannibalism, and they offered the lost Garou a chance to dance the Spiral Labyrinth. 

These new Black Spiral Dancers returned to the Material Realm to attack Romans and White Howlers alike. They dragged prisoners back to the Spiral Labyrinth, and those 

_Popular legend has it that the Emperor Nero fiddled as Rome burned. If that is so, then when Gaia burned during the Dark Ages, the Garou Nation added kindling. — Verity Argyris, Black Fury, Historian_ 

## **500 CE to 1490 CE** 

The Red Talons watched the rise of cities and human infrastructure, such as roads and bridges, with ever-growing suspicion and fury. They argued for a return of the Impergium, but the other tribes ignored them. The humans did what they have always done: cut down trees, cleared land for farming, and tried to impose the Weaver’s will upon the Wyld. 

The World of darkness 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

And when the human empires began to hunt their fellow Garou’s kinfolk, the Red Talons sneered, until the Romans began to target all wolves, endangering the Red Talons’ own herds and packs. In secret, the Red Talons practiced a diminished form of the Impergium, stalking and attacking those humans that dared venture into the woods after nightfall. 

The shadow of Rome was long, reaching out across Europe, Africa, and the Middle East. When the barbarians sacked the Eternal City, the Shadow Lords, the Fenrir, and the Fianna were there, finding glory in the defeat of the empire that had slain so many of their kin. 

The Bone Gnawers once again became refugees as they fled the city, seeking out protection and shelter in the younger European cities. They settled wherever they found the possibility of hope and protection. 

The disappearance of the White Howlers allowed for the ascension of King Arturus of the Gurahl, who united the Britons as High Chief. While his reign eventually came to an end, legend held that he would remain in hibernation until the dawning of the Age of Apocalypse. 

In 600 CE, the Silent Striders’ preeminent Theurge, Simeon Abd al Hakim, known as Fire Walker within the Garou Nation, received a sacred vision that foretold Gaia’s death by fire and smoke. First, there a Red Star would rise, and then the world’s caerns would burn to nothing more than ash. Then, Gaia would fall; not because the cause was hopeless, but because the Garou would fail in their duties. He saw the Garou Nation desperately sending out Metis werewolves into battle, seeking to gain any sort of edge. It is said that Fire Walker succumbed to Harano shortly after revealing this vision to his tribe. 

The Silver Fangs traveled north and married chieftains and leaders of the European human tribes. Here, they sought to establish their dominance once again by using the isolated nature of fiefs and villages to impose their will on humanity, fueling feuds between lords to keep them distrustful of one another and unable to attend the needs of their citizens, so they could not despoil Gaia’s treasures. A Silver Fang kinfolk, Vladimir I, claimed the throne of Russia and stabilized the tribe’s holdings. 

Centuries later, in 1066 CE, the Norman Conquest of England began, with the Silver Fangs and the Fenrir secretly working alongside the Normans. 

The Glass Walkers insinuated themselves into the Roman Catholic Church for protection from the ancient vampires that ruled the cities. They attempted to inspire places of learning in medieval Europe. With the Children of Gaia by 

their side, they worked to soften the church’s perspective and prepare humanity to accept aspects of Gaia by introducing icons of Mary as symbols of Gaia, encouraging the belief that rural landscapes are places of peace and reflection. Other Glass Walkers ventured east to explore the glory of the Parthian Empire and found kinfolk suitable for their tribe along with new scientific ideas to explore and a different perspective on the Weaver. 

As the Fianna mourned the loss of their brother tribe, the White Howlers, the Fenrir sensed weakness and began a siege on their lands that lasted for more than 500 years, until the Battle of Colntarf in 1014. A treaty with the Silver Fangs assured the Fianna’s claim on Ireland in exchange for their acceptance of the sovereignty of House Winter Snow in Britain. Meanwhile, the Fenrir took up residence in nearly all parts of the former Roman Empire and solidified their alliance with the Silver Fangs. 

Spurred on by their victories, and limited by their treaty with the Silver Fangs, the Fenrir led sea expeditions to explore new places to settle and protect. Evidence suggests they made it as far as the Pure Lands, settling in a place they named Newfoundland, which triggered a number of skirmishes with the Wendigo and the Croatan. After a few short years, the Get of Fenris abandoned the settlement. 

Legend holds that Great Fenris returned to lead his tribe against the monster Wotan. They met in battle in the Ural Mountains, where a number of Wyrm-Tained creatures protected Wotan. The Fenrir won the war, but at the cost of Great Fenris’ eternal banishment to the other side of the Gauntlet, bound in silver chains to prevent him from entering the Near Realms or the Material Realm. 

As the Uktena sought to study a potential Wyrm infestation in 1189, the Wendigo and the Croatan acted and purged the first major city in the Pure Lands, called Cahokia, somewhere near what is now present-day Illinois. This act created a divide between the three brother tribes. They argued bitterly, resulting in resentment and estrangement from each other. 

In 1229, the words of Fire Walker proved true, and the Flaying Plague ravaged shapeshifters and their kinfolk. Leaving the human world untouched, this Malfean plague brought death to untold numbers of the various shapeshifters and their kinfolk, human and animal alike. Only through the tireless efforts of Silent Strider Theurges and the aid of powerful healing spirits was the plague eventually stopped, though not before decimating kinfolk and shapeshifter populations throughout Europe and the Middle East. 

On the run from the star-spawn horrors discovered in the Deep Umbra, the Ratkin returned to the Material Realm 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

in 1345. They were horrified to discover the new cities and advances that mankind had created. They quickly settled into 13 colonies, and the following year, they released the Black Plague to cull the human population to reasonable numbers. 

The rise of the mercantile class weakened a number of tribes that counted on their old feudal relationships to maintain power. For the first time, the Silver Fangs began to consider the Glass Walkers rivals. However, the remains of the Roman Empire’s road network, and a renewed interest in trade, proved to be a boon for the Silent Striders. They swiftly built a reputation as worthy messengers and envoys. 

The Black Plague further weakened the political strength of the new nation-states, leading to the ascendency of the Church, which began to concentrate on human affairs rather than heavenly ones. Humans fell back on faith to give them a hope for survival. When the Inquisition started burning vampires, many Garou secretly cheered, believing that perhaps the humans would turn against the Wyrm and aid them in their duty to Gaia. The Glass Walkers were instrumental in saving many Garou from burning, thanks to the misinformation they seeded about how to identify a werewolf. 

The Black Furies struggled against the Church and its persecution of wise women and faiths that cleaved to the ideal of a mother goddess. They reached out to the Children of Gaia and the Glass Walkers in an attempt to stem the tide of the systematic oppression of women and children. Together, they tried to maneuver the Church into a softer position and preached tolerance. It was a bold move, and it might have worked, had the Inquisition not discovered the truth about werewolves during one of their investigations. Their strange faith worked against the Garou, and even the spirits refused to aid the trapped werewolves. 

Many Shadow Lords were impressed by the early advances of Islam, and they inserted themselves into its ranks, making inroads to the Middle East. Few formally adopted the religion for themselves; however, they came to admire the beauty of the culture, despite conflicts with the brutal vampire clan known to westerners as Assamites. Some Shadow Lords ventured closer to the Mongols, producing some of the early Hakken. 

Hoping to stem the tide of the Inquisition, some Garou attempted to reignite the Impergium—they were the first to be burned. The Inquisition fought werewolves as eagerly as they did vampires. The humans’ strength terrified the Garou, seeming as though the humans were 

possessed by the Wyrm itself. The deep-seeded memory of the Impergium and the Delirium drove them onward in their desire to eradicate the creatures of the night. Slowly, the fires died and the embers were extinguished, but the Garou had learned their lesson: always protect the Veil. 

In 1240, the Mongols invaded Russia. Supported in secret by the Shadow Lords and used as pawns to strike down the Silver Fangs, the Mongol Empire forced Russia to become part of the Golden Horde Empire, which lasted until 1480. This maneuver sparked an enmity between the tribes that endured until the Age of Apocalypse and provided the foundation for new political factions within the Garou Nation. The Shadow Lords lost their advantage over their rivals a few centuries later, when the Turks invaded their own lands. 

By 1426, the Uktena found themselves driven from most of their Central American holdings by the Aztec Empire. In time, they discovered these humans worshipped a coven of Wyrm-entities that demanded blood sacrifices from the population. Their human troops were supported by insane vampires and monstrosities previously unknown on the Material Realm, such as the vicious Heart Eaters. 

The Impaler Prince, Vlad Tepes, came to power in 1456, rising as a new enemy for the Shadow Lords. Born within their own homelands, Tepes was initially considered an ally, due to his war against the Turks. However, he later turned to the fiendish Tzimisce for succor and was transformed into a vampire. Torn between protecting their kinfolk from the Turks and allowing a vampire to gain power within their lands, the Shadow Lords turned on Dracula and attempted to assassinate him. Dracula survived the attempt and continues to war against the Shadow Lords to this very night. 

The southern tribes, under the protection of the Wendigo, became part of the League of Five Nations in 1450. This act was an attempt to heal the rift between the three brother tribes, but rivalries between various First People Nations split their internal cohesion. This division would later weaken the brother tribes right before the incursion of the European tribes. 

In time, humanity took a sharp turn towards science and logic. The Inquisition had soured them on faith and, indirectly, magic. Mankind forgot about monsters and terrors of the night. Towards the end of the 14[th] century, the fae found they could no longer sustain themselves in the Material Realm and they left en masse, fleeing to Arcadia. Many werewolves, particularly the Fianna, saw this departure as a sign of more troubles to come. 

The World of darkness 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

**
## The Prophecy of the Phoenix 

**
## _Historian’s Note_ 

_The Prophecy of the Phoenix has been translated numerous times from the original Arabic transcription as foretold in the year 600 CE by the prophet Simeon Abd al Hakim, deed name “Fire Walker”. Translations throughout the centuries have seen many small divergences. What follows is a new transcription given to me by the Garou Nation’s foremost linguistic scholar. This translation has been several years in the making and may provide new insight into the Age of Apocalypse._ 

_Verity Argyris Black Fury, Historian_ 

When Luna hung pregnant in the sky, Phoenix took me. 

As her face slipped into the Umbra, he carried me in his claws. 

High above the world. 

So that I could see beyond tomorrow. 

And I looked. 

I beheld the future. 

I saw the decimation of our kin. Hunted beyond hunting, death beyond death, to the last one. There were no more children or grandchildren. No more fathers or mothers. This was the first Sign Phoenix gave to me, telling of what the Children of the Weaver—the Humans—would do to us. 

And I looked. 

Mind’s EyE And again, I beheld the future. ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

I saw the Children of the Weaver bring forth life. A great tide of Humans rising. I saw more emerge, until Gaia groaned at their weight. Houses overrunning, rakes stabbing, hands clawing at the parched earth, as they try to feed from Her. This was the second Sign of the last days, that the Phoenix showed me, of what the Humans would do. 

I looked again. 

I beheld the third Sign. 

So many. So many children. So many Humans. And they fell against each other, one on one, and the Wyrm brought forth corruption and gave each a measure. And I saw a strange Fire. Out of control, the great Plume rose over the wilderness, spreading death wherever it touched in the dark and cold land. And I heard the agony of the Seas as She keened, for some drunken fool had poured a lake of black death out into her. 

I turned my head away in disgust, but I could not help but look again. 

And I beheld, then, the fourth Sign. 

The Wyrm grew powerful; its wings fanned the breezes of decay. It spread diseases, and they were horrible. The Herd became afflicted with diseases of the head and of the blood. Children were born twisted. Animals fell sick, and no one could cure them. In these final days, even the Warriors of Gaia could not escape the palsied talons of the sickness-bringing deathbird. 

With a tear in my eye, I looked again, and the Phoenix showed me the fifth Sign. 

Other Plumes rose like death-spears towards the beautiful sky. Piercing it, letting Father Sun burn and parch Gaia. The air grew hot; even in the darkness of Winter it was warm. Plants withered in the sun. The snows fell in the desert as a great Malady consumed Gaia. A cry of pain and disease arose from the dying forests; as one, our kin cried tears of mourning. 

Then, as though a veil were torn from my eyes, the sixth Sign was revealed. 

In these last days, Gaia shakes in rage. Fire boils from her depths and ash shrouds the sky. The Wyrm skulks in the shadows and rears to strike. The old ones are gone; the Guardians of the pathways and the Crossroads are finished. In these final days, the sixth Sign will make itself known in the Packs that form. Each Pack will be blessed with a Quest, a Sacred Journey it must perform. Such is the will of Gaia. 

And then I saw the Sky turn black, and the Moon was as blood. 

The seventh Sign I glimpsed, though I could not look on it in full. But I could feel its heat blistering my skin. 

The Apocalypse. The final days of the world. The Moon was swallowed by the Sun, and it burned in his belly. Unholy fires fell to the ground, burning us all, twisting us and making us vomit blood. The Wyrm had made itself manifest in the towers and rivers, in the air and Land. Everywhere its children ran rampant: devouring, destroying, calling down curses of every kind. And the Herd ran in fear. And the Dark Ones, the Children of the Wyrm, crawled from their caves and walked the streets in daylight. 

I turned my head from the sight of it. Phoenix told me: “This is as it shall be, but not as it should.” 

Phoenix left me then. 

Now, I cannot dream. I only remember the Signs, each one in perfect detail. These are the last days. May Gaia have mercy on us. The World of darkness 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## Age of Sorrow: Incursion of the Pure Lands 

_Sorrow is a word that means both grief and regret. I can think of no better word to describe this time in our history. — Verity Argyris, Black Fury, Historian_ 

## **1492 CE to 1759 CE** 

Italian explorer and mapmaker Christopher Columbus set sail on behalf of King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella of Spain in an attempt to locate a sea route west to Asia from Europe. He initially landed in the present-day Bahamas, and believed he had indeed reached India. In 1495, Columbus made his second voyage, and this time, he traveled with a host of Wyrm-influenced colonists who enslaved the native population as they began to settle the New World. The kinfolk of European Garou began to make the perilous sea journey in hopes of finding new land to settle, forgetting that people already lived there. 

The sheer number of immigrants overwhelmed the magical wards of the brother tribes. Initially, the Pure Ones did not understand the gravity of this new threat  until several new settlements, complete with humans armed with deadly Weaver technology, had been founded. 

In 1521, Shadow Lords came across the sea with their kinfolk, the conquistadors, and they conquered much of Mexico, slaughtering Wyrm-tainted banes and innocent human civilizations alike.  They called on the Uktena to aid them before the invasion began in earnest, expecting them to bend knee as a sign of submission in recognition of the fact that the Shadow Lords had previously defeated them.
