She cannot be challenged, unless that challenge is issued by someone of higher Rank, for the next 30 days or two game sessions, whichever is longer. 

## **Award Bonus:** +1 Glory 

## Paragon 

A Garou named a _Paragon_ is an example of self-restraint, fully in control of her rage. Her sept considers her influence a blessing to the hot-tempered in their midst. For the next 30 days or two game sessions, a Paragon can expand a trait of Honor Renown to grant a willing recipient an automatic success in tests to resist frenzy outside of combat, provided the Paragon can speak and be heard. 

## **Award Bonus:** +1 Honor 

## Reckless 

Wisdom provides a light that guides Garou towards proper behavior. A Garou named _Reckless_ is considered foolish. While carrying this moniker, she cannot gain or spend Wisdom Renown. This moniker lasts 30 days, or two games, whichever is longer. 

## **Assignment Penalty:** -1 Wisdom 

## Tarnished 

A Garou who has been caught violating the spirits is considered to be Tarnished. While carrying this moniker, she temporarily gains all of the penalties of the flaw Spirit Infamy (see **Chapter Five: Merits and Flaws, Flaws, Spirit Infamy** , page 326). This moniker lasts for 30 days or two games, whichever is longer. 

## **Assignment Penalty:** -1 Wisdom 

## Worthy 

One who is _Worthy_ serves as a paragon of justice and a keeper of the Litany. As she is an example for the young, a Den Parent often calls upon a Worthy Garou to teach 

Chapter NiNe: SoCial SyStemS 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

Cubs the ways of the Garou Nation and to inspire the next generation. Winners of this laurel find themselves granted minor social favors from others as a sign of respect and acknowledgement of the good works they have done for the sept as a whole. 

## **Award Bonus:** +1 Wisdom 

## Youngling 

All Cubs (Garou with 0 dots of the Rank background) bear this moniker. _Younglings_ are free to ask questions at appropriate times, and receive reduced punishments for any infractions committed due to their inexperience. At no cost, a Den Parent can assign the moniker _Dishonorable_ to any werewolf who harms a Youngling unnecessarily. The Youngling moniker persists until the Cub becomes a Cliath, earning her first dot of Rank. 

## **Award Bonus:** None 

## Challenges 

_“Let the Philodox have their games and the Theurges their riddles. A proper challenge between Ahroun must be brutal and savage, lest they forget the price of victory and the cost of defeat.”_ 

- Johan “Razortooth” Heimdall, Elder Get of Fenris Ahroun 

Despite sharing a common cause and a structured hierarchy, conflicts are bound to arise even in a group as united in purpose as the Garou. While a werewolf’s Rank is often enough to command respect, right and wrong can become blurred when problems fall between defined auspice and position lines. Personal disputes can erupt outside of 

the hierarchy altogether, as tiny slights and impositions aggravate a werewolf’s predatory nature. Most importantly, a Garou who views her leader as weak or ineffective can issue a challenge, provided she does not do so during an active time of war. 

Rather than see small disputes escalate into unnecessary bloodshed, Garou have a number of customs through which they resolve issues of various types. When tempers flare, two Garou may lock eyes in a brief battle of wills called a _Staredown_ . Alternately, serious issues may require a moreelaborate contest, such as a trial by _gamecraft_ or even a _duel_ . 

With the exception of Staredowns, most challenges are handled in formal and ritualized fashions, overseen by the Master of the Challenge (see **Chapter Eleven: Garou Nation, Sept Leaders, Master of the Challenge** page 518). Challenges require both parties risk at least one trait of Renown (see **Renown** , page 417). If a challenge is declined, or the results are nullified or declared a draw, no Renown is forfeit. 

Challenges between werewolves hold such gravity that most septs designate a specific area at which they occur, ranging from a special arena to a humble piece of dirt, called the _challenge mound_ . For a challenge to officially occur, the Master of the Challenge must approve it and oversee it. 

While challenges are intended to calm raging tempers and prevent the loss of life, these confrontations sometimes result in serious injury or even death, should participants get out of control. Due to the inherent danger found in many challenges, kinfolk are not allowed to take to the mound on their own behalves, even if they believe they could win the day. Of course, this restriction does not preclude a Garou from issuing a challenge on behalf of a kinfolk, although 

**
## **Fera and Challenges** 

While some Fera feel that Garou are too filled with rage and too quick to issue a challenge, every breed recognizes the benefit of a structured challenge system. Any Fera who spends a significant amount of time among Garou quickly learns the intricacies behind challenges, as she may be challenged by a werewolf at any time. When a Fera finds herself at a Garoucontrolled sept, she agrees to be bound by its laws, and this agreement opens her up to the possibility of issuing or accepting challenges. 

Many Fera breeds have their own challenge traditions, though these tend to be used internally. For example, among the Beast Courts of the East, the Hengeyokai prefer challenges that require wit and intelligence, rather than brute strength. Their challenges often mirror gamecraft challenges, ranging from chess tournaments to riddling contests. When a physical challenge is necessary, it often focuses on fighting smarter, not harder. 

**
Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

many werewolves view this act as a claim of ownership over the kin in question. Although challenges are widely used throughout the Garou Nation, they are more prevalent among certain tribes. Get of Fenris and Fianna are both well-known for hosting regular challenges, training to ensure their members are successful on the mound. 

## **Rank Challenges** 

Rank challenges are considered separate from the established challenge structure, and are detailed under **Rank: Rank Challenges** , page 412. 

## Staredowns 

## Challenges for Position 

As the Litany states, a leader may be challenged at any time during peace. Regardless whether a challenge takes the form of gamecraft or a duel, a challenger may demand that a challenged party cede her office as a condition of winning. If she wishes to uphold this tenant of the Litany, the challenged party must always acknowledge and agree to the requested condition, though she is certainly free to argue about the nature and specifics of the challenge. As the challenge still requires her consent, this restriction often puts a leader in a better position to negotiate conditions advantageous to her. Additionally, positions tied to a specific auspice cannot be challenged by those of a different auspice. For example, a Ragabash cannot challenge to become the Truthcatcher, a position only held by Philodox. 

In cases where a werewolf is challenged for her position, she is not required to risk Renown as part of the contest. Any Garou may freely decline a challenge for position during a time of war (see **Chapter Eleven: Garou Nation, Septs, War and Leadership** , page 527). 

## Challenges Above Station 

Leadership carries with it some privileges, among which is the expectation that frivolous challenges do not deserve the same treatment as ones coming from seasoned wolves. Werewolves seeking to challenge others who hold higher Rank than they do are often seen as seeking to prove something. Especially bellicose Garou who disproportionately devote their efforts towards personal advancement and not the greater good of Gaia are looked at with a suspicious eye, and are often selected for special duties to help realign their efforts. 

If a Garou seeks to challenge another Garou who has more dots of the Rank background than she does, she must risk additional traits of Renown equal to their difference in Rank. If she does not have sufficient Renown, she cannot issue the challenge, and she must wait until she has earned sufficient traits of Renown to make the attempt. Such wolves who are denied challenges in these circumstances are considered to be acting “too big for their britches,” and they are often told to pipe down. If a werewolf is challenged by another of higher Rank, she only needs to risk a single trait of Renown. 

The simplest and most commonly encountered challenge is the _Staredown_ , and it is rare to find a Garou who has not participated in more than one. Rather than relying on sheer brawn, a Staredown takes the form of a contest of wills and spiritual strength. Much like wolves in the wild, this challenge depends on posturing to determine who will dominate the moment. These challenges are often brief and can occur anywhere, even on the field of battle. They do not require the Master of the Challenge to oversee them; they are handled quickly and decisively. Red Talons were the first to initiate this challenge as a means of handling confusing human disputes, using a language with which they were familiar. In time, the Staredown became a popular means of challenge both for its simplicity and relative safety. 

When two Garou find themselves at odds over a minor issue, one of them issues a challenge to settle the matter swiftly and decisively, rather than wasting time mincing words. A Staredown challenge is initiated when one Garou locks eyes with her opponent, issuing her challenge through body language and posture alone. Once the Staredown has been initiated, the challenged Garou must either accept or admit defeat by declining the unspoken terms. 

When a Staredown challenge is accepted by both parties, the two werewolves begin immediately, circling one another and posturing. This challenge can be visceral and feral, with opponents often taking on other forms in order to make themselves more intimidating. Flexing, growling, beating their chests, and staring at one another with cold determination are all methods employed in an attempt to beat one’s opponent. Eventually, the loser drops her eyes, taking on a submissive posture as she cedes to the victor. Any werewolf, Fera, or kinfolk who has spent time within the Garou Nation understands what this challenge entails, and they are able to immediately identify who has triumphed. 

While this type of challenge is not appropriate for all issues that arise among werewolves, Staredowns are commonly used for minor skirmishes that arise between two Garou. Verbal slights, such as questioning the ability of one Garou to speak on a particular topic or a personality clash over which course of action is best can result in a Staredown challenge. Other conflicts, such as a pack member questioning the ruling of her leader or a Ragabash overstepping a boundary with her pranks, may be resolved through use of this challenge. Staredowns 

Chapter NiNe: SoCial SyStemS 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

**
## **Staredowns and Real World Bleed** 

In any live-action game, players are encouraged to immerse themselves in their roles, fully acting out the behaviors and reactions of their characters. While this adds to the ambiance of a game and enhances the enjoyment of most, it is important for players to always be aware of their surroundings. The comfort level of fellow players should be taken into account, as well as the location of the game. For example, if you happen to hold your game in a public space on a college campus, you should be aware that overtly threatening behavior may alarm passersby. Likewise, if one of the players involved in the challenge expresses discomfort at the level of intimidation employed by this challenge, then the players should resolve to simply narrate the challenge, rather than acting out the aggressive behaviors found here. 

**
are never used to settle serious problems, such as a breach of the Litany or the levying of a physical threat, although they are often used as a first step to stop these behaviors before they spiral out of control. While these challenges are swiftly decided, they are often enacted in front of witnesses, in order to resolve any question of who is the stronger Garou. 

## Staredown Mechanics 

To initiate a Staredown, lock eyes with your opponent. You must have her Gaze and Focus to do so (see **Chapter Five: Gifts: Gaze and Focus** , page 214). Your opponent must either decline, in which case she loses, or she must accept your challenge. If she accepts your challenge, each party selects one of the following behaviors and makes the opposed challenge using the corresponding test pool: 

- **Killer Instinct:** By posturing, flexing, or perhaps beating your chest, you make an impressive show of your prowess and animal ferocity. Use your **Physical attribute + Animal Ken skill + Rank** as your test pool. 

- **Force of Personality:** By growling, raising your hackles, or shouting your opponent down, you show her who’s in charge. Use your **Social attribute + Leadership skill + Rank** as your test pool. 

- **Ironclad Will:** Demonstrating an unshakable composure and an icy stare, you send chills down your opponent’s spine. Use your **Mental attribute + Intimidation skill + Rank** as your test pool. 

In the event of a loss, the losing party may retest with Willpower, subject to the normal rules, or she may spend a trait of Glory for a retest (see **Renown: Glory** , page 418). 

Once the challenge is resolved, the loser must noticeably break eye contact with the winner, turning or lowering her head. She cannot challenge, attack, or aggress against the 

winner of a Staredown for the next 10 minutes, though she can converse with the winner normally. During this time, the loser is unable to meet fully meet the gaze of the winner, averting her eyes should the winner look her way. Because of this condition, the loser of the challenge cannot target the winner with any powers that require Gaze and Focus (see **Chapter Five: Gifts: Gaze and Focus** , page 214). 

Although a Garou who has lost a Staredown challenge is unable to look the winner in the eyes, that doesn’t mean that she must ignore her altogether. In fact, doing so may be seen as disrespectful, possibly failing to abide by the terms of the challenge. The loser can still focus on the winner and pay attention to her, but if the winner attempts to look her in the eye, she must look away in deference to the challenge. This effect ensures that, should a Staredown take place on the field of battle, the loser is still able to interact with the winner as they move to face their foes. As long as the winner has the loser’s attention, the winner can still use powers that require the loser’s Gaze and Focus. Any observer can determine who is the winner and loser of a Staredown challenge. A Garou who fails to acknowledge a loss during a Staredown in an honorable fashion is automatically assigned the moniker _Dishonorable_ . If there is doubt as to whether an action is considered honorable, the Storyteller adjudicates. 

## Example Staredown 

_Clyde, a Silver Fang Ragabash, is arguing with Brooke, a Red Talon Galliard. Their two packs have worked together to slay a nest of vampires, and they cannot agree who will take possession of a gilded chalice they’ve found. As they argue, Brooke has Clyde’s Gaze and Focus, so Brooke’s player announces she is initiating a Staredown. The player of Clyde accepts. Brooke chooses Force of Personality, and snarls at Clyde. In turn, Clyde chooses Ironclad Will, and meets Brooke’s gaze with determination. They test and tie. As Brooke has chosen Force of Personality, she adds her Social attribute with her Leadership skill, for a total of 17._ 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

_Clyde adds his Mental attribute plus his Intimidation skill, for a total of 15. Brooke wins the initial challenge. Clyde may either retest with Willpower or by spending a trait of Glory, if he has it. If Clyde does not retest, or if he chooses to but still ultimately loses the challenge, he must break eye contact with Brooke. For the next 10 minutes, he is unable to target her with any powers that require Gaze and Focus, though Brooke may still target him with such powers, if he is paying attention to her._ 

## Gamecraft 

Occasionally, werewolves encounter a conflict that demands a more complex challenge than a Staredown, but one that can be resolved through non-violent means. In these situations, a challenge of _gamecraft_ may be proposed, using the form of a structured game or contest with established rules. Stargazers initially developed gamecraft, and Glass Walkers, who favor intelligence and wits over a show of force, expanded this type of challenge further. Stargazers in particular prefer to avoid violence when possible and sought a means of resolving conflict without bloodshed. In the Age of Apocalypse, with the Garou population in decline, this type of challenge has become increasingly popular, allowing two werewolves to face one another without risking serious harm or death to the participants. Although there is less inherent danger in a gamecraft challenge, and it does not carry the same gravitas as a duel, these challenges are still taken seriously as a matter of honor. 

Gamecraft is also commonly used to settle bets or boasts between Garou, determining who is the fastest, strongest, or most intelligent werewolf of the bunch. It may also be utilized as a teaching tool with young Garou, in order to show them where their strengths and weaknesses lie in a safe and controlled manner. While this use of the gamecraft challenge is often playful and light-hearted, it is no less important to the function of Garou society, offering werewolves a way to compete against one another without the lethal pressures of a duel. 

Gamecraft is a challenge between two Garou, resolved through some sort of structured competition, typically via a game or contest with established rules. A variety of contests fall under the category of gamecraft, including everything from feats of strength between Fianna and Get of Fenris, to masterful games of chess between Silver Fangs and Shadow Lords, to a race to a location in the Umbra between Silent Striders and Red Talons. Unlike duels and Staredowns, gamecraft may include more than two participants. 

Gamecraft can also be used to resolve a wide variety of issues. The challenge at hand may be a simple matter of boasting between werewolves, each claiming to be the best at something. Alternatively, gamecraft may be chosen a means of shifting 

or evening the odds in a conflict, such as when a cunning Ragabash is challenged by a powerful Ahroun. In cases where the involved parties eschew violence or consider its casual application beneath them—such as with Stargazers, Children of Gaia, or some Theurges—gamecraft may be applied to settle conflicts that are very serious in nature, even to challenge a leader for her position. In all cases, the participants must put a measure of each of their reputations on the line, each of them risking a Renown trait of their choice, which is awarded to the winner. After the outcome is decided, the contest ends as the loser makes a gesture of submission towards the winner, appropriate to the dispute. This deference may take the form of a bared throat, a bow, or even a drink offered to the winner. 

## Gamecraft Mechanics 

For a gamecraft challenge to occur, both parties must agree to its terms. Typically, the challenged party is allowed to determine the nature of the contest, and the challenger must agree that the contest is  sufficient to settle her issue. Both parties then approach the Master of the Challenge, who oversees the contest. 

Then, each party must designate a trait of Fleeting Renown to risk; the winner receives all of the Fleeting Renown risked at the conclusion of the challenge. Normally, if a shapeshifter does not have any traits of Fleeting Renown, then she cannot participate in a gamecraft challenge. However, if the contest determines who will hold a sept position, the holder of the position risks the position instead of a trait of Fleeting Renown. The two parties may also have some additional condition for winning to which they must agree, such as the right to a piece of property or an agreedupon service the loser will perform. 

The Master of the Challenge serves as judge, determining the particulars of the contest. She has full power to disqualify or invalidate the outcome if the rules are not followed. If she feels the nature of the challenge demands it, she may also designate additional parties to assist her in judgement. 

After the contest is over, the Master of the Challenge declares a winner. The winner receives all wagered traits of Fleeting Renown, and if a sept position was won as part of the contest, the victor assumes the new role immediately. 

## Types of Gamecraft 

A variety of established forms of gamecraft already exist, though in the 21[st] century, it is not uncommon for new forms to spring up all the time. The Storyteller should consider the following mechanics as guidelines, and should feel free to substitute different test pools for suggested contests or to create a more elaborate structure as necessary. 

Chapter NiNe: SoCial SyStemS 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

**
## **Gamecraft and Structured Mechanics** 

Due to the broad nature of gamecraft challenges, there are no fixed mechanics to resolve them. Typically, the Storyteller determines the mechanics to resolve these challenges, using a test pool of the appropriate attribute and skill. Rather than treat the contest as a single opposed test, the Storyteller may choose to add in additional structure to create suspense. For example, a race could be broken up into multiple challenges for different legs, or a fishing contest may involve participants winning a static test against a number drawn from a deck of playing cards. 

If all players involved agree, some contests may be resolved through roleplay, with the players acting out the challenge itself, such as playing a game of Go or participating in a live riddle contest. In these cases, keep safety and player boundaries in mind. 

As a part of roleplaying, players often create characters skilled at things they themselves are not. It is within any player’s rights to request that a challenge be settled through game mechanics. 

**
- **Athletic Competitions:** A variety of sporting competitions and feats of strength fall into this category. Contestants use their Physical attribute + Athletics skill in this challenge. Competitions of this sort are enjoyed in various forms by a variety of tribes. A contest with Get of Fenris might resemble a strongman competition, while Bone Gnawers may enjoy a game of basketball. 

- **Board or Card Games:** This category includes a variety of classic games of skill and strategy, favored by enthusiasts of all ages. Contestants use their Mental attribute + Investigation skill, or in some cases, their Social attribute + Subterfuge skill. Silver Fangs, true to their Russian heritage, are known to prefer chess, while Glass Walkers may prefer poker. 

- **Combat Arts:** When a rivalry between two combatants is not serious enough for an actual fight, the two may agree to a gamecraft challenge instead. A variety of combat arts are viable as contests, such as target shooting and martial arts exhibitions. Contestants use their Physical attributes + their Melee, Brawl, or Firearms skills as appropriate. Children of Gaia may seek this method as a non-violent demonstration of skill, while Stargazers are known to compete with elaborate katas. 

- **Footrace/Exploration:** Any form of gamecraft that requires the contestants to get from point A to point B falls under this category, including races of any length or contests that send the contestants to a particular place within the Umbra. Contestants use their Physical attribute + Survival skill to compete. Wendigo are fond of endurance races in brutal conditions, while Silent Striders are notoriously difficult to beat at distance events. 

- **Hunting:** Often used to settle lesser disputes of physical superiority, this contest challenges the contestants to put their hunting skills to the test. This trial may involve hunting a particular quarry or judging the quality of a contestant’s bounty over a fixed period of time. Contestants use their Mental attribute + Survival skill. Black Furies often choose deer hunting in reverence to Artemis, while many complain the Red Talons possess an unfair advantage in this category. 

- **Influence Competition:** A new type of contest, possibly inspired by similar competitions on reality television, this challenge pits contenders against one another as they seek to adroitly influence the human population. This activity could be measured in terms of sales generated from a product or followers earned on social media. Contestants use their Social attribute + Streetwise or Leadership skills, but backgrounds likely also play a factor. Glass Walkers are fond of these sorts of contests, challenging their Cubs to start new businesses. Children of Gaia may compete to hold the largest charity event or outreach campaign. 

- **Litany Recital:** The recital of the Litany is a simple, yet challenging contest, often used to resolve disagreements regarding matters of procedure. Participants take turns reciting lines, and they cannot repeat a line that was already spoken. For this contest, it may be fun to let the participants actually attempt to recite the Litany, allowing them a static test of their Mental attribute + Lore skill against an increasingly higher difficulty to think of a line, if the players themselves are stuck. Shadow Lords sometimes choose this method to embarrass younger opponents, while losing a challenge of this sort is known to bring shame to any Silver Fang. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

- **Performances:** Disputes among performers are not uncommon, going beyond simple creative differences. When this conflict happens, they may resolve their difficulty through a competitive performance, often involving one or more judges. For this contest, participants use their Social attribute + Performance skill, as appropriate. Fianna take contests of this sort very seriously, influenced by their roots in the bardic tradition, while Bone Gnawers have been known to engage in the occasional dance-off. 

- **Riddle/Trivia Contests:** Encompassing traditional riddles, as well as variants such as 20 Questions, riddle contests are employed by thinkers looking to establish a pecking order. Participants use their Mental Attribute + their Lore, Academics, or Science skills, as appropriate. Thanks to a group of monastic Stargazers, koans are generally agreed to be inadmissible, while it is generally advised not to make a high wager when paired against an Uktena. 

## Example of Gamecraft 

_Nathanial “Sings in the Quad,” a Child of Gaia Galliard, has had more than enough criticism from Bryton, aka “the Hookup Ninja,” a Bone Gnawer Ragabash, as to the quality of his music. He makes plain his grievance with Bryton and challenges him to do better. Bryton agrees to the challenge and proposes a poetry slam. Nathanial accepts, and the two approach the Master of the Challenge. Nathanial is a Fostern and Bryton is a Cliath, but since Nathanial issued the challenge, Bryton doesn’t have to risk additional Renown. Nathanial risks a trait of Honor Renown, and Bryton risks a trait of Glory Renown. The Master of the Challenge agrees, and the contest begins._ 

_The Master of the Challenge, working with the Storyteller, declares that there will be three rounds. The Storyteller decides that each round will be decided by an opposed challenge of the participants’ Social attribute + Performance skill. The players test and tie. Nathanial has 16 traits, and Bryton has 14. The player of Nathanial wins the first challenge. The Storyteller, narrating the outcome, declares that Nathanial starts off with a masterful set of verse, which dazzles the audience. Bryton’s first attempt doesn’t quite impress the audience. For the second round, the players test again, and Bryton wins the second challenge. The Storyteller narrates that Nathaniel does well, but Bryton erupts with a flow of lyrics full of heat and intensity. For the third round, the players test a third time. They tie, and Nathanial still has a greater test pool, but Bryton spends a point of Willpower for a retest and wins. The Storyteller narrates that Bryton drops a verse hot enough to set the streets on fire, while Nathanial’s third attempt is not as strong. The Master of the Challenge declares that Bryton is the winner. Nathanial frowns as Bryton wins both measures of Renown, yet he still bows to the better performer._ 

_As consolation, Bryton hands Nathanial a copy of his mix tape, instructing him to be careful with it._ 

## Duels 

For conflicts that demand satisfaction through violence, werewolves accept _duels_ as resolution. While most of humanity has discarded dueling as an antiquated practice, it remains a matter of high honor among Garou, driven by their aggressive natures and feral instincts, and supported by the Litany. Traditionalists maintain that the practice is essential for weeding out ineffective leaders and that offering a system of structured combat reduces the urge to lash out fatally. Overall, werewolves broadly support the dueling system, and it remains the primary means of resolving serious conflict between Garou. 

What determines the nature of a serious conflict is largely up to an individual Garou. Most agree that for a duel to be earnestly considered, it must answer a notable offense, such as a grievous insult, an unprovoked assault, or a matter of leadership. Silver Fangs are known to obsess over their personal honor, with fixated ones seeking to address each individual slight, while Bone Gnawers are known to be more relaxed. Critics among the Children of Gaia claim that in the post-modern era, physical strength is a poor determinant of right and wrong, but even the most peaceful Child of Gaia may be moved to violence to address a matter of social justice. Black Furies, themselves motivated by social justice, are somewhat divided on the matter. Some view dueling as an outdated relic of the patriarchy, while others relish the opportunity to strike a literal blow for equality. 

Duels are always fought between only two werewolves, and they always end with a pre-established condition, such as the first to be wounded or when one opponent reaches unconsciousness. As any Garou who demands the death of her opponent would also deprive Gaia of a much-needed soldier, fights to the death are forbidden. Because of this taboo, a dueling Garou is not permitted to enter frenzy under any circumstance. In such cases, the Master of the Challenge intervenes and immediately end the conflict, declaring the frenzying Garou the loser. 

A duel may also incorporate special rules as to how it will be fought, to which its participants must agree, as well as the Master of the Challenge. These conditions may include restrictions regarding which forms the participants can assume or rules governing the use of weapons and fetishes. These agreements frequently vary according to tribal lines and may also reflect the severity of the dispute. 

Both parties must risk a trait of Fleeting Renown to participate in the duel, unless one risks a position. If agreed 

Chapter NiNe: SoCial SyStemS 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

upon in advance, the loser of a duel may face additional consequences, such as forfeiting claim to a piece of property or accepting a punishment, such as banishment. Duels are often used to resolve challenges for position, which may lead to celebrating the victor as a new leader. A participant can end a duel at any time by baring her throat, indicating her surrender. As all Garou are obliged to accept an honorable surrender, the Master of the Challenge immediately intervenes to end the fighting at such a gesture. 

## Dueling Mechanics 

To initiate a duel, the aggrieved party proclaims her grievance to the challenged party, dictating the extent of her issue. The challenged party may then accept or decline the challenge, subject to the rules above (see **Challenges: Challenges for Position** , page 427). If the challenge is not about a matter of position, the challenged party is always free to decline. If the challenged party accepts, she determines the nature of the contest, which the challenger must in turn accept or refuse. 

To continue, both parties must obtain the authorization of the Master of the Challenge, who must approve the details of the contest and designate a time and location for the duel to take place. Each participant must risk a trait of Fleeting Renown for the contest to proceed, subject to modification for a difference in Rank (see **Challenges: Challenges Above Station** , page 427). 

Duels use normal combat mechanics, though the Storyteller may wish to modify the rules to accommodate additional factors, such as environmental hazards. Once the contest’s outcome is determined, the winner receives any Fleeting Renown risked, as well as any position that was at stake. 

## Types of Duels 

Numerous forms of dueling have existed in different cultures throughout the ages. Septs, tribes, and even packs may prefer a style of dueling entirely their own. The following list provides a few examples; characters should feel free to modify the terms outlined, or to suggest new forms entirely their own. 

- **Armed Combat:** A favored type of duel among warriors, armed combat is a general category allowing the use of various melee weapons. Participants may each agree to use the same weapon, or they may each opt to use one with which they are skilled. A number of tribes have a preferred weapon and fighting style, such as the Children of Gaia with _Iskakku_ , a stafffighting style, or the Silent Striders with their mastery of the _Da’sai_ . 

- **Duels of Endurance:** Drawing roots from traditional Inuit contests, these duels are often chosen by Wendigo or Silent Striders. In this contest, participants fight in a circumstance where the environment is a threatening factor, such as extreme cold or heat, often with no protection from the elements. In these cases, the fighting itself is often limited to simple unarmed blows, wrestling, pulling on the opponent’s ear, or applying the cold flat of one’s knife to the other’s cheek. 

- **Feral Contest:** In this form of a duel, participants are restricted to either Lupus or Hispo form and are denied the use of weapons, armor, or fetishes. Combat is limited to claws, fangs, and any unarmed fighting style the participants prefer. Viewed by some as a means of settling a dispute among real wolves, Red Talons and other Lupus are fond of this type of fighting. 

- **Klaive Dueling:** Considered a high form of dueling, Klaive dueling is often used to settle disputes between rival leaders or royalty. In these instances, ownership or the use of a Klaive is a requirement to participate, and contestants are allowed to use Klaives in any form of their choosing. Particularly haughty Garou are known to demand this form of fighting if they suspect their opponents are unable to secure such a weapon, though a Master of the Challenge may approve the use of a borrowed Epic or Legendary weapon fetish as a substitute. Many Silver Fangs train at _Klaviskar_ , the art of Klaive dueling, from an early age, and several practice this form of dueling exclusively. 

- **Martial Arts Contests:** In cases when participants favor fighting styles with particular rules, they can apply them towards the duel itself. These contests are often sought by purists who view any deviation from a particular style as uncouth. While settling a dispute, Bone Gnawers may agree to the rules of boxing, or Uktena may limit their contest to a traditional wrestling style. 

- **No Holds Barred:** In this type of contest, the only rule is that there are no rules. Participants can use any weapon or fetish, as well as any form. Rules concerning frenzy and unconsciousness, however, are strictly enforced. Hardcore contests of this sort are famous among the Get of Fenris, known for their brazenness and tolerance for pain. 

- **Pistols at Dawn:** Once considered a relic from the Old West, the popularity of firearms among younger Garou has prompted a resurgence of this type of duel. Participants are each granted the use of a firearm, which need not necessarily be a pistol. Both parties typically agree to both the caliber of weapon as well as a specific 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

quantity of ammunition. Fighting is often limited to a confined space, such as a single street or abandoned building. Glass Walkers, fond of their customized weaponry, often choose this as a means of dueling. 

- **Unarmed Combat:** Considered a simple, if uninteresting, structure, this type of duel has contestants fight without the use of weapons or fetishes of any kind, selecting whichever form or forms they see fit during the contest. Optionally, this type of duel may prohibit the use of particular gifts, such as Mental or Social powers, in the interest of keeping the fight purely physical. The Stargazer martial art, _Kalindo_ , falls into this category, making this type duel a favorite of that tribe. 

## Example Duel 

_Augustus Crowe, an Athro Silver Fang Ahroun, seeks to challenge Ramses, an Adren Silent Strider Ahroun for his sept position of Warder. As it is not a time of war, Ramses must acknowledge the challenge. He is known for his skill with the Da’sai, and he chooses armed combat as the form of the duel. Augustus accepts, and the two agree the fight will carry on until one of them is unable to continue. The two go to the Master of the Challenge. As Augustus has 1 fewer dot of Rank than Ramses, he must risk an additional trait of Fleeting Renown to participate in the challenge. He risks 2 traits of Glory. As Ramses is being challenged for his position, he does not have to risk any traits of Fleeting Renown. The Master of the Challenge sets a time and place where the two will fight._ 

_The combat begins as both assume Crinos form. Ramses is an expert with the Da’sai, but Augustus has been training hard for this moment and carries with him a powerful Klaive. After an epic series of blows back and forth, Augustus succeeds at disarming Ramses. The fight continues as Ramses lunges with his claws, but Augustus manages to dodge and deeply wounds him. Ramses is unable to continue the fight, and bares his throat in submission. Augustus is declared the winner. He wins back the Renown he risked, and immediately assumes his duties as the sept’s new Warder._ 

## Moots 

- _“Stay firm your feet, but let fly your spirit. Take strength from your sisters and refuse to die, even in your heart.”_ 

— Arthura Valda, Acolyte of the Order of the Merciful Mother 

A _moot_ is a ritualized game or ceremony that werewolves play to establish their position within society. These games serve a variety of social, political, and religious functions within a sept. Moots can be formal affairs or casual encounters, large 

or small, lasting less than an hour or continuing for weeks on end. A Silver Fang coronation full of ritual significance held at the heart of a bawn is a moot, as is an informal encounter between two packs discussing tactics and territory over beers at the local pub. 

Garou are inherently social creatures, reliant on community for their sense of purpose and stability. Every moot represents a social encounter away from the battlefield that builds bonds and vents aggression. As Gaia’s enemies grow more numerous and powerful, her protectors feel more isolated and despondent. Regular congregation, formal or otherwise, is important for a werewolf’s psychological fitness in the face of overwhelming odds. Those who eschew moots tend to draw attention and distrust; they are likely candidates for Harano or enticement by the Wyrm. A Garou who only spends time in combat or looking for the next battlefield is a ticking time bomb in the eyes of her peers. 

No less essential to Garou society, the mystical aspects of a moot support the spiritual welfare of the sept and its protectorate. These aspects take on a variety of forms depending on the moot’s purposes, from highly formulaic ceremonies to informal celebrations. The rites performed at a sept’s moot are critical to nourish and ward its shard caern. In the 20[th] century, when Garou controlled hundreds of great caerns around the world, a formal monthly moot was needed to maintain each caern’s power. Potent rites were enacted and mystical energies were fed into the caern, empowering it for the coming lunar cycle. Under the watchful oversight of the caern’s totem spirit, these energies were divided into bolstering the integrity of moon bridges, nourishing local spirit life, and otherwise sustaining the caern’s mystical functions. Shard caerns require high levels of maintenance to cultivate and protect, so mystic moots are even more important at these delicate spiritual outposts. 

## Concolation and Grand Concolation 

Some moots call for attendance larger than even a gathering of multiple septs. When a tribe as a whole gathers its majority of members, they hold what is known as _Concolation_ . When the entire Garou Nation gathers, it is a _Grand Concolation_ . The decisions made at these events are always momentous: tribes change political directions or the Litany itself is reinterpreted. These moots allow the most political and passionate Garou to change the course of tribes or of the Garou Nation itself. 

**Theme:** Political 

**Mood:** Tense, Exciting, Dramatic 

**Setting:** Garou Nation 

Chapter NiNe: SoCial SyStemS 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

**
## **Storyteller Advice: Running a Moot** 

A moot is a meta-game within a game session that characters play to win acclaim, refresh spent Renown, and claim the victor’s laurels. Characters understand moots as a formalized system of social rules that must be followed, lest scandal fall on them. The nature of the rules and the identity of the ultimate winner are less important than providing a reason to motivate enemies and allies to interact and compete in civilized ways that vent tension. 

There are three common player reactions to a moot; Storytellers should plan for each of them: 

- **Mastery:** A player might seek to prove her worth to others by winning the contest and thus demonstrating her mastery of the game. 

- **Scandal:** A player might leverage the moot to attempt to wound one of her character’s enemies without starting a lethal conflict. 

- **Open Revolt:** Players opt to ignore the event, making choices through their lack of interaction. 

Moots are an excellent way to facilitate interaction between usually disparate cliques of characters or to add flavor to an otherwise normal game. They are particularly well-suited to games where the players are unfamiliar with one another, and the presence of visible, eyecatching props can invite inquiry from those who notice them. 

**
## History of Concolation 

The social order of the Garou is not democratic. It is a system in which the most honorable, the wisest, and the most powerful decide what is right and wrong. Nevertheless, there are some issues that must be resolved and decisions to be made that cannot stand on the shoulders of one werewolf. The directions of tribes and the nation itself cannot go forward based on the word of one. Even the High King had a council of advisors to guide the decisions he or she laid forth. The understanding of such a fundamental fact has led to the creation of moots known as Concolations and Grand Concolations. 

These two types of moots represent the gathering of tribes or the nation to determine the answers to questions that have an impact reaching beyond the bounds of pack or sept. The first Grand Concolation formed the Garou Nation itself, at which all the tribes came together and agreed to band together in the war against the Wyrm. Since then, many tribes have held Concolations and even Grand Concolations to create tribal alliances and political factions, and to choose war leaders, such as Golgol Fangs-First, to lead major offensives against the Wyrm. 

## Mechanics of the Game 

Concolations and Grand Concolations are intense meetings of high-level politics and machinations. Whether for personal gain or for the noblest ambitions, entering into this game is a test of all involved, player and pawn alike. 

Elders of a tribe may call for a Concolation, but it takes a Tribal Councilor to convene a Grand Concolation. The logistics of Grand Concolations are such that they very rarely occur more than once a year. Tribal Concolations are more common, but the gathering of so many werewolves in one place poses a security risk both to the septs they leave behind, as well as the risk of an attack on such a concentration of Gaia’s warriors. 

## **Stage One: The Preparation** 

Concolations and Grand Concolations are not taken lightly, and there are many things that must be done in order to prepare for one of these events. The following positions are necessary for a successful Concolation. 

- **The Host:** She calls for the Concolation or Grand Concolation. Having done so, it is her responsibility to host the event. She must announce it and offer the opportunity to submit items to the agenda. Once the submissions have been received, it is her privilege and 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

**
## **Proxies within Grand Concolations and Concolations** 

Obviously not all who wish to attend such events are able to do so. Doing so would jeopardize the security and safety of septs all over the world. In such instances, members with the right to attend the moot designate one they trust to act as their proxies. She who holds a proxy speaks with the voice and standing of each and every proxy entrusted to her, and more importantly, she is given the ability vote on behalf of that proxy. 

Such a responsibility is a double-edged sword, especially when holding the proxies of two who disagree. Garou are not known for their forgiving natures. 

**
responsibility to choose the topics to be discussed. She also appoints a Warden and Talesinger for the event. 

- **The Warden:** The Warden is in charge of security for the moot. It is her role, and that of her assistants, to ensure that only those who are allowed to be present are in attendance, and that any proxies they may carry are valid. After this task is complete, the Warden patrols to ensure the security of those present at the event. Her final responsibility is to oversee any challenges that occur during the moot and to prevent any fighting outside of a formal challenge. 

- **The Talesinger:** She acts as the voice of the Concolation. It is her responsibility to announce the agenda and to ensure that those Garou who wish to speak on a subject receive the opportunity to do so. She must also do her best to keep the emotional Garou on topic when heated debates could easily spiral into conflicts with teeth and claws. 

Unless the direst of circumstances occurs, once a Concolation begins, none may depart until it ends without giving up her right to participate, as well as the weight of any proxies she may carry. 

## **Stage Two: Announcing the Agenda** 

Once all attendees have arrived, been greeted, and their identities and proxies confirmed, there is one last thing to do before the agenda is announced and the moot officially begins. All present are required to swear an oath before each other and summoned spirits of honor and respect, affirming the validity of their identities and their right to be present. 

Once the oath has been sworn, the Talesinger announces the agenda for all present. She reads the complete list and opens the discussion for the first item. Assistants she has appointed beforehand work through the crowd to identify who wishes to speak on which items. At her discretion, 

either the youngest are given the chance to speak first, allowing those of highest Rank the last word, or the Elders speak first, ensuring that all those of great Rank who wish to speak have the time they need to do so. 

When each item has been addressed and those who wish to speak have had their chance to do so, the Talesinger calls a recess, allowing those who wish to do so to speak amongs themselves. This break is when the true politicking occurs. 

## **Stage Three: The Game of Politics** 

While the Warden and her assistants watch to ensure that heated discussions take place without devolving into blows, the attendees argue their different viewpoints using both raised shouts in the open and quiet whispers behind closed doors. 

Rank is an everpresent factor in Garou society, but Concolations and Grand Concolations provide an opportunity for Cliath and Fostern to make their voices heard. Groups may engage in public discussions, pushing their points of view and trying to convert those who disagree. Others may be more covert and hold private meetings where favors and promises are exchanged in return for pledges of support. Some may even seek to challenge their detractors and, with their prowess in the challenge, win the respect and support of their opponents. After all, whatever decisions are made at this event last until at least the next Concolation or Grand Concolation, and in a world at war, such events can be few and far between. 

While the Warden and her assistants do everything possible to ensure that discussions either stay civil or are contained by formal challenges, the sheer significance of the decisions that come forth at these moots can tempt those who believe that the ends justify the means. 

When the time for discussion ends, the Talesinger calls the moot to order once more. 

Chapter NiNe: SoCial SyStemS 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## **Stage Four: Ending the Moot** 

During this stage of the moot, the attendees’ votes and choices are made known. The Talesinger reads each item on the agenda once more, and she asks the Garou present to make their choices. The Talesinger can choose how to handle the voting process, but once a decision has been made and announced, it is final. The decision cannot be challenged, as it represents the will of an entire tribe or the Garou Nation as a whole. It stands until another similar gathering of tribe or nation reviews the decision once more. 

The final event of a Concolation is a celebration. The host declares an end to formal business and opens the territory for revelry and merrymaking. Due to the rarity of such large gatherings of Garou, other moot celebrations often happen after the formal business concludes, as Garou who rarely see each other in person rekindle friendships, tell war stories, and remind each other who their brothers- and sisters-inarms are fighting alongside. 

## Pack Tourney – The Contest of Champions 

The struggle between lupine instinct and cold logic fades as rivalry between packs heats to a fever pitch. Simple rivalries may gestate into bitter, potentially bloody conflicts that threaten to divide and weaken a sept. During the Age of Ruin, the Silver Fangs organized tournaments for packs to prove their mettle in battle, guided by the training methods of the elite cohorts of the Roman legions, the Silver Fangs. These tense competitions inspired humanity’s development of the concept of chivalry. 

The Pack Tourney is a physical contest between packs that determines dominance and selects a champion pack for a sept or a tribe. This moot offers an opportunity for battle to settle issues of dominance without the fear of death and under the guidance of an experienced Ahroun. The members of the winning pack earn the moniker Champion for a year and a day, or until they are defeated in another Pack Tourney. 

**Theme:** Chaos, Excitement 

**Mood:** Bitterness, Celebration 

**Setting:** Garou Nation 

## History of the Pack Tourney 

A champion’s purpose is to fight on behalf her sept on the most dangerous missions, but also to inspire those seeking hope in the war against the Wyrm. A proper hero encourages others to become heroes in their own right. The Pack Tourney is a community rally, a sporting event, and a forge for future legends. 

This event involves the entire community, from the Sept Leader to the extended family of children and kinfolk. A proper Pack Tourney involves a feast and celebratory dance the night before, when Elders and important emissaries from other septs may bestow their favor on Youngblood packs, hoping to find new packs to tackle the difficult missions required to sustain the war effort of the Garou Nation. 

The Silver Fangs claim to have inspired legends of the Knights of the Round Table and humanity’s concept of chivalry through the invention of this moot. A number of tribes practice a stylistic variation of this moot; Black Furies engage in spectacular blindfolded archery duels, Glass Walkers battle in urban paramilitary scenarios designed to test skill and tactics, and Silent Striders endure deadly marathons to deliver a sacred message. 

Perhaps most importantly, the Pack Tourney provides a chance for the Wyrmfoe to test the battle readiness of the sept and blood its inexperienced warriors before a real battle happens. 

## Mechanics of the Game 

The Pack Tourney is a game of physical endurance and cunning tactics. The moot has four stages. 

## **Stage One: Invoking the Spirits** 

The Talesinger announces the moot, expends a trait of Glory Renown to empower it, and asks the Master of the Rite or her appointed Theurge representative to call forth the spirits to bless and witness it. She may elect to empower another Galliard to perform this task, in which case that Galliard may temporarily claim the title of Talesinger for the duration of this moot. For more details, see **Renown** , page 417. 

During this invoking, the Talesinger anoints the Wyrmfoe, or her Ahroun representative, as the judge of the Pack Tourney. 

## **Stage Two: Reciting the Rules** 

The judge recites the rules for the Pack Tourney on the field of the contest. By tradition, the following terms are defined by the judge: 

- **The Area of Engagement:** The boundaries of the tournament may be as constrained as a field or meadow, or as large as a range of mountains, an entire forest, or an expanse of desert. Packs that leave the area of engagement are considered to have forfeited. 

- **The Terms of the Tourney:** The basic rules of the tournament include the specific victory condition required to win. A judge may create a complex scenario 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

that lasts hours or a simple test that determines the winner quickly. The only requirement is that one pack must demonstrate their skill, might, and cunning in battle. Common terms include the following: 

- › **The Melee:** This tourney is a simple brawl of fist, claws, and teeth within the area of engagement. The pack with the most members left standing wins. 

- › **The Quest:** This tourney is a test of the heart and the body, where the packs compete to be the first to complete an arduous challenge, such as returning with an important fetish lost to the Wyrm or hunting down and defeating a deadly beast, such a Thunderwyrm. The judge traditionally consults with the Sept Leader before this sort of quest is assigned to ensure that the entire sept benefits from the heroism of the packs. 

- › **King of the Mountain:** This tourney is a contest to see which pack can claim a strategic location within the area of engagement and hold it against the other packs and minions of the Wyrm. 

The rules and protocols for the Pack Tourney are as follows: 

- The judge evaluates the worthiness of each pack petitioning to join the contest of champions. She may reject any pack, for any reason, but it is considered a grievous insult to an established pack. In addition, the judge may limit the number of participants from each pack, so long as the ruling is applied equally, to ensure that the moot is exciting and relatively fair. 

- The Pack Tourney is a game of brutal violence and savage acumen. The judge actively encourages the use of weapons, gifts, and powers. However, the use of Klaives and Grand Klaives against fellow Garou is strictly forbidden. If there are Wyrm-affiliated creatures inside of the area of engagement, then naturally such honorable weapons may be used to dispatch the enemies of the Garou Nation. 

- Garou in the contest shall give no quarter, short of killing opponents. Many participants receive horrific wounds and even battle scars. Death occasionally happens, but Garou are expected to stop once their rivals have shown their throats or been knocked unconscious. 

## **Stage Three: Playing the Game** 

A Pack Tourney is often the premiere event of the social season for a sept. It provides a chance for Youngblood packs 

to forge their reputations in the eye of the community. A Garou who demonstrates bravery and honor here will hear their deeds sung for many years, whereas a coward shall not be trusted on real missions for the sept and may also be memorialized as a yellow-bellied weakling. 

Once the judge is ready, she howls for the gathering’s attention and asks for the packs to announce themselves. Each pack steps forward, and its anointed spokesperson, typically its Galliard, introduces it and proclaims its valor. Such activity isn’t considered shameful bragging, but an — opportunity to rally the crowd a hero’s purpose isn’t merely to battle the Wyrm, but inspire others to see that possibility within themselves. 

The judge and her assistants lead the packs to their marks and, when ready, howl together to begin the Pack Tourney. The packs scramble and battle as required to achieve the victory condition. Some terms of the tournament may take hours to complete, away from the crowd. In such circumstances, the judge has monitors keeping watch over the packs and relaying information as needed. 

## **Stage Four: Ending the Moot** 

Once the judge has determined that one of the packs has achieved the victory condition, she howls. All participants and activities immediately cease. If there has been a death or if there is an accusation of foul play, the judge examines the wounded and deceased to verify whether victory has been achieved honorably. 

Then, the judge asks the victors to step forward. She anoints each participant from the winning pack with the moniker Champion of the sept. For more information, see **Monikers: Champion** , page 425. 

## The Great Hunt – A Tumultuous Fray 

At Midsummer, the blood of Garou burns hot and their rage boils beneath the surface, stoked by the influence of Helios. When the sun smolders in sweltering blue skies, werewolves feel the call to answer it with mayhem and bloodshed, revealing themselves to unforgiving rays of Helios as the beasts they are and will always be. To quench this thirst for havoc, the Garou call the Great Hunt—a game where they can sink their claws in their greatest enemies and honor Gaia by shedding blood in her name. 

**Theme:** Predatory Hunger 

**Mood:** Primal, Dangerous, Chaotic 

**Setting:** Garou Nation 

Chapter NiNe: SoCial SyStemS 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## History of The Great Hunt 

The Great Hunt is a tradition as old as the Garou Nation, an ancient method of both funneling the rage of the Garou during the hottest part of summer and honoring Gaia by acting, without restraint, as her warriors. When Helios is at his hottest, the enemies of Gaia are unable to hide, revealed by a magic that illuminates even the stealthiest of foes. 

The hunt is savage, and the only prevailing law is that of tooth and claw. The hunger for violence overwhelms even the most peaceful of Garou when the days grow long, and the only satisfaction is found in bloodshed. The quarry chosen is always the strongest enemy that can be found, and sometimes Garou select prey who was once one of their own who strayed too far from Gaia’s guidance. Often, a remorseful Garou will be chosen, allowing her shame to be cleansed with her death, a final sacrifice to her comrades and former allies. 

Participants in the Great Hunt seek to quell the beast within and dampen their bloodlust. Those responsible for felling the target of the hunt are considered its champions. As their success is considered a great victory for Gaia and a blessing for the nation, songs of their act are passed from caern to caern. The victors receive the respect of their fellow Garou and find it easy to seek hospitality and celebration of their deeds in most places they travel. Failure to kill the prey is an ill omen for all, and those who do not succeed in the Great Hunt become fodder for gossip, subjected to disdain. 

## Mechanics of the Game 

Most established septs understand the need for a Great Hunt. It honors Gaia while allowing her children to vent the rage that boils beneath their skins when the days are long and tempers are short. 

The Great Hunt has three stages. 

## **Stage One: The Night Before** 

The Great Hunt begins at midnight of Midsummer’s Day. All werewolves are welcome to attend, even those who may be seeking to reconcile with the Garou Nation after falling out of favor. Success in the Great Hunt means smaller trespasses may be forgiven or overlooked. 

At midnight, the werewolf acting as the Hunting Horn begins to recite the names of creatures that are possible targets of the Great Hunt, listing their crimes and the dangers they bring to those participating in the Hunt. After each creature is listed, the Garou beat their chests, howling and shouting insults based on their experience with the foe. This fervor reaches its apex after the name of the last 

creature is declared, until the Garou are in a fevered state, howling and bragging of their imminent success. 

While they chant and brag, a deep basin is passed among those participating in the moot. To build their fervor and rage, the Garou cut open their hands and drain their blood into the basin. When everyone has contributed, the blood is used to paint symbols and glyphs of power onto the faces, chests, arms, and legs of those participating in the Hunt. With each symbol a Garou is anointed with, a blessing for strength, agility, fortitude, perception, or other useful skill is imparted to her, and the other Garou chant the werewolf’s deed name, exalting her before Gaia. 

Garou from the sept fill the following positions when they celebrate the Great Hunt: 

- **The Hunt Master:** Every Hunt must have its master, responsible for arranging the Great Hunt and researching possible enemies to be ravaged. In most cases, the previous year’s victors choose the Hunt Master for the next year. If there were no victors, Garou jockey and politic for the position until someone can claim and hold it. Success after a failed hunt is considered a great honor, so the political back-andforth involved in choosing a Hunt Master after the prior year’s failure is as mentally brutal as the Great Hunt itself when choosing a favorite. 

- _The Hunting Horn:_ The Hunting Horn is the werewolf responsible for announcing the names of the creatures who may be hunted and for listing their crimes and their abilities at the beginning of the Great Hunt. She is also responsible for naming the victors, for composing the tale of their victory at the end of the Great Hunt, and for chastising those who fail, forfeit, or dishonor the moot. The Hunting Horn is chosen by the Hunt Master. 

## **Stage Two: The Great Hunt Begins** 

At dawn on Midsummer, the Great Hunt begins. The collected Garou receive a sign—such as a vision or omen— regarding which creature they are to pursue. The hunt master calls all of the participating Garou to attention and directs them to relentlessly pursue the target, and to not give up until the target is dead and its blood has enriched Gaia. 

Any manner of pursuit is allowed, and some Glass Walkers have been just as successful at tracking a target as a seasoned Black Fury or Red Talon. Particularly talented kinfolk and Unborn have also participated in the Great Hunt, even if they reap little of its rewards, considering it a great distinction to be counted among the most accomplished of their kind. The Great Hunt itself is ferocious, and carnage is expected as the Garou pay little heed to anything but their 

Chapter NiNe: SoCial SyStemS 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

hunger for victory. They must corner and kill their target before Midsummer’s end. If they do not end the target’s life before midnight, they have failed and brought ill fortune on their entire sept. 

## **Stage Three: The End of the Great Hunt** 

The Great Hunt ends when the enemy is killed or at the stroke of midnight, when Midsummer ends. Whether or not the Great Hunt succeeds, the Garou come back together to speak their stories. 

If the characters fail to kill their target, they are considered shamed and are unable to gain Fleeting Renown for two months following Midsummer’s Day. Failing in the Great Hunt is considered extremely bad luck for Garou, foretelling a year of difficulties and losses. 

If the Great Hunt suceeds, the participants bear whatever remains are left of their target back to their original gathering point, singing and speaking of the acts that allowed them to win the battle against their foe. Each of the werewolves who took part in tracking, finding, and killing the target gains 1 Glory Fleeting Renown trait. The Hunting Horn announces their names, singing of the great victory they achieved over difficult odds, with the collected Garou howling in celebration. Afterward, while their brethren celebrate, those who were directly involved in killing the target gather to choose the next Hunt Master. Once a decision is made, the Hunting Horn calls out the name of the new Hunt Master, and gives her blessing for the next year of success for the gathered Garou. 

The hunter who landed the killing blow earns the moniker Worthy. For more information, see **Monikers: Worthy** , page 425. 

war against the Wyrm and a chance to mourn and express sorrow publicly. 

This event provides an opportunity to be seen as important by the sept, to charm others with your wit, and to subtly strike at your enemies. 

**Theme** : Festive 

**Mood:** Covetous, Exciting 

**Setting:** Garou Nation 

## History of the Game of Stories and Song 

Garou society has always revered its oral tradition. From the Age of Myth, history and knowledge have been passed from one to another by word of mouth. Save for the important glyphs and entries into the Silver Record, everything the Garou have known or learned has been taught to them through the words of those who have come before. The Liar’s Dance grew out of that oral tradition and combines history and teaching with the natural tendency of the young to brag of their deeds to receive recognition. Though the rage within Gaia’s warriors has sometimes caused this moot to become violent, it is uncommon. Far more likely, attendees appreciate a well-told lie or tall tale mixed with enough truth to be believable. Of course, some stories are all the more amazing for the veracity of their retelling. 

## Mechanics of the Game 

The Liar’s Dance is a game of bragging and storytelling. The moot has four stages. 

## **Stage One: Invoking the Spirits** 

## The Liar’s Dance – A Match of Stories and Songs 

Glory comes at the cost of blood and suffering for Gaia, but acclaim comes from Galliards who share tales of bravery and courage. The Liar’s Dance is a moot that provides a pressure release from the tedious nature of an unending 

The Talesinger announces the moot and expends a trait of Glory Renown to empower it. Then, she asks the Master of the Rite or her appointed Theurge representative to call forth the spirits to bless and witness it. She may also elect to empower another Galliard to perform this task, in which case that Galliard may temporarily claim the title of Talesinger for the duration of this moot. For more details, see **Renown** , page 417. 

**
## **Prop Alternatives: Beads or Poker Chips** 

The Liar’s Dance, Slap Dance Feast, and Flyting are historically played with brightly colored stones, but the average game might not have the resources to gather enough stones for the game. These games can also be played with colored beads, poker chips, ribbons, safety pins, or anything visual that fits an individual’s taste and style. 

**
Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## **Stage Two: Reciting the Rules and Passing Out the Stones** 

The Talesinger recites the rules of the moot while her assistants hand out stones in the following fashion: 

- Every Galliard is given a number of white stones equal to her Rank. 

- Every Philodox is given a number of blue stones equal to her Rank. 

- Every Ragabash is given a number of red stones equal to her Rank. 

The rules and protocols for The Liar’s Dance are simple: 

- Everyone must participate by either being a member of the audience or by performing for the sept. 

- All performances will be judged by the Galliards, Philodox, and Ragabash. They assign stones as per their auspice. 

## **Keeping Score** 

Points in The Liar’s Dance are achieved through the collection and exchange of stones throughout the course of the game. The color of a stone denotes a specific meaning and either benefit or penalty. 

- **White Stones:** These stones reflect support from a Galliard, indicating that a story or song was told well. 

- **Blue Stones:** These stones indicate support from a Philodox, indicating that the story or song represented a good deed according to the Litany. 

- **Red Stones:** These stones reflect scandal or insult from a Ragabash. Each red stone represents an accusation that your story is too obviously untrue or that you failed to earn respect for your deed. 

## **Stage Three: Playing the Game** 

Once the stones have been assigned, the moot turns into a raging party in which guests entertain each other with stories and songs. Larger moots often may split into smaller groups, so that the audience can literally vote with their feet to hear from those who are the most intriguing. 

Garou are not restricted to boasting about themselves; indeed, the subject of this performance can be any member of the sept. Some Garou will sing of glorious deeds, others will repeat humorous stories of misfortune and how their subject overcame it, and others will spread tales of scandal. 

Every story is different based on the one telling the tale. A Garou proverb states that no two Galliards will tell the same story the same way twice. Because many of these stories are the true history of ancestors, whose spirits could very well be invoked as witnesses, Galliards are careful to lavish their embellishment in a subtle manner, ensuring that a story is not spoiled by the audience’s disbelief. 

The purpose of this moot is twofold. It reinforces the community of Garou, reminding all who hear of the burdens they have borne, the struggles they have overcome, and the victories they have achieved together, which lessens the weight of Harano that drags down the souls of those present. It also serves as a valuable teaching tool. As such, the Liar’s Dance is frequently requested by Den Parents to help teach Cubs and Cliath of the history of the Garou as a whole. 

## **Stage Four: Ending the Moot** 

Once the Talesinger believes that the moot has run its course, she finishes the event with a story of her own. At this time, all participants feel a lightening of the spirit. If they are in any of the first three stages of Harano, their Harano stage reduces by one. 

Then, the Talesinger tallies the score and declares the following monikers: 

- Gallant: The character with the greatest number of white stones 

- Worthy: The character with the greatest number of blue stones 

- Reckless: The character with the greatest number of red stones 

In the event of a tie, the Talesinger is restricted to declaring only one victor or loser in each category. In this situation, the Talesinger may choose to honor her favorite performer from those who are tied to win in the blue or white category, and she may condemn her least favorite performer from those who are tied to lose in the red category. 

For more information, see **Monikers** , page 424. 

## Slap Dance Feast – A Boisterous Revelry 

The life of a Garou is marred by death and bloodshed, a continual war with no end in sight. Over the millennia, werewolves have been forced to find ways to combat the grip of Harano and find joy where they can. The Slap Dance Feast moot allows for the celebration and revelry of all the successes of the Garou Nation—big and small. Unlike some other official functions, the Slap Dance Feast welcomes 

Chapter NiNe: SoCial SyStemS 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

Garou, Fera, and kinfolk alike. All those allied with the nation are afforded the opportunity to revel in life. 

This moot offers Garou the chance to temporarily put aside the darkness in their lives and dance in the light. 

**Theme:** Celebratory 

**Mood:** Festive, Exciting, Relief 

**Setting:** Garou Nation 

## History of the Slap Dance Feast 

Violence and death are second nature to the turbulent Garou. The Impergium and the War of Rage were only the beginning of the fighting. Since time immemorial, werewolves have had to fight against the clutches of Harano, using whatever means necessary to maintain their motivation and continue their battle against the Wyrm. Moots have often been a means for this restoration, a way for Garou to come together as a community and share in their joy and sorrow. Once, these celebrations were minor—feasts held in honor of the birth of a blessed child or a successful Rite of Passage—but as time passed, the violence faced by the Garou Nation swelled, culminating in the decimation of caerns during the advent of the Age of Apocalypse. 

The Slap Dance Feast is a very old moot that fell out of regular use centuries ago, one based loosely on the Bavarian and Tyrolian Schuhplattler dance style. In the desperate times of the Age of Apocalypse, Galliards have revived this ancient tradition in hopes of bringing some measure of serenity to their fellow Garou. While other moots focus on sharing tales of valiant deeds, the Slap Dance Feast is a wild revel, first and foremost. It is a time for Garou to come together as one family to celebrate Gaia and all they have achieved, expressing themselves through song or dance. The Slap Dance Feast is not a time for mourning or violent actions, but an opportunity for Garou to shed the weight of their past actions. This revel is most commonly held when one or more members of a sept are deep in the throes of Harano. 

## Mechanics of the Game 

The Slap Dance Feast is a game of revelry and celebration, and an opportunity for Garou to rid themselves of the shackles of Harano. The moot has four stages. 

## **Stage One: Invoking the Spirits** 

The Talesinger announces the moot, expends a trait of Wisdom Renown to empower it, and asks the Master of 

the Rite or her appointed Theurge representative to call forth the spirits to bless and witness it. The Talesinger may elect to empower another Galliard to perform this task, in which case that Galliard may temporarily claim the title of Talesinger for the duration of this moot. For more details, see **Renown** , page 417. 

As the Talesinger invokes the spirits to attend the moot, all participants can feel their heavy hearts lightening. Any Garou who are in stage 4 or 5 of Harano find themselves in a state of relief as the sorrow lifts from their shoulders. This relief allows them to fully participate in the moot and purge themselves of their sorrow. 

## **Stage Two: Reciting the Rules and Passing Out** 

## **the Stones** 

The Talesinger recites the rules of the moot while her assistants hand out colored stones in the following fashion. While the stones are handed out, the moot’s participants must elect judges for the event. This choice can be made by raising hands or shouting, or the roles can go by default to the eldest Ahroun, Ragabash, and Galliard. 

- Every participant in the moot receives a white stone, a blue stone, and a red stone. 

- Each judge receives a single black stone. 

- The rules and protocols for a Slap Dance Feast are simple: 

- Everyone must participate in the revelry. 

- The judges maintain order and declare the final victors. 

- Violence against any allies is strictly forbidden. 

- This is the time to focus on the bright things in life, rather than dwelling in the dark. 

- Participants must award all of their stones to other participants. 

- Only the judges determine who will hold the position of the stagnant. 

## **Keeping Score** 

Scoring in the Slap Dance Feast is achieved through the collection of these colored stones during the course of the game. The color of the stone denotes a specific meaning. 

- **White Stones:** When these stones are handed to a participant, it indicates that the giver feels the recipient fully embraces empathy and compassion. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

- **Blue Stones:** When these stones are handed to a participant, it indicates that the giver recognizes that the recipient is filled with pure energy and creative urges. 

- **Red Stones:** When these stones are handed to a participant, it indicates that the giver believes that the recipient has fully shed her past pain and trauma, and that she embraces her new beginnings. 

- **Black Stones:** These stones are only handed out by judges, and they must all be given to the same participant. These stones reflect that the recipient holding them did not embrace the Slap Dance Feast, and that she is stuck mourning in the darkness. These stones are optional, and judges may, by majority vote, choose not to select anyone to receive them. 

## **Stage Three: Playing the Game** 

Once the stones have been distributed and the judges elected, the moot begins in earnest. More than just a wild party, the Slap Dance Feast is a time to put aside all of the horror that the participants have experienced and enjoy their lives to the fullest for a brief period of time. The best Slap Dance Feasts are true bacchanals, filled with food, drink, and music. They are often held at night around a roaring bonfire, the flames of which serve to cleanse the souls of the participants. 

At a Slap Dance Feast, everyone is expected to participate, lest they be named the Stagnant of the moot. Participation varies from person to person, though it often takes the form of some kind of performance, be it a lively dance around the fire, a stirring tale of valor, or a playful song. 

All participants in the moot are expected to give their stones to another, and it is considered bad form to attempt to keep any stones. Those who refuse to hand out their stones may find themselves in possession of a handful of black stones and named the Stagnant of the evening.
