- Members must provide aid to other members when called to do so, provided that the aid requested lies within their ability to provide and is in accord with their duties to Gaia. 

- Members must cooperate when possible, even when it might prove inconvenient at that time. 

- The organizational style of each of the member breeds and tribes must remain intact. 

- The spirit pacts of all races have to be respected, even when they are foreign. 

- Caerns and other holy places must be open for use by all Ahadi members, provided the use is reasonable and in accord with Gaia’s interests. 

- Members must protect holy places at all cost, as they are the only way to win the war against the Wyrm. 

Chapter ten: Storytelling 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## **Setting Style Document Example: Ahadi (Blended Garou and Fera) Setting** Rage Across Lagos: Dark Tides 

**Theme:** Chaos, Rage 

**Mood:** Desperate, Exciting 

**Scope:** Lagos, Nigeria 

**Setting:** Ahadi 

## **Chronicle Teaser:** 

The port city of Lagos is the largest in Nigeria and the entire continent of Africa. In the last decade, it has become the Silicon Valley of Africa, becoming a world leader in information technology. During the height of Black Tooth’s reign, Lagos was home to numerous vampires and minions of the Wyrm who used the city as a staging ground for the assistance they offered the Simba overlord. Since the destruction of Black Tooth, his former minions have become bolder, attacking where they please without anyone to reign them in. 

With the assistance of the Glass Walkers, the Ahadi in Africa have cultivated one of the last caerns in the world, forging a small urban sept in the heart of the city. It is here that they stand together against the waves of the Wyrm. In the power vacuum left after the death of Black Tooth, numerous vampires and Wyrm creatures alike have stepped forward in hopes of taking control of the thriving underworld. 

The Caern of Last Stand is large, one of several in Africa that were protected by the Ahadi when umbral storms raged across the heavens. All breeds and tribes are welcome here, so long as they adhere to the edicts of the Ahadi. There are even rumors that the fierce Rokea patrol 

the oceans in hopes of putting a stop to the influence of the Wyrm. 

Survival in Lagos is not easy, and the Ahadi must remain vigilant in order to maintain their position within the metropolis. The Caern of Last Stand is one of the few remaining great caerns in the world, and it must persist at all costs. 

## **Graduated XP Cap:** Yes 

## **Tribal Rarity Adjustments:** 

- **Common Tribes:** Ahadi setting default 

- **Uncommon Tribes:** Rokea 

- **Rare Tribes:** Ahadi setting default 

## **Setting-Specific Mechanics:** 

- Influence: Dots of the Influence: Underworld background are limited to 3 or fewer at character creation 

- Initial XP is 40, representing mid-level characters 

## **Storyteller Notes:** 

- This chronicle focuses on the aftermath of the Endless Storm and the relationships of the shapeshifters who belong to the Ahadi. Although tensions remain between certain breeds and tribes, all those who swear allegiance to the Ahadi are willing to put this aside while they fight for the greater good. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## Organization of the Ahadi 

The Ahadi is ruled by a central council of Elders made up of a representative from each of the primary member groups. In addition, there is a councilor that represents each continent on which the Ahadi maintain a presence. These Elders often focus on issues relevant to their breeds, tribes, and locations; however, they all participate in large council meetings to discuss matters that affect the whole of the Ahadi. Kisasi has maintained her position of power, and she speaks for the Ajaba on the council. 

## Ahadi Setting Rarity Adjustments: Tribe and Breed 

The most common breeds and tribes found within the Ahadi setting are the Ajaba, Bastet, Corax, Nuwisha, and Ratkin, along with the tribes that belong to the Concordat of Stars. The tribe and breed rarity system is intended to reflect the population numbers within the Ahadi setting. 

Remember that rarity merits count against a character’s merit limit of 7 points. For example, if you purchase the Rare Character merit, you have spent 4 points, and can only buy 3 more points of merits for the same character. 

**Common Tribes and Breeds (available at no cost):** Members of the Concordat of Stars, Ajaba, Bastet, Bone Gnawers, Children of Gaia, Corax, Glass Walkers, Nuwisha, Ratkin, Red Talons, Shadow Lords, Silent Striders, Stargazers, and Uktena 

**Uncommon Tribes and Breeds (2 point merit):** Members of the Sanctum of Gaia, Black Furies, Fianna, Get of Fenris, Kitsune, Silver Fangs, and Wendigo 

**Rare Tribes and Breeds (4 point merit):** Ananasi, Gurahl, and Rokea 

## Crossover Settings: Antagonist Factions 

The themes and scope of **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Werewolf The Apocalypse** are traditionally defined by the Garou Nation setting (see **Chapter Eleven: The Garou Nation** , page 495) or a custom setting that might operate away from the baseline of the core setting, but is still recognizable as a **Werewolf The Apocalypse** setting, such as **Creating a Custom Setting: The Ahadi Setting** (see page 465). 

Storytellers wishing to push the envelope and extend the scope of their chronicles may decide to leverage optional antagonist factions rules to create a crossover setting, allowing non-traditional player-characters into the story. 

Games that utilize the antagonist factions rules are perfect for facilitating interactions between other **Mind’s Eye Theatre** books, such as **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Vampire The Masquerade** . Alternatively, a Storyteller may allow the possibility for players to portray creatures native to **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Werewolf The Apocalypse** that are traditional antagonists, such as Black Spiral Dancers, Abominations, or Skin Dancers (see **Chapter Fourteen: Allies and Antagonists** , page 611). 

To use the optional Antagonist Faction rules to create a custom setting for your chronicle, follow these steps: 

1. Define the default book and setting for the chronicle, such as **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Werewolf The Apocalypse** and the Garou Nation setting. The chronicle’s default book and setting provides the baseline rules—all setting rules modifications use the default setting as their starting point. 

2. Select the antagonist factions allowed in this crossover setting, such as vampires, Black Spiral Dancers, Abominations, or Skin Dancers. The following rules are used when creating player-characters from an antagonist faction: 

   - Players can create characters from an approved faction, from a different setting, using the normal setting rules for that character. For example, a vampire is created as a vampire using the vampire rules from **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Vampire The Masquerade** . Alternatively, a Black Spiral Dancer or Skin Dancer is made following the rules for NPCs with complete character sheets, as described in **Chapter Fourteen: Allies and Antagonists** , page 611. 

   - Characters from other settings or books must use your chronicle’s setting-specific rules. However, these characters can purchase native merits and flaws. For example, a vampire character uses all of the **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Werewolf The Apocalypse** combat mechanics, but she can purchase native merits and flaws from **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Vampire The Masquerade** . 

3. Note specific changes in the default settings to the chronicle as per normal. 

## Vampire Crossover Chronicles 

Storytellers wishing to undertake the ambitious task of integrating both vampires and werewolves into the same chronicle are free to do so. While the social structures of both vampires and werewolves make them naturally cautious, if not distrustful, of one another, there are some occasions where both groups may find themselves occupying the same area. 

Chapter ten: Storytelling 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## **Setting Style Document Example: Vampire Crossover Setting** Rage Across Chicago: the Sept of the Lost and Found 

**Theme:** Morality, Rage 

**Mood:** Brooding, Desperate 

**Scope:** Chicago, Illinois 

**Setting:** The Garou Nation 

## **Chronicle Teaser:** 

No other city in the world holds a grudge like Chicago. Between the dawn of the Age of Apocalypse and the death of vampire Prince Lodin, the bloody feud between the vampires and the Garou in this city has lasted almost three decades. 

The Sept of the Lost and Found, protected by the totem the Great Trash Heap, survived the chaos and emerged as one of the largest urban caerns in the United States. 

While the city itself is vast, there is little room at the top. The city’s Glass Walkers and Silver Fangs are constantly at odds with the Ventrue and Toreador for control of the city’s Elite, and the Underworld is divided between the Nosferatu, Brujah, and Bone Gnawers. Open violence is eschewed, as both sides are wary of an all-out conflict. The Garou are still working to rebuild and consolidate, and they are loathe to risk casualties. For their part, the vampires remember too well the slaughter that occurred during the end of the Council Wars. Instead, both sides are content to remain in their territories, picking off those among opposition who violate the unspoken truce. 

Meanwhile, the city itself faces a variety of challenges, both internal and external. The city’s financial woes have introduced a new vector for outside threats: the privatization of city services. In an effort to reduce costs, the city has already turned certain areas of its traffic enforcement over to private companies. Presently, a number of Pentexaffiliated corporations are positioning themselves 

to place bids to take operational control the city’s parks and water processing facilities. 

The struggle between Weaver and Wyld continues to play out. Under the direction of the city father spirit _Hizzhonor_ , the city pushes to expand its shoreline further into Lake Michigan, drawing the ire of _Mishigami_ , the ancient spirit of the lake. And rumors abound that the spirit that grew from the Great Chicago Fire was never truly extinguished. Too powerful to be destroyed, it was contained with the help of Uktena Bane Tenders, enabling the eventual end of the horrific blaze. Recently, a group of Black Spiral Dancers has been starting a series of fires throughout the city in hopes of empowering the captive spirit. Should it become powerful enough to escape, the city would be illequipped to combat a bane-powered inferno. 

## **Graduated XP Cap:** Yes 

**Rarity Adjustments:** Garou Nation setting defaults, Camarilla and Anarch vampire antagonist factions 

## **Setting-Specific Mechanics:** 

- Initial XP is 150, representing a major city in the **World of Darkness** 

- Antagonist Factions: Camarilla and Anarch vampire characters from **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Vampire The Masquerade** 

## **Storyteller Notes:** 

- Each group is assigned its own set of rooms within the game site, representing its territory. A third room is designated to represent common or neutral territory. One Narrator is assigned to each group at all times, overseeing player actions. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

The following sample scenarios provide examples of how a game could operate involving both groups. 

## Vampire Crossover Scenarios 

## **Working Arrangements** 

Times have changed, and this isn’t your grandfather’s Apocalypse. While no formal truce exists between vampires and werewolves as a whole, in this area, both have come to realize that neither is a threat to the other’s existence. Following the events of the onset of the Age of Apocalypse, which involved small groups of vampires providing aid to struggling caerns, similar arrangements continue to exist. In this setting, each side has established territories and open lines of communication. Fraternization is not uncommon and may take place in agreed-upon neutral locations. A formal arrangement may or may not exist as well, allowing for the escalation and resolution of disputes. While either party may accept the presence of the other in its territory, in no circumstance will a vampire be knowingly allowed into a caern, or a werewolf welcomed to an Elysium or Esbat. Both parties work together to keep the area secure and to address outside threats, and they are likely to answer a call for aid. 

Working together may take the form of shared information or resources, a battlefield alliance, or even fighting together in close quarters. Once the threat is eliminated, hostilities may or may not resume, leading to a Cold War or possibly a Working Arrangements scenario. 

## **Battle Royale** 

The gloves are off and the fangs are out as both sides fight it out to determine who controls a particular area. In this especially lethal scenario, both vampires and werewolves actively seek to remove one another, using tactics up to and including murder. While this scenario may work for a oneoff game, Storytellers wishing to have a longer chronicle need to establish complications that prevent direct assaults on each other’s territory. All parties involved have their own version of the Masquerade or Veil to protect, as human discovery would prove disastrous. The Storyteller also needs to provide motivations to prompt both sides into action, such as the opportunity to compete for resources and territory that could give a group an edge over its opposition. Conflicts between the two sides will be violent and fatal, as each is eager to remove enemy combatants. Should both parties suffer enough, or should success become too costly, this situation may change into a Cold War scenario. 

## **Cold War** 

In this scenario, both vampires and werewolves coexist under a tentative, possibly unspoken peace. Both groups are loathe to break it, either due to risk-aversion, concern about potential casualties, or for fear of drawing unwanted attention from outside parties. Following the Nights of Turmoil, vampires have become especially wary of attracting the attention of mortal hunters. The Garou, having suffered a great many casualties in the initial battles of the Apocalypse, seek to preserve their numbers to ensure future generations. Both groups tend to keep to their established territories, and open communication between the two groups is frowned upon. Those who flagrantly violate the understood rules of territory and conduct are punished by their own leaders, or if caught, quietly disposed of by the opposing group. Any cooperation requires an imminent threat to both parties, possibly leading to an Enemy of My Enemy scenario. 

## **Enemy of My Enemy** 

While there’s no love lost between vampires and werewolves, there exist greater threats in the **World of Darkness** . Should an outside and unwanted faction enter the picture, a temporary alliance may form to deal with the outside threat. Examples include: a newly developed hive of Black Spiral Dancers, encroachment from an enemy sect of vampires, or the arrival of Pentex. Cooperation between the two groups is difficult, as caution and distrust insert obstacles. 

## Vampire Discipline Interactions with Werewolves 

Use the following modifications to vampire disciplines when running a crossover game with both **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Werewolf The Apocalypse** and **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Vampire The Masquerade** . 

## **Animalism** 

- **Feral Whispers:** Vampires can use this power to communicate with Garou in Lupus form and wolf kinfolk. Garou are not required to respond favorably. 

- **Beckoning:** This power has no effect on Garou. It affects wolf kinfolk normally. 

- **Quell the Beast:** In additional to this power’s standard effects, a werewolf target loses 1 point of Rage. This effect cannot reduce the target to fewer than 0 points of Rage. Additionally, as long as this power is active, the target does not gain a point of Rage at the beginning of each round. This power cannot prevent a Garou from entering anger frenzy, but if the target is in fox frenzy, or Thrall of the Wyrm frenzy, this power causes the frenzy to end. For the purpose of this power, Garou do not have Beast traits. 

- **Subsume the Spirit:** This power has no effect on Garou. It can be used on wolf kinfolk normally; however, the user cannot make use of any gifts the host possesses. 

Chapter ten: Storytelling 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## **Auspex** 

- **Heightened Senses:** This power functions interchangeably with the power Wolf Senses. Vampires using the power Heightened Senses may attempt to pierce Blur of the Milky Eye or Coyote’s Mantle as if they possess Wolf Senses. 

- **Aura Perception:** Shapeshifters and kinfolk viewed with this power appear noticeably different, with the exception of Ananasi. However, vampires require either an appropriate Lore specialization to correctly identify targets as shapeshifter or kinfolk, or a firsthand observation that leaves little doubt as to the user’s true nature, such as reading the aura of a Garou in Crinos or Hispo form. A Fera or kinfolk’s type cannot be determined with this power. Ananasi appear to have auras identical to vampires. 

- **Psychic Projection:** This power does not permit vampires to enter the Umbra. Garou with Wolf Senses activated can perceive a vampire using Psychic Projection in the same way an observer with Heightened Senses could. 

## **Celerity** 

- **Celerity and Rage Rounds:** Celerity and Rage rounds occur at the same time. For example, vampires that act during Celerity round 1 act at the same time and using the same initiative order as werewolves during Rage round 1. 

## **Obfuscate** 

- **Obfuscate and Animals:** Garou have no innate ability to sense the presence of vampires using Obfuscate in the same way that animals do. Wolf kinfolk become generally agitated similarly to other animals. 

- **Obfuscate and Auspex:** The gift Wolf Senses functions interchangeably with the Auspex power Heightened Senses for the purpose of piercing Obfuscate powers. 

- **Vanish from the Mind’s Eye:** Garou characters attempting to pierce Obfuscate powers using Wolf Senses use their dots of the Rank background in place of levels of the Auspex discipline. For example, a Garou with 5 dots of Rank using Wolf Senses may attempt to perceive a character with 4 dots of Obfuscate. 

## **Obtenebration** 

- **Shadow Play:** Garou take damage from this power as normal, living creatures, suffering the full effects of suffocation. If the shadows are successfully attacked with a weapon enchanted by the gift Spirit Knife, they dissipate. Shadows have a defense test pool of 8. 

- **Shroud of Night:** Garou take damage from this power as normal, living creatures, suffering the full effects of suffocation. 

## **Serpentis** 

## **Dementation** 

- **Passion:** In addition to the standard effects of this power, a Garou whose emotions are heightened also gains 1 Seethe trait, and for the power’s duration, she treats her Rage as one level higher for the purpose of determining anger frenzy, cumulative with other penalties. A Garou whose emotions are dampened loses 1 Seethe trait, and she treats her Rage as one level lower than it actually is for the purpose of determining both Rage effect benefits and entering anger frenzy, to a minimum of 0 on the Rage track. For example, a Garou with 3 points of Rage whose emotions are dampened receives the benefits provided by level 2 on the Rage track. A Garou with 1 point of Rage receives no Rage effect benefits. 

## **Dominate** 

- **Possession:** This power has no effect on Garou. It can be used on human kinfolk normally; however, the power’s user is unable to make use of any gifts the host possesses. 

- **Form of the Cobra:** Garou afflicted with the this power’s poison take damage as a virility 30 toxin (see **Chapter Seven: Core Systems, Health and Damage, Damage Types, Drugs and Poison** , page 350). 

## **Temporis** 

- **Kiss of Lachesis:** Garou enjoy some resistance to this power. Each application of this power causes the target to suffer 2 points of damage that cannot be reduced or negated, but it can only increase or decrease the target’s age by one year per application. 

## **Vicissitude** 

- **Healing Vicissitude:** Garou are able to heal damage from Vicissitude normally. Once a Garou has returned to full health, she begins to heal any existing Vicissitude modifications. Each modification heals if it were 5 points of normal damage. These points have no impact on a Garou’s actual health levels or wound track. Rather, the modification is removed when 5 additional health levels are healed after the Garou is 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

otherwise at full health. This healing can come from any combination of sources, such as the Garou’s own regeneration, or gifts that heal health levels. The effects of a Vicissitude modification end after its 5 additional points of damage have been healed. If there are multiple modifications, the Garou may decide which one to heal first. Regeneration healing in this manner is involuntary; a Garou cannot suppress her regeneration to maintain a Vicissitude modification. 

## **Necromancy** 

- **The Shadowlands:** Powers that affect wraiths and ghosts, such as Torment and Bind Soul, do not affect Garou present in the Shadowlands. Powers that allow vampires to perceive across the Shroud reveal the presence of a Garou present in the Shadowlands, if she is visible there. 

- **Soul Stealing:** This power affects Garou normally. Garou who are present in the Shadowlands can interact with a target pulled into the Shadowlands. That target can only interact with characters that are also in the Shadowlands. She retains full access to her Gnosis and gifts, but her spirit does not have access to the Rage track. Meanwhile, her body’s points of Rage reduce to 1, and she continues to regenerate while her soul is absent. Her physical body is dormant until its spirit returns, and it cannot gain Rage. A victim of Soul Stealing always returns to her body at the end of one hour, provided it is still living, even if she is physically or mystically restrained. 

Wrath, she is able to assume a form that appears indistinguishable from Crinos form, and if she uses the power Shape of the Beast, she is able to assume a form that appears identical to Lupus or Hispo form. As long as the vampire is on Humanity and possess a Morality rating of 3 or more, or she is on the Path of the Feral Heart, Garou cannot detect Wyrm Taint on her. Otherwise, the power Sense the Balance detects Wyrm Taint on a vampire. 

## General FAQ 

Use the following clarifications when running a crossover game with both **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Werewolf The Apocalypse** and **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Vampire The Masquerade** . 

- Are werewolves considered living creatures for the purposes of vampire disciplines? 

   - › Werewolves are supernatural living creatures. Disciplines that specify that they only work on living creatures also work on werewolves. If a discipline applies differently to supernatural creatures, use those rules when the gift targets werewolves. 

- What happens when a werewolf drinks vampire vitae? 

   - › For complete rules, see **Chapter Fourteen: Allies and Antagonists, Other Denizens of the World of Darkness, Vampires, Vampire Blood** , page 681. 

## • **Necromantic Rituals** 

      - What happens when a vampire drinks werewolf blood? 

   - › **Smoking Mirror:** Shapeshifters and kinfolk viewed through this power appear noticeably different, with the exception of Ananasi. However, vampires require either an appropriate Lore specialization to correctly identify targets as shapeshifter or kinfolk, or a firsthand observation that leaves little doubt as to the user’s true nature, such as reading the aura of a Garou in Crinos or Hispo form. A Fera or kinfolk’s type cannot be determined with this power. Ananasi appear to have auras identical to vampires. 

   - › **Ritual of Xipe Totec:** Use of this ritual on a Garou in Homid form causes 4 levels of aggravated damage that cannot be reduced or negated. Though a Garou can heal this damage, and in turn regenerate her face, it always leaves a battle scar, regardless of the total damage the Garou has taken. 

- **Technique: Wolf’s Blood:** Vampires using this power appear indistinguishable from ordinary Garou when targeted by every possible gift that distinguishes type. If the vampire has the merit Shape of the Beast’s 

- › The potent blood of Garou is especially appealing to the undead. When a vampire (not an Ananasi) consumes a point of werewolf Blood, it provides the following effects: 

   - » She gains an additional Physical focus of her choice, which lasts for the next 24 hours. This effect is not cumulative with drinking additional Garou blood from other sources. 

   - » Each point of werewolf Blood consumed is mystically potent, doubling its effectiveness. A vampire can spend this point twice before the point of Blood is expended. Therefore, a vampire can hold twice as much Blood per point of werewolf blood consumed. Note that the amount of Blood per turn a vampire can spend does not increase, only her maximum Blood pool. This effect lasts for three nights, or until all the blood consumed has been utilized, whichever comes first. 

Chapter ten: Storytelling 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

- » For the next three nights, for each point of werewolf blood a vampire consumed, she treats her Beast traits as if they are 1 trait higher for the purpose of determining frenzy. If she consumed 3 points of werewolf blood, the vampire receives a +3 penalty to her Beast traits when determining frenzy, but not for determining Humanity loss, during the next three nights. 

      - » The Sabbat rite Blood Feast does not function on werewolves. 

- Do vampires have Wyrm Taint? 

   - › Vampires on the Path of Humanity appear to be in the first stage of Wyrm Taint, as are vampires on the Path of the Feral Heart. Vampires on an alternative Path of Enlightenment appear to have Wyrm Affinity, marking them as creatures of the Wyrm. 

**
## Creating the Story Bible for Your Chronicle 

_“There is no greater agony than bearing an untold story inside you.”_ — Maya Angelou 

Even if you’re gifted with a phenomenally good memory, it is impossible to keep every detail of every plot or NPC in your head at all times. For this reason, many Storytellers utilize a _story bible_ as a management tool. A story bible is a written depository for all of the information a Storyteller needs to know in order to write or run plots for your chronicle. It might seem like an excessive demand on your time to keep a written record of all your plots and scenarios when all you want to do is run excellent stories and entertain your players. However, when you are caught in the middle of a stressful scene, surrounded by clever players with many unexpected questions—any of which could take the story into uncharted territory—you will be glad to have a reference, especially if checking it gives you a moment or two to think before deciding on the best course of action. 

Your story bible retains facts and important information to ensure consistency in your game. Everything you create for the chronicle should go into the story bible: information about the setting, NPC descriptions and motivations, ideas for future plot arcs, and developing physical or political scenarios. Think of it as a combination diary and blueprint; it holds all the tiny pieces of information and inspiration you need to remember late at night during a tense scene when the players are demanding immediate answers to questions that are vital to the core of your mystery plot. It’s also the most valuable tool you can provide to the Storytellers who come after you. Transitioning between Storytellers becomes immeasurably less disruptive if all the chronicle’s important story facts are recorded. 

You can create a story bible in whatever form best suits your preferences and needs. Some Storytellers prefer an 

electronic file on their computers or mobile devices. Others crave the security and feel of a classic three-ring binder with pocket page dividers for each section. A few brave souls merely jot down notes in a trusty journal. 

If you have a team of Narrators, a story bible is absolutely vital—without it, you can’t be sure that your team’s plots and scenarios are cohesive and that they support the continuity of the chronicle. And you’ll want it handy for easy reference at story brainstorming sessions. 

Include the following items in a story bible: 

- **A chronology of your setting:** This list doesn’t have to be incredibly detailed, but you will want a timeline of important historical events, especially those that had any direct effect on the story you’re telling. 

- **Important NPCs:** Remember to include details such as physical descriptions, political leanings, and general personality traits. 

- **Recurring locales:** You’ll want to know how large the local sept is, or if a local unused shipping warehouse is owned by a Glass Walker who is attempting to use it to store illegal goods. Be sure that you detail the local Umbra, as this will be a vital part of your local chronicle. Add quirky details to these places to make them seem more lifelike, and if inspiration strikes, detail a few that might come in handy later. You might never use them, but your players will eventually surprise you, and you’ll need to pull the proverbial rabbit out of a hat. 

## Treat Your Locale Like an NPC 

Your story’s locale is as much a character in the tale as the fictitious individuals who populate it. Information about your locale gives it depth and texture in the minds of your players and encourages them to see and treat it as though it were real. (Let’s be clear: Chicago is a real place, but the Chicago of your chronicle isn’t. It’s a fictionalized version of 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

a real locale, twisted to match the **World of Darkness** , but it will seem more genuine to your players if you take a bit of time to research and detail it.) 

Think about the humans who live in the locale, going about their days and nights ignorant of the **World of Darkness** , yet knowing that they live in a sometimes dangerous and uncertain world. How do they live? What are their politics? How do they entertain themselves? What local customs or mannerisms make these people unique or special? 

Try to imagine what your locale’s institutions and bureaucracy might be like if they were infiltrated by the Wyrm, or owned by local Garou. Begin by obtaining some accurate information about your locale’s government and municipal services, and pass them through the Apocalypse filter. Libraries, bookstores, and online sources will yield a wealth of information on nearly any municipality, regardless of size, including population figures, government structures, and handy maps. Then choose some physical locations in the area to replicate in the game. 

For example, you might decide that the Golden Gate Park in San Francisco would fit perfectly as an urban sept. Playing there isn’t practical, but you could research pictures on the internet and showcase what it feels like to be there. 

If you have a cooperative troupe, you might consider a city planning session with a map, where players suggest and submit items and locations of interest from the locale. This crowd-sourcing invites players to invest emotionally in the chronicle and create stronger ties to the mythos of the chronicle. A player might be curious if humans in an unknown building in downtown are suddenly harassed by the Skin Dancers. The player’s immersion into your chronicle will dramatically increase if said building is a known landmark that she has personally visited or researched. 

## Developing and Running Plots for Werewolf: The Apocalypse 

_“Plot, rules, nor even poetry, are not half so great beauties in tragedy or comedy as a just imitation of nature, of character, of the passions and their operations in diversified situations.” — Horace Walpole_ 

A _plot_ is a series of connected scenarios that forms a story in the minds of the players. Storyteller plots initiate through the scenarios you provide, but your players also make a substantial contribution to the plot as they enact their characters’ efforts to achieve their daily and longterm goals. Where do plots come from? Many good plot ideas start as seeds planted in your imagination, which 

germinate when you learn to tap your own creativity. Television, movies, magazines, novels, and newspapers all provide potential plot ideas. 

The hardest lesson a Storyteller must learn is that plot happens in the mind of the player while she is experiencing the chronicle through the filter of her character. Rookie Storytellers sometimes fall in love with the cleverness of their own complex plots. These plots usually feature prominent NPCs who make the hard choices while the players merely watch, becoming spectators in their own chronicle. It is easy to become lost controlling the infinite story universe. We have the ability to do literally whatever we want, and yet sometimes, the simple truth escapes us—the story’s plot happens to the characters. Every NPC, every scenario, and every scene must further the story of player characters. 

The trick is learning to recognize the useful ideas and create stories from them. It isn’t easy at first, and sometimes it’s harder than you’d like, but it’s something you must teach yourself to do if you want to be a successful Storyteller. 

Planning plots effectively means knowing your players and the subjects that specifically hold their interests. You might be fascinated by papal politics of the 15[th] century, but unless you can find a bridge to make it interesting to your players, you may end up with blank looks and players ignoring your plots. If this happens, you should consider if you are running the wrong type of plots. 

Players have widely different attention spans and need for stimulation. You will be able to amuse some players easily with little more than the story equivalent of a ball of string. Such players are the ideal recipients of long-term plot threads and subplots, because they will dog them for long periods of time with remarkable determination and will spread their enthusiasm to others. 

Other players with shorter attention spans require a new ball of string every game session, lest they grow bored very quickly. For these players, any plot that endures beyond one or two game sessions becomes a hopeless, confusing tangle of unwanted detail. 

Some players focus on immediate social or political concerns and won’t want to deal with other types of interactions. Avoid pulling these players out of intense personal scenes with plot. There is always time to trigger a story. Wait until the tempo of the game slows before springing a dramatic turn. 

You will want to offer alternate plot tracks to satisfy the needs of all of your players. Keep track of the different types of plots and how they satisfy various types of players. 

Chapter ten: Storytelling 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

**
## **Archetypes and Designing Plots** 

Storytellers aren’t omniscient mind readers, so it can be difficult for new Storytellers to evaluate what sort of plots might work best for the players in her chronicle. An easy way to take the pulse of players is to review their character sheets and make a list of the most common Archetypes in the chronicle. An Archetype not only determines how a player will roleplay a character, but is also a signal to the Storyteller for what type of stories she is interested in exploring. A Bully Archetype is going to want to fight her way through a scenario. A Trickster Archetype is going to want to use cunning and guile to resolve problems. 

**
Warning: Some plots will take your chronicle into unpredictable, strange places. Plots can be become quite complicated, especially when characters’ individual schemes clash. Confusion of this sort is both a help and a hindrance. Confusion lures and attracts those players who like to be involved in as many plots as possible. Other players react badly to confusion, and they will cease to participate when a plot gets too complex for them to unravel. 

If you feel overwhelmed, take a step back, consider all of the options, and then make the best decision you can with the available information. 

## Plot Archetypes 

Remember, plots need not be tortuously complex to pique the interest of your players; the best plots often are those that simply provide a frame to which you and your players can attach more complex ideas as the story proceeds. 

Consider these basic plot summaries and where they could lead your stories: 

- **Seeking the Throne:** A new arrival to the sept seeks to seize power through a show of force, issuing a challenge to the current leader. All other characters must decide how to best deal with this attempted coup. Does this new potential leader help or harm their goals? Will she be able to adequately lead the sept? Do the characters ally with her or remain loyal to their current leader? 

- **Invasion:** The city is under siege from a new threat that has placed them in dire peril. The situation looks bleak for the characters in this sort of tale; they begin the story deep in the hole, and things only get worse from there. The story could revolve around a sept besieged by the threat of the Wyrm or a group of hunters who have finally found them. This type of story often pleases players who enjoy fast and physical action, offering plenty of combat and violence. Storytellers should stress the nature of this story before starting the chronicle. 

- **A Mysterious Stanger:** A mysterious force, be it vengeful or mischievous, puzzles and bedevils the characters, who must determine the nature of the entity and either come to terms with it or put it to rest. This plot is particularly good for smaller troupes, as a mystery can lose its impact quickly when shared among too many characters. 

- **Murder Most Foul:** In a classic “whodunit” chronicle setting, the deceased is often a figure of power or authority, such as a Sept Leader, tribal Elder, or other influential werewolf, and the circumstances of her death are unclear. The characters must determine who killed her and why; was it the Wyrm enacting vengeance, or internal strife that led to her death? Murder plots can be useful long-term backdrops for more immediate subplots. 

- **A Game of Dominance:** Difficulties surround every sept, and this one is no different. The Wyrm’s latest strike was deterred, although many believe that that was despite the Sept Leader, not because of her. Now, the packs of the sept must discuss who will lead them going forward. Will they allow the current Sept Leader to try to prove her worth, or will they replace her with someone who they hope is a more appropriate leader? 

## Writing Plots Suited for the LARP Experience: The Economy of Cool 

The narrative experience of a LARP is vastly different than traditional media such as a novel, a movie, or even a classic tabletop game. Classic narratives strictly follow the story and perspective of a limited set of characters, but a LARP is the combined story of every character involved in the chronicle. Economies function through scarcity and demand, which drives the entire system. A common fallacy of LARP is to assume that the real game economy is based on experience points (XP). 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

Players do not compete directly against each other to earn XP. The Storyteller awards players based on attendance, costuming, and roleplaying. Players who don’t earn the maximum amount of XP allowed in a given month can, with a bit of time, earn extra XP via downtime scenes, background reports, or roleplaying via e-mail. 

The true economy of a functioning LARP is the _Economy of Cool_ . In this system, players consume and produce that unknowable quality known as cool. How is it that some players always seem to create characters who somehow become a magnet for the spotlight? How is it that some characters never seem to shine, despite a kick-ass history, rocking character sheet, and an amazing set of gifts? The answer lies within the Economy of Cool. 

The Economy of Cool assumes that there is a finite level of cool in any given game. Players naturally accrue social “cool points” over time, and the investment of said cool points can determine how well they do in the game, and more importantly, how much fun they have. 

The best LARP plots encourage interaction between characters that increases the amount of cool for everyone involved. Imagine that Maura the Adren Stargazer wishes to open a martial arts training center. You could simply allow the player to spend XP on influences and write a downtime report to justify the expenditure. 

What if you encouraged Maura to participate in the Economy of Cool? Imagine the following possible actions. These could lead to a number of social, player-generated plots, which may spread the cool around to other characters and tie all of them together. 

- Maura could speak to her local Glass Walkers regarding the purchase of the property needed for her training center. How does this change the relationship between the Glass Walkers and the Stargazers? What would the Glass Walker Sept Leader want in return for her assistance? 

- What if Maura went to the local Bone Gnawers to seek assistance in bringing people into her new center? Would they ask that she aim towards the impoverished youth in the city for their assistance? How would the other Stargazers react to working closely with the Bone Gnawers in this capacity? 

- What if Maura spoke with local kinfolk about helping her run her new center? What would the kinfolk ask for in return? Would they even want to assist the Garou in this endeavor? 

- Are there any other characters in the chronicle who could potentially help train young people in the areas of martial arts? Imagine how these characters would feel if they had the opportunity to shine by assisting a powerful character in creating something new and beneficial for the community. 

You can entertain the entire chronicle by encouraging your players to include others and invest in their characters. Suddenly, those other players likewise have an investment in a fictional martial arts training center because it is part of their characters’ story. Slowly, this center becomes a little bit more real, as characters have meetings there and start to include it in their history and their roleplaying. 

## Social Plots 

**Werewolf: The Apocalypse** is a game of survival in the face of overwhelming odds, about a society ruled by ancient laws and venerable Elders who have lived through the war. Social scenarios are often the most difficult kind of story to manage, as this aspect of the **World of Darkness** is the farthest from our own. The **World of Darkness** serves as a dark mirror to our society, but it is distorted and cracked. We live in a society striving toward egalitarianism, where all men and women are considered equal. Werewolf society functions through dominance and earned respect, with little care for anything a character has achieved among humanity. This heavily hierarchal society can take some adjustment to properly roleplay. 

## Consider the following: 

- A werewolf’s Rank changes as she goes through her career as a Garou. Most often it will rise; however, it is not impossible for situations to demand her Rank be stripped. Elders are wizened advisors and often quite powerful, while Cliath are considered little more than children who are rarely allowed to leave the sept alone. It can take mental adjustment to set aside our modern sensibilities of freedom and embrace the hierarchal beliefs of the game. We are taught to treat each other with respect and without prejudice, which can be difficult when playing a Garou character who despises all Fera she encounters. We are taught that we are all equal under the law, but in the **World of Darkness** , the concept of justice is quite different. Tribe, Rank, and Renown often determine how the laws of the sept apply to you. 

- Most people are able to walk around in public and interact with their families and friends without fearing that they might unleash their bestial side on them. Werewolves must hide their existence from the world— 

Chapter ten: Storytelling 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

even their birth families—in order to protect themselves and their loved ones. It can be a lonely existence, only alleviated by the pack and sept structures. 

- A well-adjusted person’s conscience discourages her from doing bad things, but werewolves must sometimes do terrible things in order to continue on with their duties to Gaia. A Wyrm-tainted human might be completely innocent and an unwitting victim, but that makes her no less dangerous to the Garou. 

At the city level, your game’s need for social plots varies greatly depending on the size of the game and the experience of your players. Every game is, by a very loose definition, a social scenario. 

Here are some tips to encourage good social plots: 

- Make sure the werewolves gather for a purpose. 

- Have a character make the arrangements to determine the gathering site and invite the other werewolves to attend. Often, werewolves begin their gatherings at the sept. 

- Give the other werewolves a reason to attend, even if it is social. Moots are a perfect opportunity for social gatherings. For more information on moots, see **Chapter Nine: Social Systems: Moots** , page 433. 

- Establish the social rules of the setting. A formal moot event should feel different from a gathering to plan the next attack on a hive. 

## **Networked Game Social Plots** 

If you are playing in a networked game, consider regional (inclusive of several chronicles in a state-wide area) or even global social plots that encourage roleplaying between domains, tribes, and tribal camps. Storytellers should inspire players to host special event games and invite all the neighboring games to attend. Characters who work to produce a successful regional event show a high level of commitment to the chronicle and should be rewarded. 

Social plots can shape social dynamics all over the world. **Werewolf: The Apocalypse** is a game that includes elements of warfare, corruption, and intrigue. However, it is also a game about a world that must remain secret in order to protect all. Who else can understand werewolves except other werewolves? Use NPCs to highlight important global events to the player characters. Set the stage: make Renown, monikers, and moots important. If you care about the details, the participants will too. 

## Political Plots 

Political plots focus on the acquisition and control of political and temporal power. Politics can be defined as how a group divides basic, finite resources. Although there is often fewer opportunities for political plots in W **erewolf: The Apocalypse** , it still exists, particularly among certain tribes. The limited resources such as Gnosis, shard seeds, caerns, and influence over the environment can offer an exciting game of intrigue for characters. Additionally, a clever Storyteller may create and define other important resources and assign value to them with player participation. Once value is assigned to a storytelling asset, players will often compete with each other to control said asset. 

For example, if you decide that a certain neighborhood or wild area is a ripe location for a new shard seed to be planted, you are creating a political plot that your players can ignore or seek to control. This could lead to the Garou player-characters debating whether they can take and hold the area and cultivate it into a caern, all the while other creatures such as Black Spiral Dancers or even other supernatural beings are trying to take the location for themselves. While this story may turn into a physical plot, it has every opportunity to be resolved through politics. 

You could introduce a new human or kinfolk NPC Storyteller asset that players will want to claim. For example, you might introduce a new Chief of Police, Elizabeth Janus, who has shown strong support for protecting the environment. The werewolf who controls or influences Janus will have additional leverage when the Garou are fighting the Wyrm. 

Characters belonging to the Glass Walkers, for example, may resent her work in preventing further expansion. Characters such as the Red Talons will want to ensure she remains in their control and can put a stop to any new human interaction in the wild areas. This battle might take on many forms: influences, social scenes to influence Janus, or a brutal kidnapping to force her to their way of thinking. 

At a basic level, this plot can involve political intrigue and plotting against fellow player characters and NPCs. 

This type of scenario only works with a great deal of Storyteller preparation. In this plot, it is the Storyteller’s job to create the sandbox for the player-characters to build the plot and then get out of their way. Properly prepared political plots decrease spontaneous violence in a game, because they provide a concrete means for characters to compete without escalating to killing their enemies. Bored 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

players are most likely to start pointless attacks that drain away the game’s energy. Often Storytellers ask their players, especially those with Athro or Elder characters, to avoid violence, but how can we expect the game to agree unless Storytellers provide the proper playground? 

Here are some examples of political plots: 

- The local fae court has requested interaction with the Garou, despite the enmity between their people and the Fianna. Give additional downtime actions to the player-characters that make the best impression on the fae as they become allies. Watch your players plot to make an impression on these otherworldly creatures in order to gain their favor. 

- Create a chronicle wiki or website that details the human world of your location. Make sure to list important people, organizations, and buildings to paint a solid picture of your city. Each potential detail is an entire story waiting to happen. A detailed backdrop provides your players with a chance to highlight player agency in your game by showcasing player-created businesses, projects, and changes to the city. The more you allow your players to play in the sandbox, the more their satisfaction will increase and the battle for control over these resources will intensify. 

- What if a Shadow Lord buys the land that the Black Furies had hoped to procure to build a new women’s shelter, and instead turns it into an upscale restaurant? This is the type of insult that could start a war between the two tribes on a local level. Storytellers can encourage and educate the players on the etiquette of the setting, Rank, and Renown via plot scenarios with NPCs. This strategy is often a good way to introduce the concept with new players. 

## **Networked Game Political Scenarios** 

Networked game political scenarios require more time and effort on the part of the Storytellers, but they can be exciting and rewarding. These plots encourage players to travel between games, to use Renown, and to create inter-game factions. The players’ natural desire will often be to hold political meetings behind closed doors; encourage the opposite. 

Political spectacle provides grist for excellent games. Encourage all voting or use of Renown to be done in public, and allow the occasional backroom deal to be made. 

Event games provide great potential to generate these sorts of plots. Storytellers should encourage these games to focus on some sort of political scenario. 

Here are some examples of good political scenarios for networked games: 

- Important moots 

- Large physical threats requiring the attention of multiple septs 

- Meetings of factions 

- Regional tribal meetings 

- Peace summits between the Garou and breeds of Fera 

- Forming local treaties with other supernatural creatures 

Storytellers should do some careful research to figure out what political plots might be possible in a given state. Allow for mortals like lieutenant governors, network reporters, judges, and CEOs of large companies to become pawns up for grabs. Player-characters will expend influences and even go into debt for access to the right resource. This competition encourages characters to interact and seek assistance from other domains, which in turn will build a strong regional game. 

Elders and rare Legend characters can serve as strong devices to encourage games to avoid straying too far from the baseline canon. These characters should reward the characters that stick the closest to canon. For example, if an Athro refuses to allow a Cliath to run amok, an Elder should reward that Athro for her good leadership. The best way to encourage desired behavior is to reward it. 

## MacGuffin Plots 

A _MacGuffin_ is a term coined by the famous director Alfred Hitchcock for an object of high value and often-mysterious properties. The classic, hard-boiled noir example is a locked briefcase containing an unknown prize for which the main characters battle to claim ownership. 

A MacGuffin could be a special kinfolk or Lost Cub, a powerful fetish, or a long-lost piece of Garou lore. It must be valuable enough for the characters to desire it and to provide some sort of political, social, or physical advantage. Once introduced, the Storyteller observes and occasionally referees as the characters in the chronicle do whatever they must to possess the item. 

## **MacGuffin Example** 

After the decimation of a dangerous hive, an extensive library was found deep within the earth. Containing tomes dating back to the Middle Ages, the place seemed to have been entirely untouched by time. Despite the desecration 

Chapter ten: Storytelling 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

by the Black Spiral Dancers, the library appeared to be perfectly intact. Information may be introduced via a skin-bound tome that seems to be a written history of the Garou. Seismic activity has seen a rise in the area, and local residents have reported seeing strange lights in the night sky. Prophetic dreams might reveal that the tome holds the key to a dark, ancient evil. Once the characters realize that this location and the book hold some significance, they will seek to uncover the truth of what was buried in the earth. 

## The Hero’s Journey 

Joseph Campbell proposed the theory of the Monomyth, or the _Hero’s Journey_ . This theory proposes that one old story from humanity’s ancient history echoes in every known culture or time period. This is the classic tale of a young hero who accepts the call to adventure, takes a dangerous journey into the unknown, and returns triumphant against overwhelming odds to share knowledge. This plot is innate to our culture, but it requires that the focus be pulled off of other characters and focused on the unique character, a.k.a., the hero. 

This plot can be dangerous, especially in larger games, as it can stop the fun for everyone else while a single player gets the spotlight as she battles the villain. A wise Storyteller attempting to run this sort of plot spreads the key roles to various players and attempts to give each player a turn in the spotlight, rather than simply being parts in someone else’s story. 

## Venturing into the Haunted House: LARPing a Dungeon Crawl 

One of the most popular myths is that of the hero visiting the Underworld. In modern times, the Underworld is represented by a haunted house, into which the heroes must venture to gain an understanding and acceptance of death. Roleplaying games often translate this myth into the infamous _dungeon crawl_ , where a pack of characters must journey into a dangerous place to defeat the monsters and villains threatening them. 

It is important that Storytellers develop immersion when running this plot. If the players can imagine the horrors that their characters face, they can feel the thrill of the adventure and the depths of the terror offered in this scenario. Like the Hero’s Journey, this plot can isolate players not directly involved in the action. A Storyteller can engage all of the players by offering the uninvolved players the chance to play the role of the monsters and villains, with the promise that they will have their turn in the spotlight in the future. 

## NPC Shifts 

**Werewolf: The Apocalypse** is a game that can require a large number of Storytellers, Narrators, and dedicated NPC players to properly represent the atmosphere and immersion that makes **Werewolf** such an exciting game. 

**Werewolf** is a game where most of the conflict dictated by the setting pits the players against the environment of NPCs, plot, and story developed by the Storyteller and Narrators. While **Vampire: The Masquerade** has a heavy focus on player-initiated interaction and conflict, the environment and NPCs in **Werewolf** have a crucial role in creating an immersive experience for players. 

In order to create this environment, a Storyteller may choose to institute mandatory NPC shifts for players, allowing them to fill the roles of spirits, antagonists, or other environmental characters. Based on the length of the game, a Storyteller may set a required number of hours when a player is expected to play an NPC, rather than her own character. This option gives the opportunity for players to play both sides of the fence, enhancing the experience for their fellow players in events that are important to them and their characters, while relying on the fact that their peers will do the same when it is their turn on shift. This setup can initially be difficult to manage, especially when players feel unsure about their NPC roles or when their own characters will be involved in the events during their NPC shifts. Nevertheless, with open communication and by working with the cast of the game, this option can heighten immersion and realism, and create far more dramatic tension for everyone playing. 

Players who are required to portray NPCs should coordinate with the Storyteller about any props or clothing that they are required to have for their shifts. Storytellers should provide a sign-in sheet informing players what shifts are open so that they can choose their preferred time slots for their shifts. The Storyteller should also provide a brief description of the NPCs’ motivations and goals in order to better assist those completing their shifts. This arrangement can also be an opportunity for the game to collect a series of commonly used items—clothing, props, make up, and the like—that can be kept communally for the game. This collection allows whomever is portraying reoccurring NPCs the ability to keep a sense of continuity, and it allows players to differentiate their NPCs’ appearances from any character costuming players normally wear. 

While it is not necessary to use NPC shifts in your game, using this option along with the Quest system and other tools provided throughout this book can help ease the burden on the storytelling staff and give them the chance to focus on what they do best: providing a compelling and exciting story for all to enjoy participating in. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

## Spirit Management 

Storytellers are responsible for the creation and management of spirits in a chronicle. Using these spirits effectively helps players connect to the spiritual nature of the Garou and the umbral realms. It also ensures that your players do not lose sight of the individual goals and factional goals of their spirit allies or enemies, motivations that are often quite strange to anyone other than a spirit itself. 

When creating a chronicle, a Storyteller should map out the parts of a city, town, or wilderness area they are planning to use, taking note of certain landmarks or historical events that may have taken place there. They can use these locations and their histories to determine the types of spirits that may be attracted to an area. For example, bane spirits may be attracted to a location of a mass murder, while a glen might attract Wyld spirits. This map allows a Storyteller to keep accurate track of the current events, and provides her with an opportunity to adjust the map between games based on players’ actions. The way the spirits interact with, and react to, players’ actions creates a deeper, more visceral environment for those players who enjoy the spiritual interaction **Werewolf** provides. 

Once a Storyteller has areas mapped out and has determined what type of spirits may be attracted to those places, she should choose a faction to which the spirits are tied— Weaver, Wyld, or Wyrm—and use those factions to outline any goals the spirits may have in the area. Are they revitalizing a once-shriveled local park in the inner city, or are they weaving webs to strengthen the Weaver’s influence in a business park? 

Spirits should have motivations and methods they use to accomplish their goals, even if those goals are simple manifestations of their factions. This detail gives players rich environmental NPCs with which to interact. 

## Storytelling in the Umbra 

The spiritual realms and locations of the Umbra provide a number of avenues for adventure and exploration for the Garou. Storytellers can use the Umbra to tell stories of gaining knowledge, obtaining personal redemption, confronting an old foe, or numerous other scenarios that cannot work within the confines of the Material Realm. 

In order to enter the Near or Deep Umbra, a character must usually undergo a quest (see **Chapter Thirteen: The Umbra** , page 583). This quest only allows participating characters to enter these sections of the Umbra, and the resolution of the quest does not mean the character has achieved their goals within the Umbra. The actual goals and 

activities that happen within these unique umbral realms provide additional stories and quests of their own. 

## Creating a Story in the Umbra 

The journey and the destination are both key elements in any story set within the Umbra. Whether the ultimate destination is an umbral realm or just a location within the Umbra itself, by concentrating on these two facets, a Storyteller can enhance the experience for all players involved. 

The first phase is the journey. This segment can be broken down into the following steps: 

- Determine the destination. 

- Establish the requirements and obstacles on the way. 

- Describe the arrival destination. 

When the destination is a specific umbral realm, a Storyteller can decide whether to use the Quest Generation System with its requirements and consequences or to run the scenarios herself. No matter how she chooses to handle it, the Quest Generation System offers a set of guidelines to determine the difficulty and time requirements such a journey will entail. 

Once the method of travel has been determined, a Storyteller can move on to establishing what challenges and obstacles must be overcome in order to succeed at arriving to the destination. This requirement can be as extensive as the need to battle mighty enemies and answer challenging riddles, to something as simple as obtaining the correct map, depending on the nature of the story being told. 

When the characters arrive at the destination, the Storyteller can begin to set the stage for the second part of the adventure. This event is the actual entrance to the umbral realm or the action that happens when the players reach their destination. Stressing the differences between the Umbra they know and the realm to which they are going helps set the mood for the players, raising the tension and the drama as well as the overall level of immersion. After players conclude this segment of the adventure, the Storyteller should move on to the second phase of storytelling an umbral adventure. 

## Inside Umbral Realms 

Each of the realms of the Umbra has different rules and a setting unique to itself. These changes depend on which aspect of the Umbra the characters have chosen to explore—the Penumbra, the Near Umbra or the Deep Umbra—as well as on the realm itself. 

Chapter ten: Storytelling 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

The different laws of each realm and the ever-changing nature of the Umbra itself ensure that no two adventures, even ones that take place in the same realm, are ever the same. Playing up these differences keeps each new story set there fresh and exciting. Storytellers should make sure to emphasize the particular aspects of each realm that make them special. Describe the horrific nature of Atrocity, or the sweet, idyllic serenity of the Summer Country. By making the realms unique in and of themselves, their differences are enhanced when characters travel to other realms. 

As characters arrive in at their destination in the Umbra, the Storyteller should look for opportunities to allow characters that specialize in the Umbra their moment to shine. Give those with appropriate Lore skill specializations the possibility of knowing some of the laws of the realm they have entered, or even possible ways to exit once their task has been accomplished. As some players will make the Umbra their characters’ specialty, giving them this time to play up their knowledge and ability is vital. 

## The Reward 

Whether the characters seek information, fetishes, or wealth and power, it is important to ensure that the rewards characters receive from successfully completing their goals are equivalent to the challenges they have faced and overcome. The unique features of these challenges can also be used to highlight the strange nature of the umbral realms themselves. Presenting this information through the different lenses through which spirits see the world makes it feel all the more valuable and exciting. A spirit within the Cyber Realm may relay information clinically in its familiar terminology, which differ greatly from the same information spoken by a hero from the Legendary Realm. 

## Escaping the Umbra 

Exiting an umbral realm and travelling back to the Material Realm is the final hurdle that characters must overcome in order to complete their quest. This event provides an opportunity for Storytellers to keep the tension and drama going until the very end. 

Using the Quest Generation System to return is one option, though canny Storytellers should make sure to tailor the requirements so that the group can accomplish them, in order to avoid locking their players into a scenario where they are unable to complete a quest to return home, unless that is explicitly the Storyteller’s intention. Running the return trip as a scenario offers the opportunity to add in ambushes and other challenges, helping characters value the success they have achieved up to this point. No matter which option a Storyteller chooses to exercise, continuing to 

provide the same level of detail to the return trip as she did for the initial arrival voyage keeps every umbral adventure memorable and unique. 

For more information on the Quest Generation System, see **Chapter Eight: Dramatic Systems: Quests** : page 390. 

For more information on the Umbra and umbral realms, see **Chapter Thirteen: The Umbra: The Deep Umbra** , page 597. 

## Framing and Running Scenarios 

Conflict reveals character. The struggle of conflict isn’t just a direct means to an end—it also reveals the hidden emotional responses of your character. In ordinary life, most people go out of their way to avoid conflict, but in a successful LARP, it is an essential component of what makes a dynamic scene worthy of remembering. Characters’ struggles challenge them to grow in unexpected ways, providing the spark of life that can make the difference between a two-dimensional character and a living, breathing creation that entertains and enthralls. As the plot of the story and the intentions of the characters unfold, conflict provides a way to understand the motivations driving the characters. 

There are three types of character conflicts: 

- **Internal:** That of the character against her own nature (e.g., fear, anger, hunger) 

- **External:** That of the character against the environment or the setting (e.g., overcoming an obstacle, escaping danger) 

- **Interpersonal:** That of the character against someone else (e.g., winning an argument, fighting an enemy) 

## Turning a Conflict into a Scenario to Tell a Story 

Conflict can drive a story in unexpected directions and generate interesting scenarios. A _scenario_ is a scene where the player and the Storyteller cooperatively attempt to resolve a conflict through the application of rules. 

Imagine you are playing a Fostern Get of Fenris Ahroun named Vigdis. You were not born into your tribe, but rather adopted into it, and you have always struggled to prove that you are worthy of Fenris’ blessing. You have spent the past month investigating a medical facility that you are certain belongs to the Wyrm, and your dedication has finally paid off. You have found proof that the facility is teeming 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

with fomori, and you are determined to make your name by bringing the facility down. There is one complication, of course, when the Sept Leader—also a Get of Fenris— declares that another pack will handle the situation at the facility, and you are not invited. 

This conflict can generate a number of different scenarios in which the character has choices about which action she could take. Some examples: 

- The Sept Leader requests a private conversation with Vigdis. He has seen her frustration about his declaration, and he tries to explain his reasoning to her. This situation might lead to a second conflict when Vigdis demands the Sept Leader allow her to go on the mission, even if she is forced to go without the pack. Although the Sept Leader forbids her, Vigdis goes anyway. The Storyteller might turn this internal conflict into a scenario by an appearance from the Pack Totem, telling Vigdis why her idea might be a dangerous one. If Vigdis goes into the facility alone, she will most likely die. 

- Later in the evening, Vigdis finds herself at the facility, and spots the other pack beginning to enter. The Storyteller can transform this into a number of potential scenarios. She might require a static Stealth challenge to allow Vigdis to sneak into the facility unseen, or she might require Vigdis to make a successful Investigation challenge to allow her to discover that the facility is much more dangerous than even she thought, home to a Thunderwyrm. Here, the consequences of failure are uncertain: maybe a fomor guard spots Vigdis and she must fight to survive, or perhaps she is seen by the other pack and forced to turn back or join them. 

- Even if she’s successful in the above challenges, Vigdis may come into direct conflict with other characters, such as her packmates who are upset that Vigdis is so determined to prove herself in such a dangerous manner, or other sept officers who attempt to talk sense into her. This interpersonal conflict can be resolved by intense roleplaying, a quest (see **Chapter Eight: Dramatic Systems, Quests** , page 390), or by a complex scenario (see **Chapter Seven: Core Systems, Complex Scenarios: Combat** page 339) as the characters conflict with one another. 

## Using Scenarios to Capture the Imaginations of Your Players 

An amazing scenario stems from cooperation and collaboration between the Storyteller and the players. This delicate dance requires the Storyteller to lead while 

allowing the players to enjoy some influence on how the story progresses. The Storyteller sets the stage using enthusiasm, descriptive wording, and creative challenges, while the players reveal aspects of their characters’ personalities and values in their choices as they attempt to resolve the scenario. 

Learning to read your players and figure out which emotional buttons will excite and interest them is a critical success factor to fire their imaginative engines. Some players prefer the challenge of navigating tricky political or social situations, while others crave the danger of riding the ragged edge of disaster. Observe their actions and choices: you can learn a lot about what your players want by sitting quietly in a corner for half an hour and just observing silently as the game unfolds. Who is skulking in a corner, doing the same thing you are doing but for in-character reasons? Who is looking to supplant the Sept Leader? Who is being a bully, a tyrant, a seducer, a traitor? Players reveal goals, ambitions, and desires through observable actions. 

Listen closely to the questions your players ask you during a scene. These inquires reveal exactly which aspects of the story intrigue them. Once you know this, you can modify the scenario to specifically feature details that interest the players and push the emotional buttons that help players buy into it. When a player asks whether or not some mundane object has any hidden clues, where her character can score some high-grade poison, how to find out where a local coterie of vampires spends their time, or what she knows about an obscure point of lore, she is expressing interest that you can note for later exploitation—in the best way, by turning it into story and plot. 

This technique doesn’t suggest that you should simply let the players do whatever they want without consequences. Quite the reverse: actions must have consequences that are meaningful for the story to matter. Players want and need to know that the world their characters inhabit is logical, even if they don’t understand all of the aspects of it. If a player risks her character’s life specifically by choice, you should calculate the odds fairly and determine a good means to resolve the challenge. The balance is precarious. If the player instinctually believes that the laws of the universe are arbitrary, she will be disappointed. Immersion requires the players to have faith in the Storyteller. If gravity suddenly does not work as expected, the fictional universe feels less real. On the other hand, if a Storyteller is too strict and simply leaves behind a trail of paper corpses, players will become frustrated. 

Some players will suggest solutions to the scenario that you never imagined, but that’s good. This isn’t a choose-yourown adventure novel, so don’t worry if the players decide to take a route that leads them somewhere you didn’t predict 

Chapter ten: Storytelling 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

they would go. Communication is important to help players visualize exactly what is happening with their imaginations. Think carefully about the choices made by the players, even if they weren’t the traditionally correct choices, and be prepared to let go of your own preconceptions. Creative players boost the sense of immersion that increases everyone’s enjoyment. Such moments can lead to memorable stories. 

## Pacing of a Scenario 

_Pacing_ is the speed of action at a game session. Some scenarios move at breakneck speed; others simmer and bubble for a long time before exploding into violence; still others move along at a consistent but moderate rate. The key to pacing a scenario is to monitor the behavior and responses of your players. Move too quickly and players will miss vital clues for no apparent reason and then spend the remainder of the evening scratching their heads and feeling frustrated as the session drags on to a late hour. A game that has slowed to a crawl bores players. 

Allow the players involved in the scenario to set the initial tempo of the scenario. Some groups will also cooperate with one another in a frighteningly efficient manner from time to time, solving all the puzzles and conundrums that you thought would take them the entire night to understand, changing the pace from fast and furious to painfully slow when they run out of things to do. Others will feel completely overwhelmed by information and so uncertain of what to do next that they will cease taking an active role in the story altogether. In either case, when players start dropping out of character and seem reluctant to re-enter the game, you know that you have a pacing problem. 

Some Storytellers resolve pacing problems solely through outside threats, either introducing or removing them depending on the pace they’re trying to achieve. This strategy sometimes works, but often it becomes too obvious, giving rise to “10 o’clock monster” syndrome (the introduction of an enemy or other external threat in the final hour of the game, which is the natural time for the pace to lag). Give them the space to chew on the scenario and listen carefully to the questions they ask to see things from their perspective. Noting which questions they don’t ask is equally important. 

Once you have a feel for your group, you can adjust the tempo by introducing new threads. Fast-paced games throw new situations at the characters throughout the night, forcing them to deal with each new challenge as it presents itself and leaving little time for them to assess the big picture before the session is done. Slower-paced games lend themselves to extended communications between characters and therefore to subtler intrigues and plots. 

Players must share some responsibility for maintaining a good pace. If they don’t make characters who want things, who actively pursue goals, and who are willing to take a few risks now and then, the entire burden of maintaining game pace falls to the Storyteller. When players complain that they are bored, ask them what their goals are for the night—if they can’t tell you, then it’s probably a good idea to suggest that they form some, quickly, and start pursuing them. Characters who want nothing, who seek nothing, who desire nothing, and who are unwilling to ever risk anything for fear of losing are boring, and their players are therefore prone to becoming bored. Remember that a successful chronicle is a collaborative effort, and players must meet the Storyteller halfway. 

You can also help yourself pace stories more successfully by learning to narrate spontaneously. If you have plot outlines, you can often generate scenarios on the fly that relate to the big picture while still entertaining players. For a series of tips on how to narrate spontaneously using psychological tricks, see **Storytelling Techniques: Spontaneous Narration** , page 485. 

Alternately, you can reintroduce a plotline that the characters thought was resolved in order to keep the game session going without a break in the action—you’ve got a list of those in your story bible, right? If you have the luxury, another way to combat player boredom and maintain pacing is by appointing an “I’m bored!” Narrator. Her sole responsibility is to help players engage with the story by asking them good questions about their goals and maybe even narrating a few short plots that she has prepared against this very eventuality. 

## Awarding Experience Points 

During life, we learn from the mistakes of yesterday and prepare for the challenges of tomorrow.
