Likewise, the Fenrir have recently allied with their old rivals, the Black Furies, thanks to a budding friendship between their councilors. 

The Fenrir do not find it easy to mask their disdain, and this forthrightness does not often earn them friends. The perceived weakness of the Bone Gnawers repulses them. Likewise, they view the peace and diplomacy that the Children of Gaia espouse as a form of cowardice. 

The Fenrir Councilor rules over the Sept of Blood Fist in Black Forest, Germany, protecting the great caern. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## Glass Walkers 

Believed to be dead for nearly a decade, councilor Roger Daly returned to the Garou Nation at the start of the Age of Apocalypse with a warning from the Weaver about the possible return of the Storm Eater. Infamous for his ability to think outside the box and form impossible alliances, such as the controversial peace treaty between the werewolves and vampires of Vancouver, many credit Daly with working with Margrave Konietzko behind the scenes to form the Concordat of Stars. Cautious and perceptive, Daly has become the architect of the new faction, fostering ambitious goals to restore the Triat to balance. 

The tribe’s natural allies are the Bone Gnawers and the Children of Gaia. The Bone Gnawers share their affinity with the Weaver, and they share space in urban centers of the world. The Children of Gaia push the Garou Nation to attempt to restore balance within the Triat, a pursuit started with the formation of the Concordat of Stars. 

As a tribe, the Glass Walkers find it difficult to work with the Black Furies and the Red Talons. Both of these tribes are so focused on the Wyld places of the world and act with such little tolerance for anything they consider Weaverdominated, that the clashes between these two tribes and the Glass Walkers are as inevitable as they are frequent. 

Daly is still actively hunted by Pentex. In an effort to evade its reach, he convinced the tribal council to send him to assist the Ahadi in protecting the Sept of Last Stand in Lagos, Nigeria. He wielded considerable influence to turn Lagos into the Silicon Valley of Africa. 

## Red Talons 

Thistlechar is a fierce, taciturn wolf who leads the Red Talons by virtue of her strength. A brutal man-made forest fire burnt her as a cub, but Thistlechar has grown into a matriarch who has whelped four generations of fierce wolves. Since then, she’s fought many battles to keep humans from her lands. Her determination to attend the tribal council meetings stems from a desire to keep an eye on the other tribes and prevent them from sliding towards the Weaver. She quietly supports the Sanctum of Gaia, but her real interests lie in restarting the Impergium. For the moment, Thistlechar understands that the other werewolves are too tainted by their human heritage to ever agree to this plan on their own, so she is quietly reaching out the Lupus minorities of the other tribes to build her own faction of true wolves. 

The Red Talons often keep the other tribes at a distance, finding their perceptions too human for their dispositions, but they find a special kinship with the Get of Fenris and 

Wendigo. The Fenrir’s reverence for the wolf spirit Fenris appeals to the Lupus-oriented tribe. The Wendigo’s savage predator instinct feels natural to the Red Talons. They respect these tribes’ constant desire to test one another to determine their internal pack rankings. Likewise, they respect the Black Furies for their protection of the Wyld places of the world. Some Red Talons are actively recruiting the Lupus members of these tribes to support their desire for a new Impergium. 

The Bone Gnawers and the Glass Walkers hold the greatest portion of the Red Talons’ ire, since the Red Talons consider these tribes traitors to Gaia, having betrayed her to the Weaver and protecting the ones currently destroying the Material Realm. Every moment they spend wasted on humanity is one not spent on what is truly important: Gaia and the war against the Wyrm. 

The Red Talon Councilor rules over the Sept of the Weeping Daughter, protecting the great caern and the wilderness. Until the Age of Apocalypse, the Red Talons strictly forbade any other tribe from entering its bawn. 

## Shadow Lords 

Margrave Josef Konietzko is credited as the founding father of the Concordat of Stars and the leader who organized the quest to discover shard seeds as the method for building new caerns. The Shadow Lord Councilor has an acerbic, biting demeanor that often inflames his enemies, such as the Fenrir Councilor, Valka One-Eye. Many believe that it is Konietzko’s destiny to become the next High King, leading the Garou Nation out of the Age of Apocalypse and reviving Gaia. 

The Shadow Lords believe that there are no acquaintances in a fox hole. They pulled the Stargazers and Glass Walkers together into the Concordat of Stars, eschewing their former alliances in order to push forward and lead the faction. The Shadow Lords value the Glass Walkers’ insight into the Weaver and the Stargazers’ kinship with the Fera as valuable assets to be exploited. 

The rise of the Concordat of Stars has only increased the enmity the Shadow Lords have with the Silver Fangs, as they are no longer one tribe seeking to overcome traditional rule, but the head of a coalition of tribes seeking to do so. 

The Shadow Lord Councilor volunteered to take control over the Sept of the Wheel of Ptah in Casablanca, Morocco, protecting the great caern. 

## Silent Striders 

Born during the chaos of the Gulf War, Samira “Doomspeaker” Tahan spent her life as a refugee, surviving 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

in war zones. Shortly after her First Change, the Swords of Night recruited her, and she trained for a decade to hunt and kill vampires. During the next ten years, the Ragabash became one of the foremost experts on the subject, traveling across the world to aid different septs in dealing with these undead terrors. The Silent Striders revere her knowledge of the world and her accomplishments—out of respect for her wishes as Councilor, her tribe has refused to ally with either the Sanctum of Gaia or the Concordat of Stars. Tahan often stands as the swing-vote between the two factions, and she is often cited as the reason the Garou Nation hasn’t crowned a new High King. 

The Silent Striders strive to remain neutral in petty political alliances and feuds. This stance makes them welcome in septs across the world. They often extend friendship to the Black Furies and Bone Gnawers, both of whom honor the concept of home and hearth, and so have some understanding of the pain the Silent Striders suffer, locked away from their traditional homelands. 

Although honor and wisdom demand that they mask it, the Silent Striders have a strong distaste for the Silver Fangs and the Shadow Lords. These tribes’ leadership and machinations have factionalized the nation, creating a deep rift that requires attention the Silent Striders would rather spend hunting vampires. 

The Silent Strider Councilor assists the Ahadi in protecting the Dark River Caern, near Lisala in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. As Silent Striders prefer to roam, Tahan is content not leading a sept, instead aiding the Fera. 

## Silver Fangs 

Sabine “Silver-Swift” LaCoix ascended to Queen of House Unbreakable Hearth when Margaret Standing Stones fell in battle defending the Sept of the Crescent Moon. After High King Albrecht disappeared during the siege against the caerns at the dawn of the Age of Apocalypse, the Silver Fangs regrouped under her banner. Together with Alika Kalonimos and Valka One-Eye, she forged the foundation of the Sanctum of Gaia and led the Garou Nation through its diaspora and the plague of Harano. Tension between Margrave Konietzko and LaCoix has divided the Council of Tribes, as each seeks to claim the position of High King. 

Haughty, yet charismatic and inspiring, LaCoix rallied the houses of Europe to stand behind her and made inroads to repair the rift between Silver Fangs and Wendigo in the wilds of Russia. Silver Fangs look to the other founding tribes of the Sanctum of Gaia—the Fenrir and Black Furies—for strong alliances. Their partnership has been tempered by the heat of battle and hardened by success. 

The Shadow Lords earned the enmity of the Silver Fangs long ago, when they leveraged the Mongols against Russia, but the rise of the Concordat of Stars revived every old slight and grudge. The Glass Walkers likewise have earned the Silver Fangs’ distrust and disdain for their allegiances both to the Weaver and the Shadow Lords. LaCoix argues that the Glass Walker Councilor, Roger Daly, is a Winter Wolf and should be put down, according the Litany. 

The Silver Fang Councilor rules over the Sept of the Crescent Moon in the Ural Mountains, Russia, protecting the great caern. 

## Stargazers 

Upon finally reclaiming the Shigalu Monastery after a decade of struggle, the leader of the Zephyr camp, Anh Lai Far-Traveler, felt a distinct unease in her soul, even for an Ahroun. She couldn’t help but worry for the fate of the Sunset People and their important struggle to protect the Emerald Mother. This unease left her receptive to contact from the son of her father’s old packmate, representing the Garou Nation. Ciran Far-Traveler died years before, and yet Anh Lai felt his work had been left undone. The Stargazers had heard rumors of the Wyrm’s assault on the other caerns of the world, but lacked the context to properly understand how badly the war went for the Garou Nation. Margrave Konietzko asked Anh Lai Far-Traveler to bring the Stargazers back to the nation and to bring as many of the Fera as she could muster for the ongoing fight. She agreed to honor her father, and she spoke to the Elders of her tribe, pleading the case. They agreed, on the condition that she personally lead them in her cause and represent the tribe in the Garou Nation. 

Stargazers find common cause with the Bone Gnawers and the Children of Gaia, tribes that also emphasize balance, even if they do so without recognizing its nuances. The Children of Gaia champion those too weak to champion themselves, balancing their strength to support others who are weak. Likewise, the Bone Gnawers push for equality for all, a sentiment of balance and justice that the Stargazers can embrace. 

The Stargazers take the most issue with the Red Talons and the Get of Fenris. The Red Talons’ hatred of the Homid sides of their own natures clashes with the Stargazers’ emphasis on finding balance within oneself. The Get of Fenris share a similar weakness, focusing on strength and aggression without also honoring peace and diplomacy. 

The Stargazer Councilor rules over the Sept of Glass and Steel at the Galaxy Soho Building in Beijing, China, protecting the great caern. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## Uktena 

Aylen “Ripper” Queupul underwent her First Change during one of the numerous Chilean campaigns against indigenous land rights before the turn of the millennium. This Thuerge’s deed name was well-earned after a bloody fight leveraging spirits against human soldiers harassing a village, who were later discovered with their hearts torn from their chests. Queupul fought for two decades to protect her territory from corporations more interested in profit than being stewards of the land. The Age of Apocalypse forced the Pure Ones and the European tribes to fight back to back, protecting the few remaining caerns. Vaunted Fenrir Legend, Golgol Fangs-First, saved her life by shielding her from a volley of silver bullets during the siege on the Hollow Heart Caern. The experience changed her perceptions, and she reached out to the Bastet and other Fera for help protecting the caern. Afterwards, Elders of the Uktena tribe asked her to represent them on the tribal council. 

The impulsive decision of their Younger Brother to join the Sanctum of Gaia ensured that the Uktena joined with the Concordat of Stars, learning just what plans and desires are espoused by a camp so determined to see change come to the Garou Nation. Though they are part of different factions, nothing could ever completely shatter the tie between the Wendigo and the Uktena, and they count their Younger Brother as a staunch ally, no matter how much the two tribes might disagree on the best methods to accomplish their tasks. The Uktena also consider the Stargazers allies, admiring that tribe’s acquisition of knowledge and lore from a variety of sources the Uktena had not known existed. 

The Uktena tribe’s memory is long. They have never forgiven the Silver Fangs or the Shadow Lords, who came to the Pure Lands and set in motion the chain of events that led to the loss of their brother tribe, the Croatan. However, the Uktena are patient, and for the moment both of these tribes are needed for the war against the Wyrm. The Uktena are cautious, understanding that while a dagger is a tool, it can cut its wielder. 

The Uktena Councilor rules over the Sept of the Split Sun in Sierra de las Minas, Guatemala. 

## Wendigo 

Once the Wendigo were isolationists who avoided political machinations within the Garou Nation, leaving such things to the Uktena. The formation of the Sanctum of Gaia opened a window of opportunity for the Wendigo, allowing them to flex their political muscles and demand respect. A natural negotiator, the Philodox Joseph Wintersrage spent 

decades playing different federal agencies against each other, seeking out the best deals for his kin and securing the few remaining Wyld lands. He turned his dispassionate, icy demeanor towards the other tribes, demanding concessions in exchange for the Wendigo tribe’s support. 

Surprisingly, the Silver Fangs proved to be the tribe most receptive to Wintersrage’s maneuvers. Once the Silver Fangs were the standard-bearers for the oppression the Wendigo endured when the European werewolves first landed on the shores of the Pure Lands, but now the Silver Fangs were humbled and receptive, offering reparations. Remembering the friendship between Evan Heals-the-Past and High King Albrecht, and their sacrifice to save the world from the Storm Eater, the Wendigo followed Wintersrage’s lead and joined the Sanctum of Gaia. Their new alliance has created bonds of friendship with the Red Talons and the Get of Fenris, as these two tribes share a fierce determination on the battlefields. 

Not all things change; not all feuds die. The Wendigo continue their aggression towards the Shadow Lords for their crimes against the Pure Lands during the Age of Sorrow. Likewise, they remember well the damage caused by Glass Walkers, when that tribe was known as the Iron Riders, during the Age of Storms. 

The Wendigo Councilor rules over the Sept of the Last Breath in the Caribou River Park Reserve in Manitoba, Canada. 

## Politics in the Garou Nation 

The Age of Apocalypse began with a devastating wave of attacks upon the world’s caerns that that caused massive, unpredictable umbral storms and the diaspora of the old septs of the Garou Nation. A plague of Harano afflicted the new refugees as they attempted to survive and rebuild their lives. Some began to speculate that the spirit of Gaia was dead and that there was no longer a point to fighting. The war against the Wyrm would end not with a final battle, but a sorrowful surrender. Two new political camps, forged from an alliance of tribes, arose to lead the Garou Nation and ignite hope. 

## Factions 

## The Sanctum of Gaia 

The _Sanctum of Gaia_ , led by the Silver Fangs, pushed for a hard return to the old ways of the Garou Nation. Its member tribes also include the Fianna, Get of Fenris, Wendigo, Red Talons, and the Black Furies. They argue that werewolves 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

faltered without the support of their traditional culture, and that the lack of moots and proper chiminage to the spirits allowed the new rise of Harano spirit-sickness to infect the body of the nation. Therefore, they say, the only means of survival for Gaia and the nation is through a complete victory over the Wyrm. The members of the Sanctum of Gaia believe that Garou can achieve this victory without the aid of Fera or humans. This faction gained a great deal of prestige when its methodology proved quite effective against the spiritual sickness plaguing the Garou. 

The Sanctum of Gaia strongly believes that the only allies werewolves need are the spirits that joined them in the dawn times. Taking these partners for granted in their war has led too many Garou to see the spirits as tools for the creation of fetishes and talens, and as a security system for territory, instead of potent allies and partners as Gaia originally intended. The Sanctum of Gaia stresses that overlooking of spiritual side of itself has weakened the nation as a whole. Where once clothing and weapons were crafted by hand, using traditional means, the Weaver’s soulless mass production has allowed far too many werewolves to toss away items that can be fixed in favor of something new— the lure of the cheap and easy replacing the heart and soul that craftsmen and women imbue into their creations. 

The Sanctum’s members are realistic enough to understand that they cannot do without all Weaver-creations; however, they urge all members to avoid using them whenever possible. If a weapon or protection is needed, they point to the master artisans of the Fianna as the best possible source. At least their creations are infused with the Wyld spirit inherent to Garou, balancing out any possible Weaver influence. Some members are so devout in their belief that the Weaver is foul that they refuse to use any tool not empowered by Gaia’s Wyld energy. They espouse the conviction that only fetishes and talens blessed by spirits’ presence, or clothing made solely from the hides of animals they hunt and slay themselves, are acceptable to use; such strictures prevent the Weaver from burrowing into the souls of Gaia’s chosen. 

Sanctum members also point to the ever-shrinking acreage of untamed lands as a wound weakening Gaia—possibly contributing to the rise in cases of Harano so brutally sweeping through and thinning the ranks of werewolves. As humanity and technology overruns more and more territory, it further weakens the spiritual world, which supports the Garou. Sanctum members are convinced that this deterioration led to the return of umbral storms, which have ravaged the spiritual landscape. They are the first to counsel that only a return to rites and chiminage supporting and strengthening the spirits and their realm will allow any chance for success against the Wyrm. 

The Sanctum of Gaia points to the Ages of Myth and Legend, when humanity and werewolves alike lived in a symbiotic relationship, with Garou guarding the land and the creatures Gaia birthed within it. The Wyrm has violated that order, and as such must be destroyed. Gaia mandated this cause to her chosen warriors; unlike the faithless werewolves of their rival faction, the Garou of the Sanctum of Gaia will not fail to follow the will of their mother. 

There cannot be balance until these wounds are lanced and corruption purified, say members of the Sanctum. Only then will the Triat be able to heal. The Red Talons urge another Impergium, and this sentiment is gaining traction with some members of the Sanctum. Humanity has obviously gotten far out of hand, and perhaps it is time to restore them to a more manageable number, they believe. Such a systematic hunt of humanity would also help identify those with latent kinfolk blood, who may awaken through the Bite and join the Garou. While many still believe a new Impergium is too drastic a step to consider, even they agree that such a culling of humanity would weaken the Weaver, a goal many consider just as important. 

The Sanctum recognizes that leadership is a burden and not a privilege. Its members consider it their duty to serve as examples to their fellow members, as well as to those who do not yet agree with the dire position the Garou Nation is in, according to the Sanctum. Its members are the first to point out that the old ways—the traditions that founded and guided the Garou Nation since the creation of the first werewolves—have served them for so long, that to change now spits in the eyes of their ancestors and Gaia herself. If she had wished for change, they say, then she would have made her will known. Without their traditional codes of conduct and behavior, the Garou would not have survived to see the onset of the Age of Apocalypse; radically changing their ways now will only ensure they do not survive this important war. 

Since its emergence, werewolves have raised many questions about the significance of the Bite, and the effect this new process for triggering a First Change could have on the nation as a whole. The Sanctum of Gaia realizes that the Bite is another weapon in an arsenal running dangerously low. It is an example of natural selection, where the best and brightest will survive the change and join Gaia’s warriors. Sadly, the Bite’s success rate is very low, which is why it is vital that Garou find and protect those who may have a chance of successfully undergoing the process. Only by carefully cultivating this new resource will the Garou be able to reap the reward—an increased amount of warriors for their never-ending struggle. Thus, they believe, kinfolk have a duty to submit to guardianship until it can be determined if they should have the chance to join the Garou. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

The Sanctum reluctantly participates in the recent cease-fire between werewolves and vampires. While the Sanctum’s members understand that this truce is necessary under the current conditions, this situation has not dimmed the fire in their hearts to destroy this ancient menace. Elders of the Sanctum spend many days reminding their young and idealistic members that while for now there are bigger problems, someday when the war is won, it will be time to turn back and fight the undead. None forget that these ancient beings are almost as much of a blight upon the face of Gaia as the Wyrm and its more obviously corrupted minions. 

## **The Tenets of the Sanctum of Gaia** 

- The Weaver is a spinner of traps; her tools are one of them. 

- The Garou are Gaia’s chosen warriors, the example all others should seek to follow. 

- The pacts forged and rituals performed with the spirits are a vital part of Garou society. 

- The Bite is a gift from Gaia to replenish the Garou Nation. 

- The traditions set forth in the dawn times saved the Garou from Harano, proving their necessity. 

## The Concordat of Stars 

The _Concordat of Stars_ , led by the Shadow Lords, arose when the Wyrm sieged caerns across the world. The Shadow Lords allied with a number of Fera who helped save the last thirteen caerns. The Concordat’s member tribes also include the Stargazers, Glass Walkers, Bone Gnawers, Children of Gaia, and Uktena. The followers of the Concordat of Stars believe that the war against the Wyrm cannot be won through traditional martial means. They argue that victory can only be achieved by balancing the Triat. Furthermore, they assert that werewolves can’t accomplish this balancing process alone; they’ll need the aid of the Fera and possibly other creatures to attain it. Initially, other Garou branded this faction as misguided, but when the Concordat discovered the potential of shard seeds, with the aid of the Gurahl, the faction gained an equal footing with the Sanctum of Gaia. 

The Concordat believes that as time moves on, so must everyone: Garou, human, and spirit. Stagnation is another form of corruption, and by refusing to change with the world around them, the Garou have let this corruption take root and fester. Only by learning and growing can they embody the balance they seek to forge anew in the Triat. Gaia created 

all the shapeshifters, giving each type a form and function to suit its purpose. The Concordat asserts that only by working together can there be any hope of survival and renewal. They point to the forging of alliances between Fera and Garou— and even the lowering of hostilities with other supernatural species—as demonstrated success, bringing diversified resources to a war that the Garou have fought alone for so long without victory. The discovery of shard seeds is the Concordat’s ultimate proof that even Gaia favors its path towards balance. Concordat of Stars members want to break beyond the old traditional barriers that prevented them from trying new strategies and tactics to attempt to win a war that isn’t being won on battlefields with tooth and claw. 

They also believe that if humans cannot be counted on to find their own way, guiding them is the only true course—not slaughter. Concordat members happily use the information and power they wield to support and protect humans, especially those who could deeply impact the world around them, ensuring the Wyrm cannot derail their destinies. Concordat members urge a stronger and more thorough integration of kinfolk into Garou society as warriors, healers, mates, and visionaries. They do not want to hide their kinfolk away, keeping them from the world with the excuse of protecting them. Instead Concordat members wish to teach and prepare their kinfolk, giving them knowledge and tools to take a more active role in the world in which they live. This practice has led to innovation in the war; kinfolk and other allies create companies that use the spoils their Garou allies provide to buy, cleanse, and revitalize Wyrm-corrupted lands. Through their kin, the Concordat actively seeks influence in the mortal halls of power, leveraging humanity itself to benefit Gaia and all her children. 

Concordat members often justify their urgency by reminding others that the Age of Apocalypse has begun. They believe time cannot be wasted on arguing about each other’s failures and foibles, when the enemy without is clawing down the door. Each successfully planted shard seed provides hope for growth, representing another beachhead, another step forward, another sign of progress in a war that has seen far too many defeats for the Garou nation. The Concordat asserts that all shapeshifters can no longer sit back and defend their own territories while their cousins are slaughtered. Unity is the only hope for survival. This spirit of harmony is so strong that leaders of the Concordat have reached out to allies not before considered by the Garou. They seek out other supernatural creatures, such as mages, fae, and even vampires, to create alliances and treaties, supporting the overall goal of ensuring that a world remains in which they can all find a way to survive. They point to these alliances, including vampires coming to the aid of a caern under siege and driving away the minions of the Wyrm, as proof that such cooperation can, and will, succeed. 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

One of the deepest points of contention between the two factions is the Concordat’s willingness to challenge a fundamental aspect of Garou tradition: lineage. They continuously push forth the idea that leadership should be determined by ability, not by bloodline. The Shadow Lord leaders point to the many failings of Silver Fang royalty as an example that bloodline alone cannot guarantee good leadership—one’s actions and ability make her worthy of following. The other member tribes of the Concordat quickly highlight the abilities and talents of their own, demanding a chance to step forth from the shadow of lineage rule and prove they too possess the skills to lead Gaia’s children, not just a favored few. If Gaia continues to wish for the Silver Fang tribe to rule the Garou Nation, they ask, why does she remain silent? 

Another topic strongly dividing the two factions is the idea of balance. The Concordat of Stars believes that the Wyrm is a necessary part of the order of the world; the entropic aspect of the Triat must exist in order to allow for healthy entropy within the Material Realm and the Umbra. The Weaver’s webs have driven the Wyrm mad with pain, changing his entropic nature to corruption and decay. This taint prevents the Wyld from recycling what remains into new creations for the Weaver to form and fashion. It is not the Wyrm itself that needs to be defeated; instead, the corruption that has overtaken the Wyrm must be rooted out and destroyed, so that it can return to the role for which Gaia created it in the first place. Some Concordat members advocate the destruction of the current incarnation of the Wyrm, allowing the Wyld to forge a new Wyrm, untainted by madness, restoring the balance. Others believe that some ritual or artifact may release the madness haunting the Wyrm, allowing it to once more see clearly its purpose and resume that function. None know for sure the right course of action, but all seek to find the way to restore Gaia’s original vision, before it is too late. 

## **The Tenets of the Concordat of Stars** 

- Strength comes from unity of purpose. 

- Balancing the world brings balance to the Triat. Balance within the Triat allows Gaia to survive. 

- Leadership belongs to those with ability, not lineage. 

- The Bitten are allies by choice, never by force or coercion. 

- All of Gaia’s children must come together now, if there is to be any hope of a future. 

## Independents, Rebels, and Iconoclasts 

The factions are not monolithic forces within the Garou nation; indeed, a minority of each tribe has either refused to join either the Concordat of Stars or the Sanctum of Gaia, or they have disobeyed their Elders by joining a faction they’ve condemned. Tensions are thick between the two groups, but the threat of annihilating the Garou Nation prevents open warfare. Some packs have split because of this new ideological rift, but love and kinship often overcome political divisions, and most packs have endured. 

Packs with mixed tribal membership bear most of the stress caused by the political differences between the Concordat and the Sanctum. While the bonds of pack run deep, werewolves are passionate and emotional creatures, and political arguments are always waiting to happen. This tension has caused a growing number of packs to focus on shared ideological leanings and tendencies, with perhaps the addition of an independent or rebel from a different tribe. Shared experiences and goals, however, have made for the strangest of bedfellows, with Silver Fangs and Shadow Lords bound together by a shared pack totem and the goals of the Garou Nation, leaving arguments about how those goals should be accomplished for those rare moments of peace between missions. Each pack deals with this political divide in its own way, and the packs must come to an understanding if they have any hope to survive the dangerous and frequent missions they undertake. 

Out of all the tribes of the Garou Nation, only one as a whole has firmly held itself back from joining in the political divisions of the Sanctum and Concordat. The Silent Striders’ tribal leadership declared its neutrality when the factions first formed, publically claiming that the tribe’s role as messengers between the septs required it to remain unaffiliated with any one political camp, lest it be unable to fulfill its duties. Privately, many werewolves sneer and suggest that this reason is not the only cause of the Silent Striders’ neutrality, or even the most important one, and that the lone wanderers are far too focused on their own eternal quest to regain their homelands to be willing to step into the vicious political arena of Garou. Whatever the true reason, none can question the need for the news and messages delivered by the Silent Striders, most importantly those missives too dangerous or secret to be delivered through means that could be intercepted or overheard. Leaders within both the Sanctum and the Concordat have individually discussed the idea of their faction assisting the Silent Striders in completing their goals, in order to sway them into an alliance that would earn a majority on the Council of Tribes, but so far neither faction has accomplished the feat. While individuals may choose to join an affiliation, the Silent Strider tribe as a whole remains focused on its own political goals. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## **Camp Defector** 

Characters wishing to join a faction opposite the majority of their tribe must purchase the Camp Defector merit. While characters may have different opinions or leanings from the camp their tribe supports, they cannot be official members of the opposing faction without purchasing this merit. 

## Conflicts and Grudges 

## The King Versus the Council 

For centuries, the Garou Nation has had a High King, a Silver Fang scion who has dedicated his life to guiding the nation through difficult times. Traditionally, the High King and the Council of Tribes have had a beneficial symbiotic relationship. The High King relies on the advisors of the Council of Tribes to lend their voices when difficult decisions must be made, as well as disseminate his orders to the tribes and ensure they are obeyed. Likewise, the Council of Tribes realizes the tribes cannot survive this war or carry out the will of Gaia on their own, and its members rely on the High King to assist them in forging the tribes together as one nation. It is rare that the tribes find themselves in consensus regarding what must be done or how to best accomplish their goals, and the combined leadership of the High King and the Council of Tribes offers guidance and a clear path for the nation going forward. 

In an ideal situation, the High King seeks out and heeds the advice of his Councilors, using their insights to make decisions that affect the Garou Nation as a whole. He relies on the tribal Councilors to deal with the day-to-day matters of their own tribes, and he acts as a neutral voice to help settle disputes between two or more tribes. The council handles the minor issues that come before it, working together to come to equitable decisions that do not require the direct intervention of the High King. 

However, werewolves are creatures driven by passion and rage, and this relationship rarely works as intended. Should either party wish it, they can make the process much more difficult for one another. For instance, the High King has the ability to issue orders to specifically punish or affect one tribe, and unless that tribe’s Councilor is able to rally a unanimous response to overturn the edict, they all must suffer the dictate of the King. On the other hand, the council can bring every single issue to the High King, no matter how minor, ensuring he has little time for anything else. Or they could choose to cut him out of their dealings entirely, seeking to solve every item on their own, leaving the High King to appear as though he is nothing more than a figurehead. 

Any of these outcomes make life more difficult for the nation as a whole, further dividing its people as they struggle to wage war against the Wyrm. The loss of the High King and the advent of the Age of Apocalypse caused chaos among the Council of Tribes and the nation as a whole. The council has been forced to step up and perform the duties of both positions, whether they like it or not. The rise of a new scion will tell the true testament of the council’s actions—will it be content to release the reins of leadership, or will it struggle to keep hold of the power to which it has grown accustomed? 

## The Council of Tribes Versus the Septs 

While the High King and the Council of Tribes handle tribal politics and sweeping decisions that affect septs all over the world, the leaders of these septs face their own challenges when dealing with their people and handling the decrees from the leaders of the nation. Although Sept Leaders are ultimately accountable to the decisions made by these leaders, within their own protectorates, they have nearly unlimited authority. This situation may cause schisms when it comes time for a leader accustomed to absolute rulership of an area is urged to obey the commands of a Councilor they have never even met. 

Of course, Sept Leaders are still werewolves and part of the nation, and they are also beholden to the authority conferred by tribe, Rank, and position. This reality does not mean they always agree with the decisions made by others regarding their septs, particularly when said decisions are made by someone who has never set foot in their territory. In such cases, it is not uncommon for Sept Leaders to find unique ways to handle the orders they are given. Some Sept Leaders strive to never call attention to their people, handling their issues in-house and presenting a successful façade to any who might report to the Councilors or the High King. Others believe the more involved they are in the greater politics of the nation, the easier it is to handle interference from the leaders. Those who ascribe to this philosophy ensure they remain in close contact with any who might hold influence in the nation’s halls of power. No matter the route Sept Leaders choose, or even if they cleave their own way between these two extremes, it is their responsibility to guide their people through the potentially treacherous political waters of the nation. Sometimes the advice of a clever Ragabash or a quick-witted Philodox is all that stands between a sept’s safety and disaster, when it comes to the churn of the nation’s politics. 

Sept Leaders sometimes attempt to ignore or refuse orders passed down to them. In the massive political machine that is the Garou Nation, it’s possible that by the time the upper echelons realize their order wasn’t obeyed, it is no longer important, or it might be forgotten entirely. However, 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

it’s also possible the implied insult will not be forgiven or ignored, and powerful sanctions could descend on the offending sept and its leader. These sanctions might be as simple as calling on a nearby werewolf to investigate the sept and learn why the commands were refused. Or a Councilor might ostracize an entire sept, refusing them access to Moon Bridges between their shard and prime caern until the edict is obeyed. Regardless of the outcome, when insubordination in any form is discovered, few Councilors are willing to sit idly by while a Sept Leader refuses to submit to those higher in station. 

Ever-increasing Wyrm-sourced corruption and the loss of so many caerns has ratcheted up paranoia within the nation. Septs that refuse to obey the edicts of the High King or the Council of Tribes are suspect, and they may find themselves drawing the attention and ire of one or more of Gaia’s Legends. Few welcome the attention of werewolves known for slaughtering hives by themselves or taking on nexus crawlers, particularly when such a Garou might come to the conclusion that the simplest solution to bring a recalcitrant sept in line is to remove its leader and allow another to take her place. 

has since seen Fera joining packs and finding themselves welcome in some sept territories as allies or even friends. 

Fera may choose to undergo the Rite of Acceptance and receive membership within a sept. These shapeshifters are then considered members of the Garou Nation, with all the rights and responsibilities that entails. They are expected to serve as any Garou of their Rank and auspice would, though they often find they suffer more suspicion and distrust than most. Garou often treat these Fera as possible allies; however, wariness bred through generations is not so easily set aside. The Fera feel much the same, as they find themselves surrounded by those who slaughtered their ancestors. 

Not all Garou find the inclusion of the Fera to be a positive change for the nation. Fear and suspicion runs rampant regarding the true motives of the Gurahl, and some believe that the Fera seek homes in Garou septs for the sole purpose of stealing shard seeds, now they have been successfully grown. Others feel this alliance is the first step in reuniting all of Gaia’s Changing Breeds into an unstoppable force united against the Wyrm. The truth behind the fledgling alliance remains to be seen, and only time will tell if it will endure. 

## The Fera Within the Garou Nation 

Perhaps the most significant change within the nation since the loss of Albrecht has been the relaxation of the distrust previously shown to the Fera. The Concordat of Stars brought this issue to the forefront through their work with the Gurahl, developing shard seeds. This action, which is seen by many as the one thing that allows the nation to continue, has led many Garou to believe that perhaps the labels applied to Fera in the past have been more propaganda than truth. 

The issue regarding the Fera was brought before the Council of Tribes for discussion. Even some members of the Sanctum of Gaia agreed that Fera were potential allies the Garou could no longer afford to dismiss. Although the vote was close, the council approved giving Fera standing within the Garou Nation. This decree was the first step on a road that 

## Ronin: Outcasts of the Garou Nation 

Werewolves are inherently pack creatures. Even the gathering of packs into septs for defense and protection is indicative of their fundamental need for social interaction. As such, to many Garou, there is no greater punishment than being declared _Ronin_ . Some Garou choose death or suicide over the thought of being sundered from werewolf society for the remainder of their existence. 

The Rite of Ostracism, used to cast a Garou from her sept for a short period of admonishment, provides only a taste of the loss and loneliness that Ronin experience. The true pain comes when a Councilor or the High King declares a werewolf Ronin from her tribe or the entire nation. On a sept level, banishment is temporary; once punishment has been served, the offender is welcomed back into the embrace of her family and sept, with the hope that her 

**
## **Fera, Politics and Sept Positions** 

Fera can attempt to challenge for any lesser sept position, such as as Warder, Den Parent, or Master of Challenge. However, they cannot become the Sept Leader or a member of the Auspice Council inside of a sept of the Garou Nation. Some Fera, such as the Bastet, are pushing to have their own Breed Councilor, but this change would require the unlikely vote of a majority of those already on the tribal council. Fera cannot join the Sanctum of Gaia or the Concordat of Stars. 

**
Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

ordeal has brought about a reformation of character. For more information on the **Rite of Ostracism** , see page 544. 

When a Garou has been declared Ronin from her tribe, her predicament is much more serious. Unless her Councilor chooses to restore her position, she is for all intents and purposes dead to the tribe. At this point, a Garou has only three options: she can seek adoption into another tribe, forever giving up the chance to be reunited with her previous family; she can set out to restore her name and honor in the eyes of the tribe, and find a reason for her Councilor to welcome her back; or she can live the remainder of her life as Ronin, existing in a half-state. Without a tribe, she suffers the shame and humiliation of ostracism for the remainder of her life. 

The worst scenario by far is for the High King to declare a Garou Ronin from the nation. In this extreme instance, although this werewolf’s tribe may continue to recognize her existence, she is excommunicated from the benefits and safety that being a member of the Garou Nation provides. Others look down on her as untrustworthy or dishonorable, and they may fear the stain of her crime could spread to them as well. While her pack and sept may continue to accept her, they risk much in doing so. Any Ronin harbored by a sept risks putting her Sept Leader on the wrong side of a conflict with the High King. Such a position is not one many are willing to take without the best of reasons. The High King may also rescind this banishment, assuming the Ronin has done something to merit such an action, and in the absence of the High King the Council of Tribes can restore a Garou to the nation with a majority vote. 

For more information on Ronin, see **Chapter Nine: Social Systems, Monikers, Ronin** , page 424. 

## The Sept 

_“The septs are where the hearths burn the brightest. They are not merely the front lines in the battle against the Wyrm. They are our homes where we raise our children. They are the future. They are life itself.”_ 

## —Valka One-Eye, Jarl of the Sept of the Blood Fist 

A _sept_ is a community of Garou, their children, and determined kinfolk who have settled into a shared area of territory guarded by two or packs. Septs are founded near _peripheries_ , places where the Gauntlet that separates the Material Realm and the Umbra is thin, and spiritual energy known as Gnosis swells like an underground spring, just waiting to be tapped. The recent destruction of many great caerns resulted in a diaspora of the septs of the Garou 

Nation, as werewolves and their families were scattered and separated. The discovery of shard seeds and Gaia’s need for new caerns has inspired a renaissance of founding of new septs. The sept must always be on guard, ready to protect its holy sites from exploitation and desecration. 

Once, the tribes and their internal structures were the political backbone of the Garou Nation. Septs were considered to be the responsibility of their tribes, and it was rare for members of different tribes to work and live together. Necessity brought disparate tribes together; now, greater power and authority has come to rest in the local septs and the leaders that claim sovereignty over them. Although the tribal council has vast authority and, in theory, may call on septs to support its decisions and policies, the reality is that local leaders with potent cults of personality occasionally ignore their edicts in favor of local needs and demands, risking punishment. Advocates for sept-strong policies argue that their people and their communities must come first, as they are not simply pieces on a chess board to be maneuvered by faceless leaders they have never met. 

Septs serve many vital purposes besides the paramount protection of caerns. The sept acts as a vessel for Garou culture, preserving legends and traditions as Elders pass on and packs leave, never to return. Werewolves rarely grow old gracefully; those who have lived long enough to achieve the honor of Elder Rank rarely have the stamina or inclination to travel or quest. These Elders settle in septs, lending their formidable but fading strength to protecting septs and making their experience and wisdom available to those who would learn from them. Packs of young Garou act as the eyes, ears, and fists of their Elders, roving between caerns on simple errands or missions of vital importance. Septs are also important waypoints for traveling packs, where these travelers can meet with both fellow wanders and with locals to share news of their travels. 

## **Smaller Septs and Larger Responsibilities** 

In the wake of the diaspora of the Garou Nation, it is not uncommon for a notable werewolf to hold the responsibilities of multiple lesser offices, when there are not enough abled bodies to fill all of the roles needed to get the job done. For example, a Warder sometimes assumes the duties of a Den Parent and a Master of the Challenge. A werewolf can only hold the Abiding Renown from a single position. However, it is considered an act of hubris and scandal to claim multiple positions when there are willing and able Garou available to carry the burden. Neither the Sept Leader nor the Council of Auspices can accept the additional responsibilities from other positions. If there isn’t a werewolf to fill those roles, the sept simply keeps those positions vacant until a suitable candidate can be found. Over countless millennia, the 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

**
## **Concolations** 

When the collective wisdom and strength of two or more septs are needed, they come together in a Concolation, or grand moot, as a celebration of their unity. Woe be to the Wyrm when its forces must face the combined forces of several septs. (For more information, see **Chapter Nine: Social Systems: Moots: Concolations** , page 433.) 

**
Garou Nation established a dogmatic culture of common protocols, laws, and revered offices required to honor their ancient pact with the spirits and the governance of a sept. The young rage against the unforgiving nature of life at the septs, arguing that only by changing tactics can the Garou ever hope to defeat the Wyrm. Elders caution that only by staying the course has the Garou Nation been able to maintain the seemingly eternal battle. The majority of werewolf society has simply come to accept the harsh reality of this rigid culture as a fait accompli to maintain the support of spirits and survive in the Age of Apocalypse. 

The recent division regarding accepting Weaver spirits as equals to the Wyld in the battle to restore or resurrect Gaia has sparked new political factions—the Sanctum of Gaia and the Concordat of Stars. Hope and faith burn brightly, but divisively, and the natural response of werewolf society has been a return to the old ways as a shield to protect the Garou Nation from splitting. This vigorous reverence for the old ways has, so far, kept the political battles to a bloodless field, granting power to those who can best promote the security of the septs and the Garou. 

## Sept Leadership 

## Sept Leader 

_“A leader inspires through strength, confidence, and occasionally terror.”_ 

- —Long Fang, Shadow Lord, Master of the Sept of the Restored Fallen Skies 

Werewolves who claim sovereignty over septs and responsibility for the welfare of their members and protectorates are known as _Sept Leaders_ . They are more than the rulers of a sept; they are the heads of a civic government, spiritual leaders to their people, matriarchs and patriarchs of vast families with domestic disputes and quarrels, and commanders in chief during times of war. 

The Sept Leader carries a sacred obligation to be a living embodiment of the best ideals of the Garou and inspire a new generation. The old tales draw a connection between 

the spiritual worthiness of a Sept Leader, the health of the land, and the prosperity of the sept. Sadly, the reality doesn’t always match the ideal, but a wise Sept Leader can elevate her flock and lead the sept to greatness. 

While she is advised by the Council of Auspices, the Sept Leader is the final authority when it comes to her sept. As the leader, it is her responsibility to declare when the sept is at war, to govern it in peace, and to ensure that it is protected from the machinations of the Wyrm. The role of Sept Leader comes with material benefits conferred by the Litany: the First Share of the Kill for Greatest in Station. A Sept Leader often claims the best territory and receives tribute from those under her protection, but she pays a steep price for such glory and honor. 

Sept Leaders must be able to withstand the challenges of those who would seek to displace them, while still retaining the loyalty of those who serve the sept. They must protect their fellows with their lives and secure the sept from any threat. As rulers, they are forever apart from those they must safeguard. They stand over their friends and families in judgement for crimes against the Litany as well as against their own laws. Above all else, Sept Leaders monitor the health and cohesiveness of the sept to ensure that its members remain vigilant in their collective duty to combat the Wyrm and defend Gaia. 

A Garou becomes a Sept Leader by defeating her predecessor in a ritualized challenge vetted and approved by the Master of the Challenge. If she falls in battle, then her duties fall to the Warder until the sept’s Council of Auspices names a new Sept Leader via unanimous vote, or until another defeats all potential challengers. 

## **The Sept Leader’s authority includes:** 

- Interpreting and enforcing the Litany within the protectorate of the sept 

- Approving new members into the community 

- Exiling the undesirable or dangerous 

- Dividing the spoils of war and territory of the protectorate 

- Sanctioning breaches of sept law 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

**
## **Tribal Titles and the Sept Leader** 

By long-standing tradition, from the era when septs were mostly tribal affairs, Sept Leaders often take tribal titles to distinguish themselves. Here are some of the commonly used titles for Sept Leaders, delineated by tribe: Matriarch (Black Fury), Pappa/Momma (Bone Gnawer), Shepherd (Children of Gaia), Righ (Fianna), Jarl (Get of Fenris), Director (Glass Walker), Alpha (Red Talon), Master (Shadow Lords), Herald (Silent Striders), Lord (Silver Fangs), Luminary (Stargazer), Caretaker (Uktena), and Trailblazer (Wendigo). 

**
- Performing the Rite of the Hunt 

- Awarding and punishing those under her jurisdiction by assigning greater and lesser monikers 

- Issuing orders and mandates related to the security and protection of the sept to anyone within the protectorate 

## **A Sept Leader gains the following Abiding Renown:** 

- 1 Glory and 1 Honor 

## **A Sept Leader can spend Renown in the following manner:** 

She can expend a single trait of Fleeting Renown (of any type) to assign any greater or lesser moniker to a member of her sept or a shapeshifter caught within the sept’s protectorate. This action can be overturned by a majority vote of the Council of Auspices. 

## **Glory** 

Sept Leaders can spend 1 trait of Glory Renown to enact one of the following effects: 

- **Declare a Time of War:** Sept Leaders have the right and authority to declare a time of war for a period of 24 hours, after which it must be confirmed by the Council of Auspices. It is considered a sign of weakness to abuse this ability for minor threats. 

- **Law of Solomon:** Sept Leaders can temporarily delay any challenge between members of their sept for up to 24 hours or until those members complete a specific task assigned by the Sept Leader. This task cannot take longer than one week to accomplish. This effect cannot be used to delay a challenge for the Sept Leader’s position. 

- **Word of the Alpha:** Sept Leaders can give an order to any member of their septs. If this order is refused, the Sept Leader may elect to grant the moniker 

_Dishonorable_ or _Cowardly_ . If her target succeeds at the assigned order, she gains the moniker _Worthy_ . This ability is suspended if the Sept Leader is challenged for her position. (See **Chapter Nine: Social Systems: Monikers** , page 424.) 

## **Honor** 

Sept Leaders can spend 1 trait of Honor Renown to enact one of the following effects: 

- **Glowering Eye:** A Sept Leader can declare any single action a violation of the Litany within the protectorate of her sept. When this happens, the Truthcatcher is obligated to begin proceedings to judge the accused (see **The Sept: The Litany and the Sept, Crime and Punishment** , page 528). If she does not, she loses 1 point of Willpower for the next 30 days. 

- **Restoration:** Sept Leaders can restore the honor of a single Garou by removing lesser monikers, such as _Dishonorable_ or _Cowardly_ . 

- **Weight of the Crown:** Sept Leaders can veto a single decision made by a member of the Council of Auspices. That councilor may attempt to overrule her Sept Leader’s veto by calling for the council to vote. If the council’s decision against the Sept Leader is unanimous, then the councilor’s decision stands. 

## **Wisdom** 

Sept Leaders can spend 1 trait of Wisdom Renown to enact one of the following effects: 

- **Council of the Wise:** A Sept Leader can summon forth the Council of Auspices and mandate that they give her advice or render judgement on any matter she presents to them. Those who fail to appear within one week lose 1 point of Willpower for the next 30 days. 

- **Mercy of the King:** A Sept Leader can grant clemency to any member of her sept for any crime. 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

This act overturns any judgement issued upon them, save for those issued by the High King or the Council of Tribes. However, the Council of Auspices can overturn their Sept Leader’s mercy via a unanimous vote to reinstate the criminal’s original punishment. 

- **Law of the Land:** A Sept Leader can issue an edict that interprets the Litany for her sept. Her interpretation can be overturned by the Council of Auspices via a unanimous vote. 

## Warder 

- _“Glory is for the young without responsibilities. The war is here, protecting this fragile shard seed from the world.”_ —Thaddeus “Wired Heart” Fitzgerald, Child of Gaia 

_Warder_ is a word synonymous with trust and duty within the Garou Nation. A werewolf who claims such a position is entrusted with the sacred obligation to ensure the protection and maintenance of a caern. She may never leave the boundaries of the bawn, so long as she holds this vigil. Instead of wandering, she spends her time establishing and organizing the defenses of the caern, preparing contingency plans to deal with any attack. A wise Warder develops methods of summoning reinforcements, plans safe escape routes for non-combatants, and ensures that an ultimate fallback is available if the sept is ever overrun. Even the Sept Leader will retreat before a Warder gives up her post. 

The wave of attacks on the world’s caerns that marked the beginning of the Age of Apocalypse slaughtered the previous generation of Warders. Tales of those nights say that Warders banded together to serve as a valiant rearguard, ensuring that the populations of old septs were able to escape the destruction and eventually rebuild the nation. Galliards solemnly sing dirges of the heroes who surrendered their bodies so that the spirit of the nation could endure. Heavily influenced by the examples of those who came before them, the new generation of Warders consider themselves ephemeral guardians prepared to give their own lives to ensure that this flame continues to burn. 

A Warder’s devotion to her duties must take precedence over all other political or personal loyalties. Her paramount concern must always be the protection of the caern. The holder of this office must be above reproach and beyond any hint of corruption. For these reasons, many Warders choose to live as lone wolves, refusing to force a pack to limit its travels from the caern and its bawn. Packmates of those Warders who choose to live among other Garou often comprise the inner circle of the Warder’s Guardians, supporting the Warder in her duties. 

In smaller septs, where there are not enough worthy Garou to take the responsibilities of lesser sept offices, the Warder also assumes the duties of the Den Parent and the Master of the Challenge. In such cases, a Warder frequently seeks to train young werewolves to fill these roles as soon as possible, allowing herself the time she needs to devote to the caern. 

The Warder often works closely with the Sept Leader, since they share a territorial claim over the protectorate. If the protectorate is home to a shard caern, this partnership becomes doubly important, as it is the responsibility of the Warder to safeguard the shard seed. However, the Warder’s powers are limited. She cannot declare war parties or set missions against the Wyrm, though she can decide who must stay to defend the protectorate when others go to fight. 

As the chief guardian of a caern, a Warder is otherwise separate from the authority of the Council of Auspices. In matters regarding the security and safety of the caern and its bawn, the Warder has complete command, even over the Council of Auspices, so long as she holds office. However, should the Warder fail in her duties or be unable to perform her required tasks, she may be voted out of office by the Council of Auspices. Any member of the sept who demonstrates her worthiness to the Master of the Challenge may challenge the Warder whenever the caern is not in immediate danger. If the Warder falls in battle, the Sept Leader can appoint a temporary replacement to hold the post until the Council of Auspices confirms a new Warder. 

(For more information on Caerns, see **Chapter Twelve: Caerns,** page 553.) 

## **The Warder’s authority and duties include:** 

- Organizing the security and protection of the caern and its bawn 

- Empowering Guardians to protect the caern 

- Vetoing any decision that threatens the security of the caern or its bawn 

- Enlisting any Garou within the protectorate in active defense of the caern or bawn, no matter their position or Rank 

- Protecting the shard seed at any personal cost, even death 

- Using any force necessary to defend the caern 

- Exiling undesirables from the caern 

- Holding immunity from prosecution for actions taken in defense of the caern 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

- Determining who is allowed to commune in the caern to regain Gnosis 

## **A Warder gains the following Abiding Renown:** 

- 1 Honor and 1 Wisdom 

## **A Warder can spend Renown in the following manner:** 

## **Glory** 

Warders can spend 1 trait of Glory Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Call to Arms:** A Warder can call forth the defenses of the caern to fight the enemies of the sept and protect it from harm. She may reward those that come to the caern’s defense with 1 trait of Fleeting Honor Renown. 

## **Honor** 

Warders can spend 1 trait of Honor Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Sacred Responsibility:** The safety of the caern is the Warder’s responsibility. She can overturn any decision made by any sept position, including the Sept Leader, if she believes that it endangers the caern. If the Warder’s target fails to comply, she gains the moniker _Dishonorable_ . (See **Chapter Nine: Social Systems: Monikers** , page 424.) 

## **Wisdom** 

Warders can spend 1 trait of Wisdom Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **A Word to the Unwise:** Warders can ban someone from the caern who they believe endangers it. When a Warder’s target fails to comply with this ban, the target gains the moniker _Reckless_ . (See **Chapter Nine: Social Systems: Monikers** , page 424.) 

## Guardians 

Anointed by the Warder, _Guardians_ protect the caern and its bawn from any threat. They are tasked with assisting the Warder in patrolling the bawn, guarding the shard seed, and any other duties required to safeguard the caern. It will take many generations for the current crop of shard caerns to properly mature into great caerns. Until then, the Guardians serve as living shields, keeping vigil over shard seeds and preventing minions of the Wyrm, or even other Garou, from stealing it. 

Guardians hold a place of honor in any sept they serve. Their duties sometimes restrict their freedom to roam and seek glory and Renown, but a steadfast Guardian knows that she is contributing to the future by maintaining her watch and defending the bawn against possible incursion. A wise Warder organizes a sizeable pool of Guardians to keep them patrolling in shifts, allowing them to seek glory outside of their vigils. When the Wyrm becomes so bold as to attempt a direct assault upon a caern, the Guardians have the chance to prove why they hold such a sacred trust. 

## **A Guardian’s authority and duties include:** 

- Free use of the caern heart or shard seed 

- Immunity from prosecution when acting under the Warder’s orders 

- Exemption from other duties while protecting the caern 

## **A Guardian gains the following Abiding Renown:** 

- 1 Honor 

## Den Parent 

_“I knew your mother, Cub. She died a Legend. Kept the rest of us alive when the sky was nothing but fire and ash. Refused to fall, even when her body was nothing but broken bones and pain, until she knew you were safe. She asked me to help you become the sort of man she could be proud to carry her name. We’re gonna do that together, no matter how much it hurts.”_ 

—End of Sorrow, Den Mother, Child of Gaia 

Few duties in the Garou Nation are as paradoxically touched by both sorrow and hope than that of a _Den Parent_ . The Garou fight an endless battle where victory comes at the cost of blood and, eventually, their very lives. The only comfort for a parent heading into danger is the knowledge that the Den Parent of her sept will ensure that any orphaned Cubs and kinfolk are raised properly, with love and security in the arms of the sept. A Den Parent must watch over the children of their fallen friends and allies, ensuring that the next generation is prepared to fight the Wyrm. 

Werewolves commonly accept the wisdom that it is the responsibility of the entire sept to work together to raise and prepare Cubs for their lives as werewolves. A Den Parent coordinates family members, teachers, Elders, and specialists into an educational regiment that prepares the young in her care for their difficult lives as Garou and for the war that awaits them. Den Parents must learn to teach Lupus, Metis, and Homid youths of vastly different tribes and auspices about their upcoming roles in Garou society. 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

Their goal is to educate the Cubs about what it means to be Garou, the differences and responsibilities of the auspices to which they were born, and the laws of the Litany. Whenever a Den Parent cannot teach something herself, it is her responsibility to direct those Cubs to other teachers who can best continue their specific education. 

Den Parents serve as the shepherds of the young, protecting the Cubs and kinfolk of the sept. Nothing will bring the Garou of a sept boiling forth ready to battle swifter than the beleaguered cry of a Den Parent for aid. Since the start of the Age of Apocalypse, Den Parents have also been saddled with the responsibility of ensuring that kinfolk are not Bitten without explicit permission, as there are werewolves too shortsighted to understand that such actions risk the future. Den Parents guard the local kinfolk from harm and mistreatment. They have the power to punish anyone who harms or kills a kinfolk, and Den Parents can only be overruled by a vote of the Council of Auspices in such matters. 

## **The Den Parent’s authority and duties include:** 

- Ensuring the training and protection of the local Cubs 

- Policing and guarding the sept’s kinfolk 

- Judging the Rite of Passage for all Cubs 

- Keeping the sept’s linage records 

- Drafting any member of the sept once per 30 days to instruct Cubs 

- Assigning Cubs to assist a higher-Rank Garou of the sept in exchange for instruction 

## **A Den Parent gains the following Abiding Renown:** 

- 1 Wisdom 

## **A Den Parent can spend Renown in the following manner:** 

## **Glory** 

Den Parents can spend 1 trait of Glory Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Protect the Cubs:** Den Parents can call forth the forces of the sept to defend the Cubs and kinfolk. Those who ignore this summons may be punished later, losing access to 1 point of Willpower for the next 30 days. After the threat is over, the Den Parent may choose to reward those who served well by granting them each 1 trait of Fleeting Wisdom Renown. 

## **Honor** 

Den Parents can spend 1 trait of Honor Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Sacred Responsibility:** Teaching the young is the duty of all members of the sept. Den Parents can assign any member of the sept to instruct a Cub or Cliath on a single gift, rite, or lore. In exchange, the student must perform tasks to make her teacher’s life easier, loaning her a downtime action. (For more information, see **Chapter Eight: Dramatic Systems, Downtime Actions** , page 387.) The Den Parent can then reward both the teacher and the student 1 Fleeting Renown trait of her choice, if she feels that they have earned it. 

## **Wisdom** 

Den Parents can spend 1 trait of Wisdom Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Guardianship:** Den Parents can assume personal responsibility for any Cub or kinfolk, directly transferring the vulnerable into their custody. Furthermore, they can assign the moniker _Dishonorable_ to anyone who they believe has harmed a Cub or kinfolk. (See **Chapter Nine: Social Systems: Monikers** , page 424.) 

**
## **It Takes a Village: Connecting Students and Teachers** 

The Den Parent can assign any member of the sept to teach a Cub or Cliath a gift, rite, or lore. In exchange, the student must perform a task for the teacher, providing her with a bonus downtime action to be used within the next 30 days. In addition, the Den Parent can assign the resources of the sept (see **The Sept: Workings of a Sept, Sept Benefits** , page 526) towards any particular pursuit that seeks to educate or safeguard the sept’s Cubs or kinfolk. Such a request might involve instructing Cubs about the Umbra, teaching a gift, or sharing battle experience. 

**

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## Master of the Challenge 

_“Only strength properly tested can be considered reliable during a battle.”_ 

—Jitendra “Walks the Storm” Mosau’u, Uktena 

The legends surrounding purity of a challenge lie close to the heart of the Garou Nation. Perhaps this tradition is why a _Master of the Challenge_ is among the most honorable and important positions within a sept. Her sole duty is to oversee and sanction all challenges that occur within the boundaries of the sept’s protectorate. If the Garou of the sept lose faith in this system, it leads to bloodshed and violence that weakens the community. 

Often, Philodox naturally gravitate into this position, but the reality is that any Garou of significant reputation can take up this duty. Often those werewolves reaching old age serve in this role in order to still contribute to the health of the sept without burdening it. The Master of the Challenge is expected to stay out of the sept’s politics and be a neutral observer. 

The Master of the Challenge has the authority to deny the legitimacy of any challenge, to determine the legality of any challenge’s terms, and to verify and authenticate the outcome of any challenge within her dominion. No formal challenge can take place without the sanction of the Master of the Challenge. As such, her word is final regarding any challenge she oversees; only when she makes her decision is a challenge considered truly over. 

It is considered a sign of respect to notify the Master of the Challenge before confronting your rival. Those wishing to advance in Rank must first contact the Master of the Challenge and request a proper trial that fits her schedule in order to do so. This process allows the Master of the Challenge to identify a Garou of the appropriate auspice and Rank to assist her, even if she must find such a worthy Garou in another sept. Often, the Master of the Challenge will aid other septs in this capacity, as her schedule allows. 

If there has been a breech in terms or a violation of the Litany during a challenge, the Master of the Challenge determines a suitable punishment. If a loser refuses to acknowledge the winner’s victory, the Master of the Challenge may also elect to administer a punishment. If a winner refuses to accept an honorable surrender, the Master of the Challenge may choose to reverse the outcome and punish the winner. 

The Council of Auspices can override a decision made by the Master of the Challenge with a unanimous vote. If it uses this privilege more than twice, the Master of the Challenge typically resigns in disgrace, lest the Fool call her scandalous. If forced, the Master of the Challenge can be removed by vote of the Council of Auspices, during 

which time the councilors may also elect to demote the Master of the Challenge in Rank. 

Some less-honorable Sept Leaders have attempted to hide behind the Litany and escape challenges to their position through the declaration of a time of war. In these instances, the Master of the Challenge has the unique ability to overrule the Sept Leader’s declaration and allow challenges to her leadership to take place. 

For more information on challenges, see **Chapter Nine: Social Systems: Challenges** , page 426. 

## **The Master of the Challenge’s authority includes:** 

- Overseeing all challenges by either personally witnessing them or appointing trusted judges to witness them on her behalf 

- Determining the terms of physical fights 

- Monitoring contests and declaring winners and losers 

- Setting when and where a challenge will take place 

- Determining if a Rank challenge is valid 

- Connecting with other septs to procure appropriate judges for challenges 

## **A Master of the Challenge gains the following Abiding Renown:** 

- 1 Honor 

## **A Master of the Challenge can spend Renown in the following manner:** 

## **Glory** 

A Master of the Challenge can spend 1 trait of Glory Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Halting a Challenge:** A Master of the Challenge can delay any challenge for up to 30 days, until she has time to judge the matter. This ability cannot be used to delay challenges issued to the Master of the Challenge herself. 

## **Honor** 

A Master of the Challenge can spend 1 trait of Honor Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Punish Cheaters:** A Master of the Challenge has the authority to punish cheaters for violations of the rules of engagement and the Litany by assigning them the moniker _Dishonorable_ . Furthermore, she may make 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

recommendations to the Truthcatcher, requesting that her target face formal judgement. 

## **Wisdom** 

A Master of the Challenge can spend 1 trait of Wisdom Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Invalidating a Challenge:** A Master of the Challenge can invalidate the outcome of any challenge that occurs within her sept’s _protectorate_ , so long as she states a plausible reason for doing so. 

## The Council of Auspices 

The _Council of Auspices_ is the governing legislative body of a sept, whose members serve as advisers to the Sept Leader. It is their responsibility to help handle the day-today work involved in the smooth management of sept life. They also offer counsel and advice on decisions the Sept Leader may need to make, and they handle tasks that she delegates to them. 

The five preeminent members of each auspice comprise the Council of Auspices in every sept. Each particular auspice holds a specific title and set of responsibilities. Anyone who holds a position is expected to put the benefit of the sept before her own concerns or else find herself swiftly replaced. In theory, any member of a particular auspice can challenge for the auspice’s seat on the council, if the Master of the Challenge deems her worthy. 

The Council of Auspices is responsible for the creation of lesser laws known as _Sept Laws_ , which determine proper behavior within the sept’s protectorate. They help the Sept Leader determine how the Litany is interpreted locally and define exact methods of judgement and punishment. 

## **The authority of each member of the Council of Auspices includes:** 

- Creating lesser laws, known as Sept Laws, for behavior within sept territory 

- Removing a Garou from any sept position, except Sept Leader, by majority vote of the council 

- Overturning a decision from the Sept Leader by unanimous vote of the council 

- Possessing immunity from being appointed as Guardians by the Warder, or as teachers by the Den Parent, while they hold their positions. This immunity does not prevent them from electing to do so, merely from being forced by the Den Parent or Warder to do so. 

- Training and counseling the members of their particular auspice 

## **A member of the Council of Auspices gains the following Abiding Renown:** 

- 1 Honor and 1 Wisdom 

## **A member of the Council of Auspices can spend Renown in the following manner:** 

She can expend a single trait of Fleeting Renown (of any type) to assign any lesser moniker to a member of her auspice within the jurisdiction of the protectorate of the sept. This action can be overturned by a majority vote of the Council of Auspices. 

## **Glory** 

A member of the Council of Auspices can spend 1 trait of Glory Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Remove the Unworthy:** By majority vote of the Council of Auspices, the councilors can remove any sept officer from her position, with the exception of the Sept Leader. Before the vote is held, the councilors participating in the vote must each spend 1 trait of Glory Renown, regardless of its outcome. 

**
## **Rule by Council** 

It was once more common for septs to be governed by a Council of Auspices, sometimes referred to as an Elder Council. In such cases, a council has the same authorities and privileges as the Sept Leader; furthermore, when its members vote unanimously, and when each spends the appropriate Renown traits, they can achieve the same effects. (For more information, see **The Sept: Sept Leadership, Sept Leader** , page 512.) 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## **Honor** 

A member of the Council of Auspices can spend 1 trait of Honor Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Legislation:** By majority vote of the Council of Auspices, the councilors can issue a new interpretation of the Litany or a local Sept Law. Before the vote is held, the councilors participating in the vote must each spend 1 trait of Honor Renown, regardless of its outcome. 

## **Wisdom** 

A member of the Council of Auspices can spend 1 trait of Wisdom Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Judgement of the Council of Auspices:** By majority vote of the Council of Auspices, the councilors can overrule any sept position or authority on a single matter. Before the vote is held, the councilors participating in the vote must each spend 1 trait of Wisdom Renown, regardless of its outcome. 

## Fool (Ragabash) 

**
_Fool_ might be the title of the Ragabash seat on the Council of Auspices, but only the naïve take the meaning of the name literally rather than metaphorically. Many see the Fool as the voice of political dissent within the sept. She serves as the pressure valve by which competing dominant werewolves are moderated into making wise decisions about the sept’s future. 

The Fool teaches the Litany by challenging it and giving voice to the unspoken concerns of the sept. While engaged in the duties of her office, the Fool becomes exempt from the normal protocols that govern Garou behavior. She is immune to punishment from any sept office, but that does not mean that she has carte blanche to do whatever she wishes. A Fool who pushes the boundaries too often finds herself challenged by her would-be students or the victim of her less-official expressions of displeasure.
