The Fool serves as the devil’s advocate for the sept. She can accuse anyone, no matter her position, of a crime against the Litany, and the Truthcatcher must judge the matter. Such privilege does not come without risk. Should the accused be found innocent, the Fool must endure the punishment that her target would have suffered. Additionally, the Fool is tasked with ensuring that an accused receives a fair trial when judged by the Truthcatcher. She can appoint 

any Ragabash to serve as an advocate, helping prove the defendant’s innocence or pleading for mercy in the form of a lesser sentence on her behalf. 

Arguably, the Fool’s most important duty is to oversee the others of her auspice, ensuring that the Ragabash of the sept act in a manner that benefits its werewolves, not just themselves. The Fool directs her fellow Ragabash to learn information about the sept’s enemies, scout for new missions or opportunities, and test the sept’s defenses to ensure they are sound. 

## **The Fool authority includes:** 

- Accusing any member of the sept of a crime 

- Appointing a Ragabash to defend any Garou accused of a crime before the Truthcatcher 

- Holding immunity from prosecution for breaking any Sept Law or committing any lesser crime while she holds the office of Fool 

- Assigning Ragabash to investigate members of the sept for scandal 

- Assigning packs and individuals to locate and identify threats to the sept and its members 

- Assigning packs and individuals to gather intelligence on known threats to the sept and its members 

## **The Fool can spend Renown in the following manner:** 

## **Glory** 

The Fool can spend 1 trait of Glory Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Dare the Unknown:** The Fool can assign any Ragabash or pack with a Ragabash member to carry out a scouting mission into dangerous territory on behalf of the sept. If the Ragabash or the entire pack succeeds in the mission, the Fool can award all successful participants 1 Fleeting Renown of any type. 

## **Honor** 

The Fool can spend 1 trait of Honor Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Fool’s Right:** The Fool can escape social punishment for any lesser crime or offense for her words or pranks. She can reject an attempt to assign her a moniker. This ability can be vetoed by a majority vote of the remainder of the Council of Auspices. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## **Wisdom** 

The Fool can spend 1 trait of Wisdom Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Speak Truth to Power:** The Fool can accuse any member of the sept, including the Sept Leader, of a violation of the Litany. The Truthcatcher is obligated to try the accused or face the punishment for the crime. If the accused is found innocent, the Fool suffers the punishment. 

## Master of the Rite (Theurge) 

Garou are flesh and spirit bound together by Gaia to protect both the Material Realm as well as the Umbra. Their need for gnostic nourishment is great, and sometimes this drive leads to errors of judgement that offends the spirits. The Theurge seat on the Council of Auspices, known as the _Master of the Rite_ , ensures that the sept remains in harmony with the spirits. 

The Master of the Rite’s sacred duty is to monitor the spiritual health of the members of her sept, from the battleweary warriors and guardians to the Cubs learning how to handle life after their First Change. She must help replenish the totem that maintains the mystical energy of the caern. In pursuit of these responsibilities, she manages the spiritual debts of the sept by assigning Theurges tasks, such as ensuring proper chiminage is paid, performing rites, and maintaining spiritual pacts. With this duty also comes the responsibility to attend to the welfare of spirits themselves, ensuring that they are strong and ready to help in case the sept has need of them. This task involves everything from offering proactive chiminage, to building up the stock of favors the spirits owe to the sept and its members, to creating and maintaining environments on the sept’s protectorate that are inviting to spirits, encouraging their presence. 

The Master of the Rite oversees all rites performed within the protectorate of the sept, ensuring that spiritual pacts are maintained. Anyone seeking to cast a rite within the boundaries of a sept must first acquire permission from its Master of the Rite, who must determine the petitioner’s worthiness. It is completely within her discretion to allow anyone to cast rituals freely or prohibit someone from casting them as a punishment or due to lack of training. 

Practical concerns regarding the Material Realm also weigh heavy on the Master of the Rite. She coordinates with the Warder concerning the defenses of the caern and maintains an armory of talens for the Wyrmfoe to utilize. Such duties would overwhelm a single werewolf; therefore, in order to attend to so many obligations, the Master of the Rite can marshal other Theurges and assign them tasks. 

The Master of the Rite can grant special recognition to those who have served well by giving them the honor of casting a major rite for the sept or serving as one of her assistants. It is the responsibility of a Master of the Rite to authorize others as trainers, instructing those wishing to learn rituals and ensuring that all seasonal rites are performed correctly and successfully. 

## **The Master of the Rite’s authority includes:** 

- Assigning Theurges to monitor the performance of rites for the sept 

- Monitoring and reporting on the concerns of the spirits within the sept’s territory 

- Ensuring that Garou maintain good relations with local spirits 

- Overseeing and maintaining any unclaimed fetishes or talens within the sept’s armory 

- Ensuring that all fetishes created in the sept’s territory are done so in accordance with the spirits’ wishes 

- Informing the Truthcatcher of any violations of spiritual bans or failure to pay chiminage by members of the sept 

## **The Master of the Rite can spend Renown in the following manner:** 

## **Glory** 

The Master of the Rite can spend 1 trait of Glory Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Call to Arms:** You call forth the spiritual defenses of the caern to fight enemies of the sept and protect itself from harm. (For more information, see **Chapter Twelve: Caerns** , page 553.) 

## **Honor** 

The Master of the Rite can spend 1 trait of Honor Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Tarnished:** The Master of the Rite can punish any Garou within the sept’s protectorate for abusing spirits by burdening her with the moniker _Tarnished_ . 

## **Wisdom** 

The Master of the Rite can spend 1 trait of Wisdom Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Chiminage:** The Master of the Rite can assign any werewolf or pack a task that fulfils the chiminage requirements of the sept or honors a deal with the spirits. The Master of the Rite can 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

award 1 Fleeting Wisdom Renown trait to those who complete this assignment. 

## Truthcatcher (Philodox) 

Many Garou consider justice to be a matter of pragmatic action rather than theoretical philosophy. It is the role of the _Truthcatcher_ , the Philodox seat on the Council of Auspices, to ensure judgement comes from equal parts mercy, wisdom, and retribution. She enforces the application of justice as it applies to the Litany and Sept Laws within the protectorate of her sept. 

A Truthcatcher oversees the entire legal and justice system of a sept. It is her responsibility to determine whether those accused of breaking laws are guilty or innocent, and to provide punishment or chance of atonement to those who are found guilty. Clearly, in larger septs, this responsibility is too much for a single werewolf, so she can empower other Philodox to act as her deputy judges in specific cases. 

Mediation between packs of a sept falls under the jurisdiction of the Truthcatcher’s office. She performs all ceremonial binding promises, such as oaths, alliances, and geasa. Her office keeps records of punishment and scandal, working with the Talesinger to ensure that punishment is public, so that it can discourage others. Likewise, the Truthcatcher assigns redemption quests to those seeking to be forgiven for past sins and crimes against the Litany. 

- Witnessing oaths, promises, and other ceremonial bindings 

## **The Truthcatcher can spend Renown in the following manner:** 

## **Glory** 

The Truthcatcher can spend 1 trait of Glory Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Death Before Dishonor:** The Truthcatcher can assign a redemptive quest, of an appropriate difficulty, to a Garou who has been assigned a punitive moniker. Should the petitioner succeed, and the spirits believe she is worthy, her crime is redeemed and her moniker is removed. (See **Chapter Nine: Social Systems: Monikers** , page 424.) 

## **Honor** 

The Truthcatcher can spend 1 trait of Honor Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Law of the Land:** The Truthcatcher can issue an edict that interprets the Litany for her sept. This clarification can be overturned by the Council of Auspices via a unanimous vote held between its other four members. 

## **Wisdom** 

Atonement quests and tasks fall within the purview of the Truthcatcher and her assistant Philodox. Those Garou who show true remorse for their misdeeds, even while serving a punishment, can petition for or be offered the opportunity to atone for their actions. If the Truthcatcher accepts their request, they are assigned a task or quest, even possibly a geas, which they must complete in order to receive absolution for their offenses. Immediately after the Truthcatcher certifies the task’s completion, any ongoing punishments the werewolf suffers for her crime end. 

For more information on matters of judgement and crimes, see **The Sept: The Litany and the Sept, Crime and Punishment** , page 528. 

## **The Truthcatcher’s authority includes:** 

- Adjudicating and holding trial over violations of Sept Laws or the Litany 

- Appointing a Philodox to judge a Garou accused of a crime 

- Assigning redemption quests to restore a criminal’s honor 

The Truthcatcher can spend 1 trait of Wisdom Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Mercy of the King:** The Truthcatcher can grant clemency to any member of her sept for any crime. This act overturns the judgement originally issued against them. However, the Council of Auspices can overturn this mercy via a unanimous vote held by its other four members, levying the original sanction back onto the criminal. This process cannot be used to overturn justice issued by the High King or the Council of Tribes. 

## Talesinger (Galliard) 

Pivotal to Garou society, the myths and tales enforce a single conceit: so long as you are remembered, you cannot die. If this is true, then the Galliards who serve on the Council of Auspices, the _Talesingers_ , hold the keys to immortality. To be mentioned in song, to have deeds told near the hearth, is the closest that a werewolf can conceive of paradise. 

A Talesinger organizes other Galliards, and together, they ensure that the sept’s records are correctly kept, that the 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

laws of the sept are known to all, and that the histories and stories of the sept’s members are told and remembered. She tends to the Hall of Heroes, and she records the important deeds of the sept. Within the monument to the ancestors, the Talesinger is lord and master, ensuring the vital links to history are never in danger. When the sept faces threats, here the Talesinger stands as a bulwark against those who would defame history. 

She spreads tales of those under her charge to other septs and likewise collects information about the world. Often a Talesinger puts together an overheard comment or phrase, passing it along to the Fool, unleashing the Ragabash to learn the truth from these scraps and rumors. A worthy Talesinger keeps Elders well-informed of threats and victories that might be important to the defense of their sept, and she encourages Cubs to seek out adventure when they formally join the nation. She is responsible for assisting the Den Parent with the historical education of the sept, helping ensure that young Garou are taught all that they need to know to be successful when they join the nation’s ranks. 

Moots fall under the bailiwick of the Talesinger. She assigns a Galliard to organize and perform moots at sept functions and is responsible for ensuring that they are all run properly according to tradition and for the good of the sept. With the incredible importance surrounding the proper execution of a moot and its attendant events, the Talesinger is like a conductor overseeing her symphony, assigning positions to those best suited to them and ensuring the harmony of these important affairs. 

A Talesinger also guards against the insidious spirit-sorrow known as Harano. She assigns herself and her assistants to perform moot events to brighten spirits and learn the newest achievements of the sept’s members. She monitors packs for their emotional and mental health, taking steps to correct things if they begin to fall out of balance. 

## **The Talesinger’s authority includes:** 

- Keeping the sept’s records, including all Sept Laws, crimes, punishments, and celebrations 

- Battling Harano by inspiring morale in the sept 

- Recording the sept’s achievements in its Silver Record 

- Assigning Galliards to oversee moots 

- Overseeing and maintaining the Hall of Heroes 

## **The Talesinger can spend Renown in the following manner:** 

## **Glory** 

The Talesinger can spend 1 trait of Glory Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Mark of Punishment:** Once the Truthcatcher has passed judgement on a guilty party (see **The Sept: The Litany and the Sept, Crime and Punishment** , page 528), the Talesinger calls on spirits and the Garou Nation to assign any sept-level moniker to the target, revealing her crime. Any Garou who encounters the target for the duration of the moniker immediately knows of her crime and punishment. 

## **Honor** 

The Talesinger can spend 1 trait of Honor Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Deeds and Words:** The Talesinger can perform a song or tell a tale about the deeds of a werewolf or an entire pack. So long as the sept approves of the performance, the werewolf or pack gains 1 Fleeting Renown trait of any type. 

## **Wisdom** 

The Talesinger can spend 1 trait of Wisdom Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Measure of Sorrow:** The Talesinger can ask the spirits of the sept to take a measurement of Harano from a Garou within it. She doesn’t learn who might be infected with spirit-sorrow, but she learns the sept’s general state of emotion. 

## Wyrmfoe (Ahroun) 

If a Sept Leader is the monarch who rules a sept, the _Wyrmfoe_ , who holds the Ahroun seat on the Council of Auspices, is the general of the army that defends its protectorate. She serves as the primary tactician for the sept. 

The Wyrmfoe is the living embodiment of the sept’s rage, hunting creatures of the Wyrm and securing the protectorate. It is her responsibility to assign individuals and packs to war parties, supplementing the work of the Warder’s Guardians by assigning patrols for Ahroun around the protectorate. When a Sept Leader calls for a time of war, the Wyrmfoe leads the Garou into battle. 

One of the less-obvious duties of the office of the Wyrmfoe is to ensure that the defenses of the sept are well-stocked. 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

She keeps the sept’s mundane armory stockpiled and ensures that supplies are secured and food and water are plentiful. The Waystation is her dominion. It must always be ready to take on extra troops, house messengers moving between septs, and shelter the wounded after a battle. 

## **The Wyrmfoe’s authority includes:** 

- Assigning packs or individual sept members to war parties to combat specific threats 

- Assigning Ahroun and their packs to patrol the sept’s protectorate beyond the bawn 

- Clearing the sept territory of any Wyrm influences discovered 

- Assigning Ahroun to lead a hunt when called on to do so by the Sept Leader 

- Handling the logistics of the sept, ensuring food, water, and other resources are available 

- Overseeing the sept’s armory and maintaining the mundane supplies within it 

- Leading the packs to war when the Sept Leader declares a time of war 

## **The Wyrmfoe can spend Renown in the following manner:** 

## **Glory** 

The Wyrmfoe can spend 1 trait of Glory Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Glory is for Heroes:** The Wyrmfoe can assign a Garou or a pack a mission to fight the Wyrm. If they succeed in their mission, she can grant them 1 trait of Fleeting Glory Renown. 

## **Honor** 

The Wyrmfoe can spend 1 trait of Honor Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Call to Arms:** The Wyrmfoe calls forth the defenses of the caern to fight the enemies of the sept and protect it from harm. Afterwards, she can reward all of those who come to the caern’s defense with 1 trait of Fleeting Honor Renown. 

## **Wisdom** 

The Wyrmfoe can spend 1 trait of Wisdom Renown to enact the following effect: 

- **Heat of Battle:** The Wyrmfoe can halt any challenge until after an ongoing battle finishes and she and her war party return to the sept. Those who refuse to heed her wisdom lose half of their remaining Willpower, rounded up, for the rest of the game session. 

## Workings of a Sept 

## Establishing a Sept 

As pack creatures, Garou often band together in a central location in order to defend a caern and the territory surrounding it, an area called a _sept_ . The sept serves many purposes to those who have dedicated their lives to it, from providing a base of operations to offering a place of succor. These central locations are the cornerstones of Garou society, small bastions formed from a lifetime of working and fighting together. 

A new sept can be established in several different ways. Two or more packs might come together with the intention of cleansing and defending a periphery and its surrounding territory. Once they have defeated any lingering threats, cleansed the area of taint, and secured the location with defenses and shelters, they can petition a great caern for a shard seed. Alternatively, a group of packs may be awarded a shard seed for their work together, which they must defend while they seek a suitable place to plant their new caern. This scenario rarely occurs, as there is great danger in having an unplanted shard seed. The risk of the seed falling into the clutches of the Wyrm or being destroyed is high, and few are willing to risk such a rare and fragile artifact. 

Perhaps the most important step in creating a new sept is choosing where it should be established. As every sept is, at its core, a defensive shell built around a caern, werewolves prize easily defensible areas. A shard seed must be planted within a cleansed periphery, and the sept must be established in the surrounding area. This location can be found within a Weaver-infested city or a Wyld-dominated woodland, and each location offers its own unique benefits and challenges. 

Claiming territory for a sept in a Wyld-oriented area offers the safety of anonymity. Here, a shard seed can be planted in a place where few humans will have a need or desire to go in the first place. The lack of human population makes it easier to protect the Veil, ensuring that it remains unbroken during attacks or missions. This location often forces defenders to rely on standard methods of protection and security, such 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

as regular manned patrols, traps and snares, or spiritual defenders, rather than more modern security systems. In this day and age, a Wyld-aligned sept may draw attention when outsiders begin to question why so many people are living together in isolation. 

Alternatively, claiming a territory for a sept in a heavily human-populated area can bring its own host of difficulties. Cleansing the area sufficiently to allow a shard seed to be planted, obtaining ownership or an official reason to have people at the desired location, and explaining why no one is allowed in certain areas are all problems that must be dealt with in order to ensure the safety of a shard caern within an urban area. These disadvantages are balanced by easy access to urban centers providing high-tech security and to the everyday resources that allow a community to thrive. 

In order for a sept to be successfully formed, a minimum of ten Garou are required to ensure the area is not in immediate danger of failure or destruction. This requirement allows most important positions to be filled by individual werewolves, and it ensures the sept can run in a stable manner while still having enough members to perform off-site missions and keep the sept running. In small septs, multiple positions are often held by a single Garou until the sept has grown. In these circumstances, it is common to call on kinfolk and tribemates to help support the fledgling sept, bolstering its numbers and increasing its defenders. 

The final act of consecrating a sept is planting its shard seed. Once this act has been accomplished, a spirit is summoned to serve as the totem for the newly born caern. Those present make a pledge, binding themselves together as an official sept under the auspices of that totem spirit and in the name of the shard seed. From this point forward, the duty to see to the succor and success of the new shard caern lies in the hands of those present. 

## Life Inside of a Sept 

After a Garou experiences her First Change, her life is irreparably altered. In most cases, she cannot return to the life she once led. Often, the sept becomes the only home she has left, and the people she shares it with the only family on which she can count. While some werewolves, particularly Glass Walkers and Bone Gnawers, try to maintain a connection to their human or lupine lives, most Garou turn to a sept and its territory to sustain and shelter them. 

Garou who live within a protectorate are expected to contribute both to the protection of the sept and to the needs of their fellow occupants. Many werewolves learn skills that benefit not just themselves, but also everyone else 

who makes a sept their home. Hunting and crafting abilities, both mundane and mystical, are highly prized, as they help support the needs of families and those who are unable to leave the sept’s territory. 

While many Garou are unable to hold down a regular nineto-five job, they have found other means of earning money and providing necessities for their protectorates. The rise of the internet and online sales has facilitated sept life to a large degree, allowing Garou to sell crafted items and purchase necessities without having to set foot off the sept. There are also those who—even when not patrolling or serving as an active Guardian for the sept—help watch the territory’s boundaries, dissuading humans from wandering in. Others serve as lookouts, watching for anything strange wandering near the sept’s borders. 

Garou and kinfolk who reside on sept lands also take on much of the responsibility for raising and training the young. Due to the frantic and dangerous life of a warrior, many Garou parents rely on kinfolk and others to help raise children to know what is expected of them and what their future roles will be. As such, Garou also expect those who are not busy to assist with the education of all, not just their own children and mates. This expectation also extends to training young Garou Cubs and Cliath, helping them become stronger assets to their septs. It takes a village to raise a child, but it takes a sept to train a werewolf. 

## Sept Size 

The geographical size of a sept is based on the strength of its shard seed and on the number of Garou bound to the caern through its totem spirit. A shard seed can increase in power if there are enough Garou to support it, and if the seed itself has matured by gathering sufficient gnostic energy to increase its strength. However, shard seeds are fragile things, and there are a number of factors that can lead to the decline of a caern’s strength. Losing too many Garou or having to uproot the seed and replant it in another territory are two ways a shard seed may be weakened or destroyed. 

Any sept without a shard seed, no matter the size of territory claimed or the number of Garou present, remains a level 1 sept until a seed is obtained and planted into the land. Once this event happens, the process for growing the sept’s power can begin. 

The Storyteller determines the number of Garou and kinfolk PCs and NPCs at a sept, then calculates that sept’s level. Once the sept’s level has been determined, the size of the sept dictates a number of advantages and disadvantages its members receive. 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

||**Sept Level**|**Minimum Number**<br>**of Garou**|**Territory Size**|**Protectorate Size**||
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
||1|10|1/3rdof a mile or less|Area around bawn||
||2|15|1/3rd- 2/3rdof a mile|Small town||
||3|20|2/3rd- 1 ¼ miles|Large town||
||4|30|1 ¼ - 1 ½ miles|Small city||
||5|40|1 ½ miles or more|Large city||

## Sept Benefits 

The strength of a sept is reflected in the benefits it offers to those bound to it. Below are advantages common to all septs: 

- **Support Staff:** For each level of the sept, kinfolk, shared influences, and unsuspecting humans assist it when directed. The Sept Leader can assign these assistants to whomever she chooses each month. Each level of support staff provides 1 extra downtime action each month. When a sept is higher than level 1, these levels can be divided among multiple werewolves or assigned as a whole to one Garou. 

- **Spiritual Defenders:** Spirits that make a sept their home receive energy from the gnostic power that flows through their sept. The Stock NPC rating for the common spiritual defenders of a caern is equal to the sept’s level. 

However, being bound to a powerful sept can have disadvantages that come from residing where gnostic energy is too great to be disguised. These disadvantages are listed below: 

- **Obvious Power:** The growing spiritual power of the shard seed acts as a beacon to anyone capable of perceiving these energies. Anyone attempting to discover the location of a sept through supernatural means receives a wild card bonus equal to the sept’s level on Investigation, Occult, or Awareness skill challenges. 

- **Second Cousin Twice Removed:** The larger a sept becomes, the harder it is to know those you share it with well. Enemies attempting to infiltrate a sept receive a wild card bonus equal to the sept’s level on Subterfuge skill challenges to deceive others about their identity. 

## Sept Geography 

All septs and the territory they claim share certain distinguishing features, no matter their size or location. 

Each sept has a _territory_ and a _protectorate_ . The territory is comprised of everything within the physical and spiritual boundaries of the sept itself. A sept’s territory encompasses the living and working area of the sept. It’s the land where the Garou live, and it serves as a base of operations for the werewolves of the sept. All of the important locations required for a sept’s maintenance is located within this territory. Guardians patrol this area, and the Warder claims its boundaries as her responsibility. A sept’s territory holds everything from cabins for housing Homids to wild Lupus dens, alongside the challenge pit and caern heart. 

The _protectorate_ is the area the sept claims as its responsibility. A protectorate always encompasses the sept’s entire territory, but it may also include a greater geographical area. An urban sept, for example, may claim a city park and its surroundings as its territory, but that same sept’s protectorate could include the entirety of the city itself. In the wilderness, a sept might claim a valley as its territory and the entirety of the forest as its protectorate. Travelling within the protectorate of another sept does not infringe on the Litany, but it can be considered disrespectful to not at least request permission when knowingly doing so. Entering the territory of another sept without invitation or announcement, however, is a serious crime indeed. The perpetrator can swiftly find herself before the sept’s Truthcatcher, assuming the Warder and her Guardians feel merciful regarding such trespass. 

Without these boundaries, a sept is incomplete. Once they are established, prominent packs or individuals often create shrines within their sept to honor their totem spirits for their patronage. These shrines can be anything from elaborate altars and decorations to simple wind chimes hung among the trees, giving wind spirits something to enjoy. Different septs often designate various areas for their challenge mounds. Some create elaborate, fenced pits and 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

provide plenty of seating for witnesses, while others go with a simple area marked as a meeting place. Customs vary between septs and often reflect their traditions and their Sept Leaders’ backgrounds. 

The following locations are commonly found within a sept: 

- **Caern Heart:** The most intensely guarded and defensible area of the sept is its _caern heart_ , where its shard seed is interred. Septs without a shard seed still maintain this location in preparation for the day they are gifted a seed. More than any other location, this place is the Warder’s domain and responsibility. Her permission alone grants others access to this sacred space. Only the most trusted and respected visitors are allowed access to a caern heart of another sept, and even they are closely watched while present. For more information, see **Chapter Twelve: Caerns** , page 553. 

- **Hall of Heroes:** Where the memories of the fallen are kept, this place is a constant reminder that glorious deeds and honorable actions immortalize Garou. Nothing speaks more to the history of a sept than the _Hall of Heroes_ within its territory. This place carries the stories of those who have died; holds displays of significant items from the past, including fetishes, weapons, and armor; and hosts Gatherings for the Departed when it is time to mourn. Garou often enshrine new relics here, telling tales of the warriors who bore them and reveling in their victories, while mourning their loss. The hall is also a shrine for ancestor spirits. Werewolves perform ceremonies here to seek the protection and guidance of those who have come before, and sometimes, very real answers are obtained. Ancestor spirits are not merely a focus of belief, but they are also actual spirits that may manifest in times of need to offer advice, teach gifts, or aid in the defense of the sept. It is the Talesinger’s responsibility to ensure the maintenance and upkeep for this important facet of Garou society. 

- **The Waystation:** This location provides succor to the wounded as well as any respected visitors at the sept. This place is a combination armory, inn, and hospital, serving the needs of werewolves. It is a gesture of respect to be given access to a Waystation, as it is here that the Wyrmfoe stores the supplies necessary for continuing both offense and defense against the Wyrm. Everything from food and water supplies, spare weapons and armor, and medical supplies are stored here in anticipation of their need. The Waystation serves as a hospital for wounded who need supervision and cannot recuperate unattended. It houses messengers and trusted guests who are visiting the sept or passing through. Finally, it provides a defensible location at which Garou rally in 

case the sept itself comes under attack, and it serves as a bunker for Cubs, kinfolk, and any non-combatants who may otherwise be in harm’s way. 

- **Lupus dens and Homid housing:** A sept’s territory also includes homes. While some may choose to live outside the boundaries of the sept’s territory, every sept maintains enough space to house all of its members. In case of a time of war, or if the sept finds itself under constant siege from its enemies, it cannot afford to have its defenders worrying about where they will safely sleep. While some dwellings are utilitarian in nature, suitable for housing anyone who may need a place to sleep, most are customized by those who occupy them to be true homes for the sept’s community. Even in urban septs, it is not uncommon to find warehouses or even apartment complexes transformed into living quarters perfectly comfortable for Homid or Lupus occupants. 

## War and Leadership 

The Garou Nation is a society at war. Werewolves live and breathe the desperate fight against the Wyrm and its minions, while struggling against sorrow at the inevitable losses they experience. As such, when a Sept Leader declares it to be a “time of war,” the event is truly significant. This declaration is commonly made when nothing less than the marshalling of all the sept’s resources is required in order to accomplish the task in front of them. 

A Sept Leader pronounces a _time of war_ under one of two conditions: when the sept’s territory is besieged by physical enemies, or when a mission must be undertaken that is so significant that it will take a majority of the sept’s resources to complete. Some less-honorable Sept Leaders have attempted to hide behind the Litany and escape challenges for their position through the use of such a declaration. In these instances, the Master of the Challenge has the unique ability to overrule the Sept Leader’s declaration and allow challenges to her leadership to take place. 

During a time of war, the leadership of the sept remains in the hands of the Sept Leader. However, the logistics of gathering individuals and packs becomes the responsibility of the Wyrmfoe. With the Wyrmfoe’s input, the Sept Leader develops a strategy for the sept and its members, leaving final tactical decisions in the hands of the Wyrmfoe. This division of duties allows a Sept Leader to continue to direct the sept while the Wyrmfoe takes direct command of packs and individuals, forging them into a weapon intended to clear away the threat. 

Once the threat has been removed, the Sept Leader officially declares the time of war ended, allowing the sept 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

to return to its normal state of affairs. Sept Leaders who attempt to keep the sept in a state of war and readiness are often warned to be sure that need remains, as failure to swiftly deal with a threat may indicate that the fault lies with the leader, not the enemy. When a sept is no longer in a declared time of war, challenges may resume, and those who took up additional or different responsibilities return to their normal duties until they are needed once again. While there is no true peace for the warriors of Gaia, these times are known as a _“time of peace,”_ showing a clear distinction from those times when the werewolves go to war. 

## The Litany and the Sept 

Generations have followed the guidance of the Litany in order to conduct their lives, relying on these codified rules to govern the most important and vital aspects of Garou society. Even in a society that follows an oral tradition, where meanings change as they are repeated, these laws are so important that they have been preserved through the ages. However, the choices regarding how to enforce these rules has changed as time has passed and the cultures in which the Garou live have evolved. Galliards and Philodox argue and rail over the changing meanings of the times and even of the very language itself. As such, it falls to the leaders of sept and nation to determine how to enforce Gaia’s most ancient laws. 

A Sept Leader establishes expectations for law within her sept. While changes to the interpretation of the Litany applicable to the entire Garou Nation must be handled by the members of the Council of Tribes, a Sept Leader is well within her rights to decide how enforcement of the Litany is handled within the boundaries of her power. These lesser laws are known as Sept Laws, and they can vary wildly between septs. A Council of Auspices also has the ability to create Sept Laws and see to their enforcement. This power can be wielded at the delegation of the Sept Leader, or if there is no Sept Leader, the Council of Auspices can assume her responsibilities until a new leader emerges. 

While a Sept Leader may choose how they are interpreted, she cannot invalidate the laws set forth in the Litany. For example, a Sept Law cannot make it permissible for Garou to mate with Garou, but it can stipulate the punishment for such breaches. For example, a sept with a Red Talon Alpha might only impose an Ostracism Rite on those found guilty of eating the flesh of man, while the same sept run by a Silver Fang Lord might levy the Satire Rite, or even death, for such a transgression. 

These interpretations are not the only ways Sept Laws are used. They are also set forth to delineate the behavior a Sept Leader expects and allows within her protectorate. Such 

laws can cover a wide array of topics, from curfew times for access to the caern heart, to how many Garou may be gone from the sept territory at any given time. These laws help ensure that a Garou dwelling on sept territory understands the boundaries of behavior without the need for constant challenges and Staredowns, which may prevent her from accomplishing her duties. 

A wise Garou immediately inquires about the Sept Laws of any territory she visits, as they can differ wildly between Sept Leaders. Ignorance is no excuse when it comes to the laws that govern a protectorate, and while it may buy leniency from some, lack of knowledge is not accepted as a defense. As such, it often falls to the Council of Auspices and their assistants to ensure that those newly arrived to the sept are informed of the laws the Sept Leader has set down. After all, an outsider’s failure to know Sept Laws reflects as poorly on the one who failed to inform the guest as it does on the guests themselves. 

## Crime and Punishment 

The Litany is the framework upon which the Garou have built their society. This set of laws shapes the behavior expected of all members of the nation. When these laws or the Sept Laws that descend from them are broken, it falls on those chosen by Gaia and Luna to punish the transgressors, ensuring that this framework does not falter. In order to handle these situations, Sept Leaders, Councils of Auspices, and the Council of Tribes have developed a loose guide regarding how crimes are judged and punished within the Garou Nation. 

## **Trials Among the Garou** 

1. **Accusation:** When a Garou is accused—whether by her own admission, the findings of a clever Ragabash, or by declaration of a sept official—the presiding Truthcatcher determines whether the crime was committed in violation of the Litany or the laws of the sept. When one of the tenets of the Litany is broken, the matter immediately proceeds to the Truthcatcher for trial. When a Sept Law is broken, the Sept Leader has the right to sanction or pardon the violation as circumstances warrant. If the Sept Leader chooses to exercise clemency, the crime is forgiven, and no further action is required. If she does not, then the case is given to the Truthcatcher for trial. 

2. **Assembling the Court:** Once in the hands of the Truthcatcher, a process begins to ensure the matter is handled quickly. The Truthcatcher decides whether she will judge the case herself or hand it over to another Philodox to judge. If an assistant is chosen to judge a 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

trial, then for the duration of the trial, she speaks with the voice and authority of the Truthcatcher. The Truthcatcher then calls upon the Fool to either speak in defense of the accused, or to appoint another Ragabash to do so. The Talesinger also stands witness, or appoints an assistant, in order to ensure proper records are maintained for the sept. The witness is responsible for communicating any change in Renown or Rank to the sept and nation as required. Finally, the Wyrmfoe stands as champion for the court, or appoints an assistant to do so. The champion stands against any challenges brought forth during the course of the trial. 

3. **The Trial Proceedings:** Once the positions for a trial have been filled, it can officially begin. When Garou law is invoked, the process is swift and final. The Truthcatcher asks questions to be answered by the Fool, the accused, or any witnesses that may have seen the crime unfold, and the Truthcatcher considers all evidence presented to her. Then, she renders judgement and sentences punishment if required. At this point, the Sept Leader can step in on matters of Sept Law and overrule the Truthcatcher’s judgement. Doing so has its own dangers, and this right is rarely exercised. The Truthcatcher may challenge the Sept Leader’s right to overrule her judgement. If successful in her challenge, the Truthcatcher proceeds with the original punishment. 

4. **Outcome:** The accused is either exonerated or found guilty and incurs punishment for her crime. If she is exonerated, she is free to go and the incident is treated as if it never happened, with no stain on the honor of the accused. If she is found guilty, there are 

a variety of punishment rites that may be chosen as suitable punishment, depending on the severity of the crime. Garou society is not lenient and has no time for weakness; in the case of some heinous crimes, the only suitable punishment is death. If the punishment rendered is execution, the Sept Leader has one final option: the ability to allow the Rite of the Hunt. Doing so grants the offender a final opportunity to be remembered with honor, wiping away her transgressions when she dies at the hands of the hunters. 

## Examples of Punishments Among Garou 

## **Severe Punishments** 

These punishments are reserved for major violations of the Litany, endangerment of a caern, or for those who willfully and intentionally violate Sept Laws. 

- **Rite of the Hunt:** When the sentence is death but some honor remains, this ritual execution grants purification and a restoration of lost honor to the convicted as soon as they are deceased. 

- **Death:** In some cases, a crime is so serious that nothing less than death will suffice to atone for it. When a crime is so great that no honor remains, Garou do not hesitate to kill the offender. 

- **Satire Rite:** Being stripped of Rank is not only a loss of status, but also a powerful marker of shame, calling into question the target’s ability to be trusted with responsibility. 

**
## **Rite of the Hunt** 

A punishment that can only be levied by a Sept Leader or the High King, the Rite of the Hunt is a death sentence. The Rite of the Hunt is used in one of two ways. A Sept Leader may grant this ritual as a form of respect for a Garou who has been sentenced to death, providing an opportunity to restore her honor in death and be remembered without disdain. It can also be levied on one who deserves death but is not in the custody of those who can execute such a sentence. In these cases, a Sept Leader or the High King announces to those under her authority the name and identity of the one hunted. This pronouncement serves the purpose of alerting the one marked so they may choose to return for judgement and honorable death, as well as unleashing the rest of the Garou to hunt down one who has escaped punishment. Those marked receive the moniker _Hunted_ . 

For more information on monikers, see **Chapter Nine: Social Systems: Monikers** , page 424. 

For more information on Rite of the Hunt see **Rites: Rite of the Hunt** , page 545. 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## **Moderate Punishments** 

Unintentional violations of Sept Laws and pushing the boundaries of the Litany without breaching them are examples of moderate offenses. 

- **Ostracism:** This rite serves as a reminder of what a criminal might lose if she continues to fail to follow the behaviors expected of werewolves. Losing access to the community and family of pack and sept is often more than enough to scare a wayward Garou straight. 

- **Stone of Scorn:** Nothing helps reinforce the punishment for wrongdoing more than being constantly reminded of the shameful act you have committed. This punishment allows a Truthcatcher to assign any greater or lesser moniker as a punishment. 

## **Minor Punishments** 

Personal insults, minor infractions of Sept Laws, and failure to complete assigned tasks through negligence are all minor offenses that may be punished in the ways listed below. 

- **Voice of the Jackal:** High-pitched squeaks replace the offender’s voice, warning others and serving as a reminder that words carry as much weight as deeds. In a society with a core tenant of honor, even the pettiest of insults can have far-reaching ramifications. In such cases, the Truthcatcher can assign any lesser moniker as a punishment. 

- **Sept Duty:** Being prevented from leaving sept lands and unable to participate in missions that offer the chance to gain Renown and acclaim is often a suitable reminder about acceptable behavior. 

## The Pack 

_“Are there times when the Lucky Birds want to murder each other? Only every day we’re alive. But we’ve literally been to Malfeas and back. We love each other. And I swear, every time that Johnny Three-Bones gets us in dutch with the sept Elders, I ask myself a simple question: is it really a violation of the Litany to put him out of our misery? Yet, we always stand by him. Why? We’re the Lucky Birds, and we stand together.”_ 

— Charlie Whitehorn, Pack Leader of the Lucky Birds 

The pack is the cultural frame through which players experience the world of **Werewolf: The Apocalypse** . Wolves hunt and live together in packs due to instincts evolved throughout several millennia, but it is a mistake to dismiss a pack as an expression solely of a werewolf’s animalistic nature. Humans also evolved to form communities, huddled 

around fires in the dark nights long ago. Werewolves might be the apex predator in the ecosystem, the strongest of Gaia’s warriors, but many jackals can take down a lion with the right amount of patience and luck. 

A Garou pack is more than a collection of individuals working together for survival. It is a gestalt born of instinctual drives and a purpose—cemented into a spiritual, communal marriage of willing souls dedicated to each other. Living as a werewolf means more than simply surviving. It also means connecting to the world and sharing the enduring pain and responsibility of being Garou among your brothers and sisters in arms. 

In the Old Tongue, packmates are called _Anamae_ , or soulfriends, roughly translated to mean those who are your family by choice. This idea is a powerful concept for Garou, who are often cast out when they experience their First Change, exiled from the homes and the lives they knew. A werewolf pack is a family bonded by choice through a living totem: a spirit that represents the inner nature of this spiritual communion. Packmates are kith and kin; they are a werewolf’s immediate family, best friends, and brothers-inarms all in one. 

Elders often teach Cubs that the strength of a wolf lies in her pack. One wolf can be strong, but a pack can become legendary. Packs are the foundation of Garou society, typically ranging in size from three to twelve members, often with each of the five auspices represented. However, this is not always the case, as fate or circumstances sometimes requires desperate measures. 

Bonding and politics between packmates can lead to brilliant stories about close relationships and bloody sibling rivalries that explode under the pressure of external enemies or close quarters. Garou from rival tribes with centuries of hatred might become _Anamae_ over time. The war might turn bitter and mighty nations may fall, but a pack always sticks together. Survival in the face of the Wyrm depends upon the unity of the pack. 

## Pack and Totem 

A _totem spirit_ is the unifying force that truly changes a group of diverse Garou working together into the family that is a pack. Serving as both a guide and a protector, the chosen spirit imbues pack members with a portion of its essence, granting them abilities beyond those available through gifts or rites. No pack can be truly complete without the unification of Garou and spirit enabled by a totem. 

When choosing a totem spirit to represent a pack, players should work with their Storyteller to decide the pack’s goals. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

This decision helps players select a spirit that most closely represents those goals. For example, a pack dedicated to war and hunting down the enemies of Gaia might be best represented by Fenris Wolf or Griffon, while a pack that scouts and strikes where an enemy is weak would be better supported by Rattlesnake or Owl. 

This choice also helps the Storyteller decide what benefits the totem grants to the members of the pack, choosing from the list presented in **Chapter Four: Character Creation** . While each player may choose a different totem benefit from the list, the choice of the spirit totem itself gives the Storyteller a baseline from which to create the pool from which players will select. 

This choice also gives the Storyteller ideas for goals the spirit may have, as the bonds of pack patronage go both ways. Just as the characters rely on the totem spirit to grant them unique abilities and power their gifts, the spirit also demands aid from time to time in return. Missions like these and other interactions between the characters and their totem spirit can add remarkable depth and drama to any story. When characters are forced into scenarios where their desires and the totem spirit’s goals differ, deeply important character choices are made with long-lasting consequences. 

For more information on creating pack totems, see **Chapter Four: Character Creation, Step Eight: Assign Additional Backgrounds, Background Descriptions, Totems** , page 201. 

## The Taxonomy of Werewolf Packs 

A pack is a deceptively elegant social organizational tool that can be leveraged in a diverse selection of circumstances. Garou possess the intellect and creativity of humans merged with the loyal instincts of wolves. They are a fusion of both species, so their social needs are more advanced than those of either species alone. 

Biologists use taxonomies as a means to scientifically identify, classify, and study animals in nature. This section examines 

a werewolf pack from that perspective and outlines their classifications, life cycles, and common structures. 

A pack is defined by more than its stated goals. The purpose of a pack and the events that brought its members together forever defines its flavor and being a part of it affects each individual member. The combination of shared purpose and the means by which a pack is formed determines the pack’s classification. 

Some of the most common pack classifications are as follows: 

## Motif Pack 

Motif packs are among the most common types of packs in the Garou Nation. This type of pack consists of a group of Garou who have a collective, driving goal or an idea they wish to accomplish or uphold together, and who have convinced a totem to sponsor them in its pursuit. This driving goal becomes an overwhelming obsession in their lives. They may go so far as to name themselves after the bane of their enemies and decorate their flesh in sigils and symbols to curse their foes. Their ambitions are typically open-ended—kill all vampires, protect humans from the Wyrm, or defend the Wyld. Such tasks are often impossible to achieve, and the pack spends a lifetime advancing its cause, until a new generation of Garou rises to take its place. 

## Destiny Pack 

A destiny pack is a collection of Garou champions who are specifically selected by a mighty totem spirit to complete a dangerous task considered vital to the interests of the Garou Nation and Gaia. These tasks might include things such as defeating a terrible enemy or stopping an insidious plot of the Wyrm. Such a mission is always voluntary, due to the extreme danger involved. These packs are sometimes nicknamed “hero packs.” Being selected to join one is always considered worthy of Renown, as the odds of survival are thin at best. Destiny packs rarely have the time to form complete bonds; indeed, their alliances are often temporary. 

**
## **Coloring Outside the Lines** 

The following information is intended to be a creative guideline for building a pack. The options provided here are the ones most commonly occuring among Garou, but players can and should get creative when building their packs. While all packs should have a unified purpose or style as represented by their totems, how they achieve that structure is wholly at the pack’s discretion. Players define a pack type and may choose options from any section that fits their needs. Alternatively, they may create their own pack types and roles that match the unique flavor of their pack and spiritual totem. 

**
Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

The most famous destiny pack is the Silver Pack, which is chosen when needed by the totem Phoenix to complete a mission vital to the interests of the Garou Nation. 

## Branded Pack 

A branded pack is a collection of fallen Garou seeking redemption in the eyes of Gaia, the spirits, and the Garou Nation. The spirit of a branded pack is almost always a spirit of redemption, such as Unicorn. A Garou’s motivation to join a branded pack comes from deep-seated shame. Her crime was not a minor violation of the Litany, but a severe failing that has brought scandal to the werewolf or her family. A Garou who has sired a single Metis might be forgiven without joining a branded pack if he shows the proper contrition. A Garou who sired half-a-dozen Metis without care brings horrific shame upon himself and his tribe, and the only redemption available to him might be serving within a branded pack. The nature of the sin depends largely upon the auspice, tribe, and character of the Garou in question. For example, the Get of Fenris consider cowardice on the field of battle to be an especially horrific sin, particularly for an Ahroun, while another tribe might not believe that such cowardice—while shameful—merits joining a branded pack. 

A single sin against the Litany does not condemn the entire pack. Garou expect that the pack leader will handle the affairs of the pack, including its punishment. But a pack that fails to properly punish one of its members who has brought unforgivable shame on them suffers from the same scandal. After all, some say, if they lived in close quarters and spiritual union with her, why couldn’t they stop her? 

Few worthy Garou allow their packs to endure the stigma of their personal shame. They become lone wolves until they find a way to redeem themselves in the eyes of Gaia and other werewolves. The problem is that lone wolves have a tendency to get into too much trouble on their own. How can you trust a criminal Garou in your territory when she already failed her former pack? Sometimes, a hero must fall to her lowest point before she can find redemption. Often, these fallen Garou seek each other out, knowing their redemption lies together, as they try to complete a near-impossible mission. If they fail, then at least no worthy Garou have died. If they succeed, they find redemption. Some Elders will form a branded pack from a collection of lone wolves near their sept who have previously failed. Such a pack functions as a support group for its members and helps them help themselves by giving them a common purpose to work towards. Likewise, some Garou refuse to leave their packmates, despite scandal and shame, and their presence transforms the pack into a branded pack. 

## Tribal Pack 

A tribal pack forms when a number of Garou wish to embody one of the stated virtues of their tribe and seek out patronage from one of their tribal totems. Once, this type of pack was common, but now, the thinning of the Garou population and the loss of historical territories has discouraged this type of pack. The common wisdom among the Garou Nation is that differing perspectives from various tribes are properly balanced for interpreting Gaia’s will. Tribal packs are considered old-fashioned by many of the young, but they are considered to be quite prestigious within the confines of the tribes, particularly in the eyes of their older members. Certain tribal packs are chosen by the Elders of the tribe to act as champions for the tribe. They are called on to defend the tribe against aggression from within the Garou Nation as well as from outside threats. Outsider tribes tend to view such packs with a healthy dose of suspicion and even scorn, if they consider the tribe unworthy. 

Many tribal packs tend to organize themselves to ensure that they represent all of the auspices and that they have as many different perspectives as possible, exemplifying both the tribe and the Garou Nation. Most tribal packs are generational packs, with members of diverse ages and Ranks. The seasoned veterans take care of the young and pass along their wisdom to the next generation of warriors fighting for Gaia and the tribe. 

## The Life Cycle of a Pack 

A pack is a living organization with a complete life cycle. It comes into being, grows into a mighty force, and then settles down to procreate to ensure the propagation of the next generation. Each stage of this life cycle has unique expectations and responsibilities within werewolf society. 

## The Youngbloods 

“Youngbloods” is the common nickname for a pack of newlyminted Cliath who have endured the challenge of the Rite of the Passage, earning their totem. They heard the call to adventure and are ready to make their mark upon the world; however, they are still inexperienced and often require the mentorship of the sept’s Elders. Eager Youngbloods often take enormous risks to prove their worth to local authorities, but more often than not, they get themselves into trouble. 

Garou expect those of higher Rank to provide an occasional helping hand to mentor Youngbloods. Experience is earned through blood and tears. Garou do not coddle their young, but they do watch and protect them when it is required. Often, new recruits find such treatment overbearing and resent the interference. Seasoned veterans try to watch 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

from a distance, but won’t hesitate to step in at a moment’s notice to interrupt a mission with a last-minute rescue if it means preparing the young for the battles of the future. 

Veteran packs and Elders of the sept shepherd Youngbloods until they have the experience and wisdom to stand on their own. However, such protection and mentorship comes at a cost. Youngbloods are expected to defer to seasoned packs and to perform assigned tasks when the veterans are busy elsewhere. Tasks can be anything from fighting minor banes, patrolling the caern’s bawn, or seeking out ritual components for an Elder Theurge. 

Naiveté eventually gives way to experience, and the young Garou grow into their place in the world. If they are lucky and survive, they evolve into Wayfarers. The first task given to a pack who has outgrown its callow “Youngbloods” status is to mentor a junior pack while assuming their duties as Wayfarers. 

## The Wayfarers 

Once a pack has found its footing and has outgrown the Youngbloods stage, it is expected to contribute to the success of the local sept and eventually the Garou Nation with blood and sweat. Packs that attain this adult status are called “Wayfarers,” because the pack is expected to travel anywhere in the world, and to the Umbra if necessary, to get the job done. Wayfarer packs are the backbone of the Garou Nation, and the highest stage of the life cycle most packs achieve. Elders expect them to serve on the front lines, taking up important missions in the war against the Wyrm. 

Wayfarers defend caerns, seek out the secret plots of the Wyrm, and battle horrific terrors in dark and dangerous shadows of the world. The cost of victory is often measured in the blood of heroes, and few packs age past this stage with their packmates intact. The life of a Garou sometimes burns bright and intense for a short time, only to be snuffed out in one terrible moment. 

A few packs survive the horrors of the world and start to settle into a family life, raising children and potential Garou. As they do so, they become Generational packs. 

## Generational Packs 

The fight continues, even when soldiers begin to settle into family life and assume responsibilities that prevent them from being traveling warriors. Once a Wayfarer pack has fought its battles and earned its glory, the time comes to protect caerns, pass along wisdom to the young, and nurture Cubs. Generational packs defend the bawn of caerns and protect the septs that shelter the very young and the old. 

Generational packs are not retirement homes for werewolves. Packmates are expected to hold their own in fights and to help administer the resources of local septs and caerns. These packs are the glue of werewolf society, ensuring there is a home for Wayfarers to return to. Such tasks might not be the most glorious, but they keep the world turning. Generational packs take care of thousands of little details required for Garou to keep the Wyrm from completely taking over human society. 

For elderly werewolves, this group is often the last pack they join, as they seek to be useful until the ends of their lives. Generational packs help these Garou maintain a purpose until they creep too far towards violating the Litany. Rather than simply killing one of their own, many packmates ensure that an ailing Garou has the opportunity to battle the Wyrm once last time, rather than be put down like an animal. 

## Pack Organizational Structures 

Garou society tends to be conservative. Many of the cultural traditions and the Litany itself come from the experiences and consequences of events that occurred thousands of years ago. Yet, they are still considered relevant and vital in the dark days of the Apocalypse. Correspondingly, it is perhaps surprising to find such variety and innovation in the packs’ organizational structures. 

The Litany does not require any particular organization from packs, so long as sacred law is not violated. The only true requirements for a pack structure to be considered valid are that the members of a pack agree to its structure, and they find a pack totem that is willing to sponsor them. Conservative totem spirits reject anything they consider to be radical or beneath them, but liberal ones might accept—or even look for opportunities to sponsor—innovation. As a result of this freedom, there is a good deal of experimentation in pack structures, especially among Youngbloods trying to reinvent the wheel. This advantage allows the Garou to adapt to strange circumstances and keep the Wyrm off-balance. 

Players and Storytellers are encouraged to review these organizational structures and experiment with concepts that work specifically for their chronicle and story. 

Here are some of the most common Garou pack organizational structures: 

## Wyld Packs 

A Wyld pack is a traditional organizational style inspired by the lupus instincts of Garou. Members of these packs hope that a return to the roots of the pack structure provides an edge to werewolves in a time of industrialization, when the 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

Weaver dominates. Wyld packs run similar to native wolf packs, using an open family style with traditionally benevolent, but authoritative, parental figures leading the group. These packs are usually larger than average, often ranging from five to twelve members living, hunting, and patrolling together. 

The pack is led formally by a Pack Leader (typically the strongest Alpha), who is supported by a group of loyal Betas. These packs tend to be very hierarchical, using Alpha, Beta, and Omega roles. The pack’s actual structure is very ad hoc, depending on the will of the Pack Leader, who may assign tasks and duties as required for the good of the pack. 

Social dynamics focus strongly on position and the relative strength of those involved. Mobility is very fluid, depending upon an individual’s will and power at any given moment. This sort of pack freely uses Staredowns to resolve conflicts. (See **Chapter Nine: Social Systems, Challenges, Staredowns** , page 427.) This flexibility allows Wyld packs to adapt to almost any threat in the field, and yet also provides the stability needed to keep the chain of command intact during tense situations. Some wolf-inspired Wyld packs with more than ten members have two Pack Leaders, one male and one female Alpha, who help govern the pack. This dichotomy can cause unusual splits in authority, especially among non-Lupus Garou, but it works for some packs. 

Occasionally, a Pack Leader arises who captives her packmates and transforms her pack into an autocratic structure that overcomes the traditional needs of the pack. The instinct to follow a powerful leader is often too strong for these Garou to resist, and following a charismatic leader can seem the safest way to survive a war. 

## **Roles that Garou in Wyld packs often assume include:** 

## • **Pack Leader** 

A Pack Leader is the authoritative, parental figure in the pack who makes all of the major decisions for as long as she retains control. Her authority is absolute; she cannot be questioned without a traditional Staredown challenge or a challenge for her position. 

## • **Beta** 

The second-in-command of the pack is often the strongest of the Betas or a young Alpha who will one day seek to replace the Pack Leader. She serves as an aide-de-camp for the Pack Leader and as the manager of the troupe. She often helps supervise raising any Cubs or young who live near the pack and listens to her packmates when they feel they cannot approach the Pack Leader. If the Pack Leader is unavailable, the Beta then steps forward to lead the pack until she returns or another takes her place. 

## • **Lore Master** 

A Lore Master serves as both shaman and oracle, seeing to the spiritual and intellectual needs of her pack. She often helps her packmates commune with the spirits and heal any rifts they might feel with Gaia. Many packs tend to consider this role optional, only filled when the pack is large enough to warrant the further division of responsibility. This role is often assumed by the highest-Rank Theurge within the pack. 

## • **Historian** 

A Historian records the general history of the pack, noting its great deeds and bitter failures. The pack record is often read aloud at pack meetings and occasionally at moots, when a deed must be shared with others for a Garou to claim Renown or suffer scandal. Some packs use this position as a propaganda tool, bolstering its members’ Renown. Others use it as a way to maintain continuity when packmates die, ensuring that its members will live on forever. Such a role is often assumed by the highest-Rank Galliard within the pack. 

## • **Omega** 

An Omega is the lowest of the low within a pack. She accepts the scorn and abuse of her packmates, knowing she serves a vital role in the community. The Omega is the only one who can laugh at the Pack Leader with impunity. She has the right and ability to say anything to anyone with a reasonable certainty she will not be punished too badly. Who takes the Omega seriously? This useful position helps the pack confront unpleasant or uncomfortable truths, because the Omega can say seemingly outrageous things others won’t or feel they can’t. Sometimes the Emperor has no clothes, and only the Omega has the chutzpah to point it out; after all, what standing does she have to lose in the eyes of her packmates? 

## Collective Packs 

Some packs eschew traditional hierarchies altogether, delving deeply into the ancient roots of Garou culture and tradition. Collective packs take the auspice gestaltsoul theory and push its envelope, attempting to merge their roles within the pack into a single collective being. 

Collective packs believe one’s auspice is more than just the phase of the moon under which a Garou was born; it is a blessing directly from Luna that grants special insight and knowledge into all matters that fall under its bailiwick. In such a pack, leadership changes hands often, and the highest-ranked auspice most appropriate to the situation, referred to as the Auspice Prime, takes 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

the reins of the pack until the problem is resolved. For example, during a battle, the Auspice Prime of the pack’s Ahroun directs the pack in battle with her strategy, personal might, and inspiration. 

Devotion to the collective ideal requires a great amount of determination and dedication. Garou are expected to defer to more-experienced auspices and to trust in the insight of their packmates. Even when it feels wrong, placing faith in Luna’s blessing is expected and each pack member must trust that each of her packmates was chosen for a purpose. 

Tensions arise within Collective packs when the Auspice Primes disagree regarding which auspice should reign over an encounter. For example, the Auspice Prime of the Philodox might believe mediation is the best solution during a tense situation with another pack, while the Auspice Prime of the Ahroun might be certain that a direct assault is better. When there is a difference of opinion on which auspice has authority in a situation, the Council of Prime Auspices vote, and a simple majority decides the matter. This resolution works well, until one or more of the unrelated auspices abstains from a vote and forces a stalemate. 

Any Prime Auspice may be challenged by a member of her shared auspice via the method most traditional to their jurisdiction. (For more information, see **Chapter Nine: Social Systems: Challenges** , page 427.) 

## **Roles that Garou in Collective packs assume are as follows:** 

## • **Prime Auspice Ragabash** 

The Prime Auspice Ragabash rules over matters concerning stealth, cunning, and the questioning of traditional wisdom. She often challenges the wisdom of the other Prime Auspices within the pack, and she argues about who should claim dominion over matters that fall into the grey areas between jurisdictions. 

## • **Prime Auspice Theurge** 

The Prime Auspice Theurge rules over matters concerning spirits, the mysteries of the Umbra, and rites. She often conflicts with the Prime Auspice Galliard regarding matters of prophecy and lore. 

## • **Prime Auspice Philodox** 

The Prime Auspice Philodox rules over matters concerning law and mediation. She also rules the pack during a time of peace, but often clashes with the Prime Auspice Ahroun about the nature of peace and war, and which auspice should be ascendant at any given moment. 

## • **Prime Auspice Galliard** 

The Prime Auspice Galliard rules over matters concerning lore, the history of the Garou, and prophets and communicators. She often conflicts with the Prime Auspice Theurge regarding matters of prophecy and lore. 

## • **Prime Auspice Ahroun** 

The Prime Auspice Ahroun rules over matters concerning conflict, war, and violence. It is expected an Ahroun shall lead during war. Tension arises when the Philodox believes peace is still possible, but the Ahroun believes the pack must take up arms. 

## Democratic Packs 

At the end of World War II, a wave of positive sentiment spread regarding the power of democracy against the tyranny of fascism. Some Garou returning from the war were tired of the rigid chain of command present in military life and wanted to experiment with how they organized their tribes. Modern spirits based on American folk tales, such as O’Mighty Dolla, The American Dream, and John “Iron” Henry, welcomed this new style and freely offered their patronage to packs seeking to embrace their ideals. Democratic packs have become popular among Youngbloods in recent years, especially among the lessconservative tribes, such as the Bone Gnawers, the Fianna, and the Glass Walkers. 

Some packs follow the American model of representative democracy, in which any Alpha or Beta can vote on important issues for the pack. Others strictly follow Robert’s Rules of Order on Parliamentary Procedure to ensure matters are resolved quickly and effectively. Major issues are decided by the voting citizen members of the pack, but the privilege of voting may be restricted to the acknowledged Alphas and Betas. Democratic packs often mix and match from different styles of democracy, such as parliamentary procedure, Jeffersonian republicanism, or direct democracy. American packs tend to favor mimicking the politics of the United States via representative democracy. 

The problem many Democratic packs face is that it is easy to preach about the will of the people when the people agree with you, but it can be difficult for an Alpha to cede her authority to the opinions of those who she might regard as her lessers. Occasionally, disputes can get out of hand, and when the mob turns, things can get ugly quickly. Issues are often decided with a simple majority vote. Eligible voters discover that having a voice in government requires a certain amount of discipline, lest they allow their emotions to turn them into a mob. 

Chapter eleven: the Garou nation 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## **Roles that Garou in Democratic packs assume are as follows:** 

## • **President** 

The President is an elected leader who possesses all of the authority of a Pack Leader and who handles the day-to-day business of the pack. The difference between a President and a traditional Pack Leader is that any eligible voter in the pack—typically Alphas and Betas—can protest the President’s decision by calling for a motion against it during a time of peace. Likewise, the President serves as leader only by the will of the voters and can be removed at any time without a traditional challenge. The elected President must be gregarious—keeping her pack happy and willing to support her plans—without the traditional means to enforce her will. 

## • **Secretary** 

The Secretary serves as an aide-de-camp and secondin-command for the President. She acts as leader when the President is unavailable. Should the President become incapacitated for any reason, it is her function to call for a new election and monitor it. Traditionally, a standing Secretary cannot run for President during an election, unless there is an emergency. Otherwise, she serves as the pack’s historian, keeping note of all of the important events, votes, and records of previous office holders. Her most important function is to determine which pack citizens are eligible to vote. A Beta temporarily demoted to Omega status due to a mistake may find she no longer can vote until she recovers her reputation. Of course, this decision may alter the outcome of any pending votes. 

## • **Master-at-Arms** 

The Master-at-Arms is the military leader of the pack. She leads it into battle and enforces its will. Should the President refuse to step down when voted out of office, the Master-at-Arms’ duty is to remove the President from office, with violence if necessary. By tradition, a Master-at-Arms cannot run for the office of President, but there are precedents for coups d’état against Presidents during a time of war if the pack is in danger. This sort of maneuver is risky, since the pack’s totem spirit will judge the righteousness of the act and decide whether or not the pack is still following the ideals of democracy. Often, if a Democratic pack falls from its ideals, it becomes an Authoritarian pack. 

## Authoritarian Packs 

Strength comes from unity of purpose and will. Authoritarian packs believe that the corruption of the Wyrm has spread 

because the Garou Nation is weakened by petty internal squabbles and inept leadership. It is written in the Litany that a leader may not be challenged during a time of war. The time of peace has ended on Gaia; the time of the Apocalypse is now, and only the strength granted by the leadership of a benevolent tyrant can shepherd the Garou through its storm. 

Authoritarian packs believe that if the world is to have any hope of surviving, a few must surrender false freedom to seize victory. War is eternal; only those with the discipline to endure the battle will survive to its end. Authoritarians believe that the ends always justify the means. Packmates pledge their utter loyalty and devotion to their leader and enforce her will to complete their pack’s mission. 

A number of septs have banned Authoritarian packs, believing they violate the tenet of the Litany that states _The Leader May Be Challenged at Any Time During Peace_ , but the Authoritarians argue that the time for peace has faded, and that the Garou Nation is now in an eternal war. Rumors of Authoritarians violating the Litany to achieve their goals have circulated widely, but Galliards have yet to sing specific details of any scandal. Some suspect these packs of being one step too close to the Wyrm or of being cults of personality rather than true packs. These Garou watch Authoritarian packs’ activities very closely. The reality is that few leaders will risk their position or their pack by flagrantly violating the law of the land. The danger must always be worth the prize, and thus these packs must make a public show of supporting the Litany. 

Totems who accept Authoritarian packs, especially more conservative tribal totems, feed on their loyal fervor and encourage this style of organization. Authoritarian packs led by a Philodox tend to be more political in nature, leading to a dictator-style leadership, seeking to impose its will upon others. Ahroun leaders tend to encourage a military hierarchy, preparing for a never-ending battle for glory and honor. 

## **Roles that Garou in Authoritarian packs assume** 

## **are as follows:** 

## • **The Leader** 

The Leader is the all-powerful tyrant of the pack. Her word is theoretically law and cannot be challenged at any time for as long as she lives. The hard truth is that more than one Authoritarian pack Leader has died in a bloody coup d’état when there were no witnesses present. A wise and charismatic leader appears to listen to her followers. She needs the majority of the pack to continue to love her, risking it all on the belief that she alone can help them survive the end of the world. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

- **Minister of Harmony**
