## Abominations 

_Abominations_ , known as the “Pale Ones” or “Undying Creatures,” are werewolves that have been Embraced by a vampire. Provided they survive this traumatic experience, they awaken with their Garou abilities intact along with additional abilities granted to them by their vampiric vitae. Abominations who survive their creation almost invariably fall to the Wyrm. The self-loathing and hatred within their hearts due to their very nature easily opens the door for the corruption they had previously fought so hard against. 

Even Black Spiral Dancers consider becoming an abomination a fate far worse than any death in service to their master. While they may seek to make any use of them, Abominations are never trusted or welcomed as one of the Wyrm’s own. Nor are they accepted by other vampires, who see only their former lives as werewolves, rightly fearing what such a twisted combination does to the mind of any who survive long enough to endure it. Cursed by the Wyrm, these creatures live a half-life, doomed to stalk the earth as 

## **Sample Vampire NPC** 

Ophelia Giovanni 

Giovanni Independent Alliance Vampire **Stock NPC:** Rating 5 

**Specializations:** Empathy, Gifts: Silent Strider, Gifts: Theurge, Leadership, Occult **Health Levels:** OOOOO 

**Blood:** OOOOO OOOOO **Rage:** OOOOO 

**Special:** Ophelia has a test pool of 10 in most challenges. In challenges involving Empathy, Leadership, or Occult, her test pool is 15. In addition, Ophelia has access to all of the Theurge- and Silent Strider-affinity gifts. 

Ghosts are Ophelia’s specialty, and she’s very good at what she does. Like many of the Giovanni, she is ruthless and dedicated to her work—gathering souls to tithe to Venice. She has had more than one run-in with Silent Striders who look to put the restless dead to sleep. She is a member of the Independent Alliance, and she holds no loyalty to the Camarilla or the Sabbat. 

vampires, hunted both by those who they once called family and by many members of the ilk that made them. 

Most Garou do not survive the Embrace. The very lucky ones die immediately, but others linger for hours or even days as their connection to Gaia seeks to reject the Wyrmand Weaver-tainted blood and allow a merciful death. Those werewolves who survive the Embrace are dubbed Abominations, and rightly so—what little of their psyches remain are as likely to be as dead as the shells that now house them. Even Pentex is known to eschew such creations as unstable and counter-productive. 

Although it is rare, some werewolves who fear death seek out a vampire and beg to be Embraced, even knowing the possibility exists that they will die in the attempt and the certainty that they will be hunted for the remainder of their existence. More often, Abominations are created when power-hungry vampires capture a Garou and attempt to Embrace her in the hope of gaining a terrifying ally or thrall. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

**
## **Abomination Player-characters** 

Abominations make excellent antagonists for **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Werewolf The Apocalypse** stories, but Storytellers should take great care before they allow them as player-characters. The sheer power of this character type, as well as the unpredictable reaction to it by other Garou, leads to far more antagonism in a game than is usually healthy. For detailed information and crossover rules for Abomination player-characters run with **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Vampire The Masquerade** , please see **Chapter Ten: Storytelling, Crossover Settings: Antagonist Factions, Vampire Crossover Setting** , page 467. 

**
Those Abominations who survive are incredibly powerful, and many of them quickly fall into insanity, degenerating into bestial creatures whose rage and need for blood threatens the Veil at every turn. The rare few who retain their sanity become dangerous hunters who often attack vampires and Garou alike. The Age of Apocalypse has seen a rise in the prevelance of these creatures, with more of them surviving the Embrace. There is no clear answer regarding what has changed to make Abominations more likely to survive, but some Theurges believe it is just another indication that the Apocalypse draws ever closer. 

## Organization 

It is rare for an Abomination to survive the Embrace, let alone thrive afterwards. Often, these creatures are killed by either vampires or werewolves immediately after they have turned. When they do survive, they stick to the outskirts of humanity, living a life of solitude. Some Abominations feel that they must atone for their crimes and seek out others of their kind to kill in hopes of regaining Gaia’s favor. Aside from these bloody encounters, Abominations avoid one another as readily as they avoid all other supernatural creatures. 

## Appearance 

Abominations retain much of their appearance from before their Embrace; however, they also take on aspects from their new vampiric nature. Storytellers should refer to **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Vampire The Masquerade** for additional information about the vampire clans and how they manifest. 

## Weakness 

While Abominations retain both their werewolf and vampiric abilities, the drawbacks are massive. Once the Embrace is complete, an Abomination loses all of her connection to Gaia and to her former tribe. She can still access the gifts she had previously learned, including tribal gifts, but any new ones must be taught to her by a bane spirit. Additionally, an Abomination is fully blood bound to her sire, and she has all of the weaknesses of both vampires and Garou. She retains her tribal weakness and gains the clan weakness of her vampiric sire. 

When a Garou is Embraced, she undergoes an excruciating change that often lingers for days or even weeks. The spiritual aspect of her personality fights the vampiric blood attempting to invade her body. Garou who are particularly powerful often find the experience blessedly brief, if extraordinarily painful. 

The longer a Garou lingers on the cusp of life and death, the more likely she is to spiral into madness, as the vampiric blood takes over her body. 

Chapter Fourteen: allies and a ntagonists 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## Creating an Abomination NPC 

To create an Abomination, use the same rules for creating standard werewolf player-characters or Stock NPCs, with the following exceptions: 

- Abominations are a blasphemy against the will of Gaia, being both dead and alive as vampires and werewolves. They are Wyrm affiliated-creatures, corrupted and forever marked with Wyrm affinity. 

- Abominations are considered neonate vampires, with all of the associated penalties. They have a resource called _Blood_ , with Blood pool of 10, and they can spend 1 point of Blood per turn, as a Garou spends Gnosis. Abominations can spend a point of Blood to heal a point of normal damage. They take aggravated damage from flaming weapons, incendiary ammunition, and from silver. When exposed to sunlight, Abominations take between 1 and 5 points of aggravated damage per turn, at the Storyteller’s discretion. 

- If Storytellers are using the **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Vampire The Masquerade** rules, at character creation, Abominations gain a free dot of one discipline, and they may purchase up to 3 additional dots of up to three separate vampire disciplines at inclan costs. This selection results in a total of 4 dots between one or more disciplines. These disciplines must be selected from the following list: Animalism, Auspex, Celerity, Dominate, Fortitude, Obfuscate, Presence, or Potence. Abominations do not have access to techniques or elder powers. For Stock NPCs, this effect may be simulated by granting the Abomination a single bonus tribal or Fera gift affinity. For more information, see **Chapter Ten: Storytelling, Crossover Settings: Antagonist Factions, Vampire Crossover Setting** , page 467. 

- Abominations gain and lose Rage normally, succumbing to anger frenzy as normal Garou do. Stock NPCs may purchase the Rage specialization. 

- Abominations can only purchase gifts from their auspice affinity. Stock NPCs can only specialize in a single auspice gift affinity, but are otherwise restricted from obtaining additional affinity specializations. In addition, they cannot purchase or specialize in any tribal or breed gift affinities, unless they using this choice to simulate a vampire discipline. 

- As pariahs, Abominations cannot possess any sect or faction-specific merits or flaws detailed in **Mind’s Eye Theatre: Vampire The Masquerade** . An Abomination cannot possess any merits or flaws that reflect social standing, such as the werewolf merits Heroic Lineage, Legendary Lineage, or Tribal Affinity; or the vampire merit Reputation. Abominations can possess tribal and clan merits, with the exception of the Caitiff merit Auspicious; fate does not look kindly upon such horrors. Abominations cannot take the Thin Blooded flaw. 

- Abominations are able to shapeshift like typical werewolves. They suffer all of the effects of the Curse, Seethe, and Delirium, and enter play with a vampiric Humanity rating of 2. Having already succumbed to the Wyrm, they do not suffer the effects of Harano. 

- If a Storyteller chooses to create a complete character sheet for an Abomination instead of using Stock NPC rules, these Abomination characters gain beast traits and are susceptible to vampiric fear, hunger, and anger frenzies. They can also gain and lose Humanity. 

- An Abomination may enter and leave the Umbra by spending one turn focusing within a Wyrm-tainted area. Crossing the Gauntlet in a Wyld or Weaver affinity area requires three turns of focus. 

- When an Abomination succeeds in damaging a Gaian Garou or Fera within melee range, the target becomes tainted by the Wyrm. If the taint is not cleansed, it begins to corrupt the Garou. For more information, see **Chapter Eight: Dramatic Systems, Wyrm Taint** , page 384. 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## Sample Abomination Stock NPCs 

## **Sample Abomination NPC** 

Akar-Neferu/Antef Wind-Runner 

Silent Strider/Follower of Set Abomination **Stock NPC:** Rating 5 

**Specializations:** Brawl, Dodge, Gifts: Ahroun, Gifts: Silent Strider, Rage **Health Levels:** OOOOO 

**Blood:** OOOOO OOOOO **Gnosis:** OOOOO OOOOO 

**Rage:** OOOOO **Special:** Antef has a test pool of 10 in most challenges. In challenges involving Brawl or Dodge, his test pool is 15. In addition, Antef has access to all levels of the Ahroun- and Silent Strider-affinity gifts. 

Born to English Romanichal kinfolk who immigrated to the U.S. during WWII, Antef joined a nomadic biker gang, the Lowroad Growlers, and headed for Route 66, to explore Mexico. The gang came across a Temple of Set in Death Valley and decided to investigate the vampire nest during the day. Only Antef survived the massacre that ensued. He was tortured for nearly a decade and then taught Set’s truth by Typhonic priests. To complete his corruption, the priest sired Antef, who adopted the Setite name AkarNeferu, meaning The Lovely Killer. 

## **Sample Abomination NPC** 

## Karin Lester 

Silver Fang/Ventrue Abomination **Stock NPC:** Rating 4 

**Specializations:** Gifts: Silver Fang, Intimidation, Leadership, Rage 

**Health Levels:** OOOO 

**Blood:** OOOOO OOOOO **Gnosis:** OOOOO OOOOO **Rage:** OOOOO 

**Special:** Karin has a test pool of 8 in most challenges. In challenges involving Intimidation or Leadership, her test pool is 13. In addition, Karin has access to the first four levels of the Silver Fang-affinity gifts. 

Karin was Embraced by her father, who was distraught at the thought of his child dying while he never aged. She survived the attempt and has thrived despite her new lot in life. She maintains a hatred for vampires, and she understands that any werewolf who encounters her will shoot first and ask questions later. 

Akar-Neferu now believes that Gaia must already be dead and that her shell must now be destroyed to ease her passing into Duat. The Garou are only prolonging her death throes, torturing her spirit. This Abomination has a secret haven in Las Vegas and makes yearly excursions to disrupt the septs and sow discord. 

Chapter Fourteen: allies and a ntagonists 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

## The Fae 

Perhaps the Garou’s nearest ally among the other supernatural denizens of the world are the _fae_ , magical creatures who live their lives caught between two worlds. 

These entities have the ability to harness immense magical powers fueled by emotions and dreams. This magic can sometimes inspire madness in the people—supernatural and mundane—they encounter, when a victim finds herself unable to discern reality from the _Dream_ harnessed by the fae. 

In the Age of Apocalypse, there exists tension between the Fianna and the fae, altering the close relationship the tribe has always fostered with its cousins. The spurned fae princess has forbidden interaction between the fae and the Garou. Though many have different theories as to the reason for her orders, none know for sure what has happened. In protected places, some fae insinuate that her outrage is merely a cover, and that she is fostering a pretense to conceal the identity of another who has been wronged by the Garou. Some fae still maintain old alliances with Garou in secret places, away from the prying eyes of their monarch. These relationships are often brief and filled with the intense emotions for which the fae are so well known. Despite this passion, there are rare instances of lasting friendships that have been maintained between the two groups. 

Even as the Garou have found their society shifting, so too have the fae changed, and not necessarily for the better. Those who knew of them before the Age of Apocalypse have noted a darkness that has fallen over the beautiful and mythic creatures. Their magic is most indicative of this change; gone is much of these creatures’ playful joy, replaced by a cunning found in fairy tales of old. Those among the fae who know the truth of their fate are not discussing it. What is known, however, is that once the fae had among them a number of very young ones—their childlings—who seem to have all but disappeared. 

Fae spend much of their time masked as mortals, hiding among the common folk of the world, attempting to blend in and live their lives. When one does choose to reveal her true form, she is an unearthly creature of power, myth, and glorious presence. Some are regal and exquisitely beautiful, while others are hideous and gruesome monsters. No matter what their appearance, these creatures are striking and unforgettable in their true forms. 

Even among the Fianna, little is known about the intricacies of their customs and society. While some Fianna can boast of close relationships with fae, even going so far as to being invited into their freeholds to participate in their revels, 

more is still unknown to the Garou than that which is understood by them. Some werewolves recognize that the fae appear to function as a pseudo-feudal society, with their rulers taking on titles such as Count, Duchess, or Queen. Fae activity seems to increase during certain times of the year, particularly equinoxes and solstices, and primitive festivals call them from hiding. 

Faeries encountered in a werewolf story can be compelling allies or terrifying antagonists. While the fae understand and empathize with the duties of the Garou, between the short, violent lives of werewolves and the edicts of their princess, fae are often reluctant to help their werewolf allies very much. When a fae does make the decision to assist a Garou, she does so with the knowledge that she risks her very existence in the process. Fae tend to look at their Garou counterparts with equal parts fascination and sorrow, and they can relate to the loss that every werewolf feels. Garou find the fae to be beautiful and mysterious kindred spirits. 

## Creating a Fae NPC 

To create a fae, use the same rules for creating standard werewolf player-characters or for creating Stock NPCs, with the following exceptions: 

- Fae utilize a resource called _Glamour_ . Glamour is used to fuel fae magic, similar to the way that werewolves use Gnosis for their effects. All fae have a Glamour pool of 10. They can spend up to 1 point of Glamour per turn, and they automatically regenerate 1 point of Glamour every 10 minutes. Fae regain Glamour by experiencing the Dream, or by inspiring humans to acts of creativity, such as art or music. 

- Fae take aggravated damage from pure iron weapons. Alloys that include iron, such as steel, do not inflict aggravated damage on fae. 

- A fae can spend Glamour to heal herself or any friendly character within one step of her location, healing 1 point of damage, normal or aggravated, for each point of Glamour expended for that purpose. 

- Fae are always considered to have a Rage level equal to their Stock NPC rating. This Rage level doesn’t go up or down, and they suffer none of the ill effects of having high Rage. 

- A fae may choose to select the Gifts specialization (see **Stock Non-Player Character Generation, NPC Specializations, Gift Specializations** , page 612). 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115)

Theo is ruthless, a cold-stone killer who cares little for anyone. He’s a Redcap who will eat anything, but he has a special taste for the flesh of young women. Despite this predilection, he has his own sense of honor and has dedicated his existence to opposing werewolves at every turn, due to their actions spurning his princess. 

## Mages 

_Mages_ are humans who possess the talent and insight necessary to harness true magic by shaping the very strands of reality. Their magic tempts the dangers of _paradox:_ the fundamental resistance of the universe fighting against them, as they alter the natural world to suit their whims. There is a deep-seated mistrust between Garou and mages, whom werewolves often refer to as “Namebreakers,” although the two groups have come together in the past to destroy common enemies. 

The Garou know little about mages, though they understand that there are different factions found among them. Some of these will-workers are heavily connected to the Wyld and are slightly unhinged or unpredictable. Others are heavily connected to the Weaver, adhering strictly to the laws of paradox. Still others are connected to the Wyrm, and these creatures embrace the insanity of their corruption. Very unbalanced mages may even seek out places of power and drain them completely, an act that is even more harmful in the desperate times of the Age of Apocalypse. Werewolves 

and mages draw their power from the same source, and with the loss of so many caerns, conflict arises over the few remaining avenues of power. The birth of shard caerns has attracted the renewed interest of mages who are interested in finding ways to claim their own share of the newlygrowing power. 

Mages can be incredibly powerful, performing awe-inspiring feats that bend reality. However, most prefer subtle effects that do not draw down the ire of paradox, using coincidence and disbelief to cover their tracks. Very few mages resort to fireballs or powerful, obvious alterations of reality. 

Some Garou believe that paradox is a punishment from Gaia, levied on the mages who believe they can change the natural order of the world. Small pockets of Ragabash have even attempted to harness the power of paradox spirits in hopes of using the wild power for their own needs. This experimentation has proven to be largely fruitless, as very few werewolves are able to use these spirits in any meaningful way. 

There are those who believe that the reason werewolves and mages get along so poorly is their common downfall—pride. Mages are proud in their belief that they can change reality to suit their whims, and Garou are proud in their belief that they can win an impossible war. Despite this fundamental conflict, werewolves have worked with mages on occasion, particularly when the sacred, powerful spaces that both honor are threatened. Although they are able to harness a great deal of power, mages are still simply human. They are undetectable by Garou and often go about their daily lives as any other human would. 

Mages within a **Werewolf: The Apocalypse** game can make interesting and challenging antagonists or powerful allies. Alliances are rare, but may be fostered when a major threat occurs. More likely, these groups will conflict, be it over places of power in the world or over where the boundaries of nature should reside. 

## Creating a Mage NPC 

To create a mage, use the same rules for creating standard werewolf player-characters or Stock NPCs, with the following exceptions: 

- Mages are considered to be supernatural creatures, not humans. Powers that have an increased effect on humans do not enact those increased effects when used against a mage. 

- Mage NPCs utilize a resource called _Quintessence_ . Quintessence is used to fuel gifts in the same way werewolves use Gnosis. All mages have a Quintessence 

Mind’s EyE ThEaTrE: WErEWolf ThE apocalypsE 

Alona McCreary (Order #15311115) 

pool of 10. They can spend up to 3 points of Quintessence each turn, and they automatically regenerate 1 point of Quintessence every 10 minutes. 

- Mages can concentrate for one turn, taking no other action, and spend a point of Quintessence to heal 1 point of normal or aggravated damage. 

- Mages may choose the  Gifts specialization (see **Stock Non-Player Character Generation, NPC Specializations, Gift Specializations** , page 612). 

- Mages possess the ability to cross the Gauntlet by expending a point of Quintessence, using the chart from **Chapter Thirteen: The Umbra: The Gauntlet** , page 585. 

- Whenever a Mage uses a gift, she is at risk of incurring paradox. She must make a static Mental attribute + Willpower challenge versus their Stock NPC rating + level of gift used. Failure on this test causes them to incur 1 point of normal damage. If the gift used is of a higher level than the character’s Stock NPC level, she takes 1 point of aggravated damage. The Storyteller may choose to waive this test or give the character a wild card bonus when gifts are used in a manner that doesn’t seem to bend reality. For example, using Airt Perception to listen to spirits would not incur a paradox challenge, while using Master of Fire to shoot fire at an enemy would.

## Wraiths 

_Wraiths_ are the souls of the dead who refuse to rejoin the cycle. They are not ancestor spirits: those willing souls that remain within the Umbra to guide and nurture future generations. Instead, wraiths refuse to release the tenuous grasp they retain on the Material Realm. Wraiths can be a boon or a burden to the Garou, and Silent Striders have a passion for understanding and dealing with both good and troublesome souls who become ghosts. Wraiths can be sources of valuable information to their friends or allies. However, they can also be continuous sources of frustration and danger to their enemies, or to those who obstruct them in their search for even a taste of the life they have lost. 

Wraiths generally remain in the _Shadowlands:_ grey wastelands that contain reflections of the living world. The laws of the underworld are psychological; echoes and manifestations of the real world hold substance only if they have emotional meaning. Within this bleak landscape, wraiths struggle to remember their purposes and fulfill them, always seeking to find peace and transcend beyond death into the unknown. 

Wraiths are intangible beings, creatures who are rarely encountered by most werewolves. Only the Silent Strider tribe retains any significant relationship with them. These Garou understand ghosts in a way that few others do, and they dedicate much of their existence to assisting uneasy wraiths to move on in the afterlife. The relationship between the restless dead and Silent Striders can be a dangerous one. Inside each wraith exists a darkness, a _shadow_ within her that is akin to the rage that can consume a Garou. When the shadow takes over, the wraith becomes a slave to the passions that drive her inner psyche. — If she cannot resist her shadow, she risks becoming a _spectre_ little more than a cruel, mindless spirit bent on destruction. 

Wraiths can be an interesting addition to any **Werewolf: The Apocalypse** game, although their implementation is limited, due to the fact that Silent Striders are the only characters who can reliably interact with them. However, these creatures can be compelling NPCs: ghosts who are looking for a way to cross over or spectres who are looking for revenge against one who harmed them in life. 

## Creating a Wraith NPC 

To create a wraith, use the same rules for creating standard werewolf player-characters or Stock NPCs, with the following exceptions: 

- Wraiths are considered to be supernatural creatures, not humans. Powers that have an increased effect on humans do not enact those increased effects when used against a wraith. 

- Wraith NPCs utilize a resource called _Pathos_ . Pathos is used to fuel gifts and to heal, in the same way that werewolves use Gnosis. All wraiths have a Pathos pool of 10. They can spend up to 3 points of Pathos each turn, and they automatically regenerate 1 point of Pathos every 10 minutes. 

- Wraiths typically exist in the Shadowlands, where they may view—but not interact with—the real world. Wraiths cannot attack or use powers on individuals in the real world unless the wraith manifests in it. 

- Due to their intangibility, wraiths may have great difficulty traveling, as they cannot interact with physical vehicles. There are echoes of old conveyances within the Shadowlands, but these are neither reliable nor free to utilize. 

- A wraith can spend Pathos to manifest in the physical world for one hour. While manifested, the wraith can attack or use powers on individuals in the physical world as if the wraith was a normal creature of the Material Realm. A wraith has a number of health levels equal to the amount of Pathos spent initially for this purpose, to a maximum of 5 health levels. If a wraith runs out of health while manifesting, she is forcibly returned to the Shadowlands. A wraith cannot spend Pathos after manifesting in order to gain more health levels, though she may use Pathos to heal, as described above. 

- A Wraith may choose the Gifts specialization (see **Stock Non-Player Character Generation, NPC Specializations, Gift Specializations** , page 612). 

**
## **Shapeshifter Souls** 

When a shapeshifter dies, her spirit returns to the cycle of death and rebirth, awaiting its next incarnation. Some Garou choose to avoid this fate, becoming ancestor spirits and guiding their descendants and other shapeshifters from the different Tribal Homelands or other umbral realms. Due to the twin nature of spirit and material that makes up all shapeshifters, they do not become wraiths.
